experiments.title: Does Matching Landing Page Imagery to Meta Ad Creative Increase Ebook Signups and Donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: If the landing page includes imagery that matches the creative used in Meta ads, the resulting visual continuity may reinforce message relevance and reduce cognitive friction for visitors arriving from those ads. This alignment could increase the likelihood that visitors complete the ebook signup and potentially make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2026 - 02/28/2026
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy research organization dedicated to advancing scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom. The goal of this test was to evaluate whether including an image on the ebook landing page that matched the creative used in Meta ads would increase email acquisitions and donations. All other elements of the landing page remained unchanged. As with the previous test, traffic to the page came from multiple channels, including Meta, Performance Max, email, and email prospecting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Control (No Matching Image)
Visitors: 20,209Signups: 2,367Signup Rate: 11.71%Gifts: 118Revenue: $5,977.40Response Rate: 0.58%RPM: $295.80
Treatment (Matching Image Added)
Visitors: 20,319Signups: 1,481Signup Rate: 7.29%Gifts: 58Revenue: $2,708.00Response Rate: 0.29%RPM: $133.30
What We Observed
The control significantly outperformed the treatment across all key metrics.
Compared to the treatment, the control generated:
+886 signups+60 gifts+$3,269.40 in revenue
The treatment saw substantial declines in:
Signup rate (↓ ~38%)Response rate (↓ ~50%)Revenue per thousand visitors (↓ ~55%)
Why We Think This Happened
The added image likely disrupted the landing page’s visual clarity and diverted attention away from the primary conversion action.
Because traffic came from multiple channels, many visitors likely did not have the context of the Meta ad the image was intended to match. Without that context, the image functioned more as a distraction than a reinforcement.
In contrast, the control page maintained:
A simpler, cleaner layoutStronger visual hierarchyClear focus on the primary action
Next Steps
Test minimal landing page designs vs. image-heavy versions to confirm whether simpler layouts consistently drive stronger conversions.Evaluate placing images below the signup form so they support the offer without distracting from the primary action.Develop channel-specific landing pages so Meta visitors see matched creative while other channels see a simplified version optimized for conversion.

experiments.title: How Meta Campaign Optimization Goals Affect Donor Acquisition Costs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing Meta ad campaigns for donations rather than email sign-ups for prospective supporters reached through Meta Ad campaigns will achieve more cost-effective donor acquisition because signals of high intent help ad platforms prioritize users who are more psychologically ready to give, not just willing to exchange an email address.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 01/04/2026
Global Christian Relief (GCR) has a strategic priority to grow its email subscriber base as a pathway to long-term donor growth. While acquiring email addresses at a low cost is important, the team recognizes a core tension: not all subscribers are equally likely to become donors. This experiment is designed to explore how acquisition strategy influences downstream donor quality, not just volume.
Specifically, this test examined whether Meta’s ad optimization goal affects the cost-effectiveness of acquiring future donors through a content offer funnel. The central question is whether optimizing campaigns for email sign-ups (names) or for donations (purchases) ultimately leads to lower donor acquisition costs.
The goal of this experiment is to determine whether optimizing earlier in the funnel for volume (email sign-ups) or later in the funnel for intent (donations) results in more cost-effective donor acquisition over time. The findings will inform how GCR balances scale and quality in its paid acquisition strategy and how it approaches list growth as a driver of sustainable fundraising.
To test this, GCR promoted a single content offer through Meta ad properties, including Facebook, Instagram, and Threads. Ads were duplicated across two campaigns that were identical in creative, audience, and spend, with the only variable being the Meta Pixel optimization goal. One campaign was optimized for email sign-ups (email optimization, serving as the control), while the other was optimized for donations (purchase optimization, serving as the treatment).
The audience for both campaigns was built using a combination of existing GCR donors and a lookalike audience derived from that donor data, ensuring the ads reach people with a higher likelihood of alignment and potential generosity. Ad spend was split evenly between the two campaigns to allow for a fair comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Optimizing Meta campaigns for donations rather than email sign-ups led to significantly stronger donor outcomes. While the email-optimized campaign generated more overall traffic, the purchase-optimized campaign produced more than four times the donation conversion rate (0.47% vs. 0.03%) and drove 9 of the 11 total donations, despite receiving fewer impressions and a smaller sample size.
The relative difference between the two approaches was substantial: the purchase-optimized campaign outperformed the email-optimized campaign by 1,480%, with a 99.4% level of statistical confidence, exceeding the threshold required for a valid result. This indicates that the observed performance difference is highly unlikely to be due to chance.
We also measured the Gross Cost Per Donor (GCPD) as a key metric in this test. The email optimized campaign yielded a GCPD increase of 347%—further proof that optimizing for donations in Meta Ads campaigns is a much more efficient use of ad spend than optimizing for more top-of-funnel conversions.
These results suggest that Meta’s optimization signals meaningfully influence who is delivered into the funnel, not just how many people enter it. When the platform is instructed to optimize for donations, it appears to prioritize users who are psychologically closer to making a financial commitment, even when the initial interaction is a content offer rather than a direct donation ask. In contrast, optimizing for email sign-ups likely attracts lower-intent users who are willing to exchange an email address but are less prepared to give.
Importantly, this test highlights a key tradeoff in list growth strategy. While optimizing for email acquisition may reduce cost per subscriber and increase list volume, it can significantly dilute downstream donor quality. For Global Christian Relief, where the ultimate goal is list growth, optimizing for higher-intent actions earlier in the funnel is more effective.
The implication for future campaigns is clear: when donor acquisition is the primary objective, optimizing paid media for donations—even within an indirect, content-driven funnel—can yield fewer but substantially more valuable supporters. This finding supports a shift away from volume-first acquisition metrics and toward optimization strategies that reflect true donor intent and long-term fundraising value.
Open questions remain around scale and efficiency: how this approach performs at higher spend levels, whether similar patterns hold across different offers or audiences, and how email engagement and lifetime value differ between cohorts acquired under each optimization goal. These areas represent strong candidates for follow-up testing.

experiments.title: Will Testing Different CTAs in Facebook Ad Copy to Increase Donor Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a direct ask like “Click to Give Bibles” for Facebook audiences will achieve higher donor conversion than a softer “Click to Learn More” call-to-action because because clearer donation language reduces ambiguity and increases motivation to act on the giving opportunity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2026 - 01/30/2026
Bible League Canada was preparing to launch a new set of Facebook ads focused on a clear value proposition: asking users to donate to place Bibles with waiting believers around the world. In previous campaigns, some ad variations ended with “Click to Learn More >>”, while others used a more direct ask of “Click to Give Bibles >>”.
To better understand which type of call-to-action language drives higher donor conversions, the team set up a simple A/B test. The only element changed between versions was the final line of the primary ad copy:
Control: “Click to Learn More >>”
Treatment: “Click to Give Bibles >>”
Everything else — the ad creative, targeting, and budget — remained the same. The ads were shown to a broad Facebook audience, including existing donors, subscribers, lookalike audiences, and open targeting.
The big question we wanted to answer was: Does a direct ask improve donor response by providing clarity and urgency, or does a softer, curiosity-based CTA encourage more engagement that leads to giving?
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 23 days, and found that the direct, action-oriented call-to-action language significantly outperformed the softer alternative in driving donations through Facebook. Ads that ended with “Click to Give Bibles” led to 117.6% more donations than those ending with “Click to Learn More,” with a statistically significant 98.6% level of confidence. This is a major win for Bible League Canada and strongly supports using clearer giving language in our future ads.

When we dug further into the data, we found that this stronger performance was driven by both increased engagement and improved conversion:
The direct CTA generated 5.5% more ad clicks compared to the control, trending toward statistical validity at 92.7% confidence.
 	Those who clicked the Treatment were also twice as likely to convert into donors, with a 1.75% instant conversion rate for the Treatment compared to 0.88% for the Control.
the Treatment also showed a higher average gift
$52.99 CAD
Gross Cost Per Donor dropped from $170 (Control) to $87 (Treatment)
 	ROI more than doubled, from 0.25 (Control) to 0.61 (Treatment)

experiments.title: How Facebook Messenger vs. Web-Based Petition Flows Impact Donor Conversion from a Content Offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that reducing friction and delivering the petition flow directly in Facebook Messenger would result in more petition signers and a higher donor conversion rate, because users would be more likely to engage and act when they stay within a native platform environment with minimal disruption.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/28/2025 - 09/24/2025
Human Coalition increased donor acquisition using petitions on social platforms. This test aimed to increase petition signers and their conversion into donors by reducing friction and keeping users on Facebook.
The test targeted audiences built from lookalike models of Human Coalition’s donors and subscribers. The control was a traditional funnel: a Facebook ad drove users to a landing page with petition language and an email capture form. After signing, users were redirected to an instant donation page.
The treatment utilized a social media automation tool called ManyChat to manage the entire experience within Facebook Messenger. Users who engaged with the ad were entered into an automated workflow triggered by ManyChat, which messaged them through Messenger. This workflow provided the user with a variety of options to present the value proposition they would typically read on a landing page. Once they were ready, the user read the petition, submitted their name and email, and were then asked to donate via a link sent to them in their DM inbox.
The test was run as a Facebook A/B campaign with identical audience targeting to prevent overlap and ensure a clean comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test revealed a key trade-off between cost efficiency and donor intent:
The Messenger-based treatment drove more traffic, more signers, and had a dramatically lower CPM ($27.20 vs. $65.01). It achieved:
 	2x the reach
 	4x the clicks
 	49% more signers
 	However, donor conversion was significantly lower in the treatment group:
 	The control converted 4.1% of petition signers into donors (46 donors from 1117 signers)
 	The treatment converted only 0.48% (8 donors from 1662 signers)
 	Control revenue was $1,498; treatment revenue was just $170
keeping users on-platform via Messenger reduced friction for petition signing, but may have also lowered perceived commitment or donor motivation
Messenger's rapid, transactional format may have attracted lower-intent users
For reach and petition acquisition
For donor conversion
Messenger for acquisition, followed by a timed email donation ask
 	Testing richer storytelling or urgency-building in the Messenger flow
 	Introducing a follow-up web-based donation journey post-petition

experiments.title: Testing Form Friction: Will Requiring a Phone Number on a Content Offer Landing Page Hurt Sign-Ups from Facebook Traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that requiring a mobile phone number on the sign-up form for a Facebook video series acquisition offer will result in a small drop in conversions but more valuable contacts because motivated users will tolerate the added friction in exchange for high-value content.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2025 - 11/11/2025
Focus on the Family has been working to grow their SMS program and wanted to test the impact of requiring a mobile phone number on a content sign-up form.
We decided to test this on a free video series titled Contagious Joy, which was being promoted on Facebook with acquisition ads. Visitors who clicked the ad would land on a sign-up page with a form asking for First Name, Last Name, and Email Address.
In the Treatment version of the page, a fourth required field—Mobile Phone Number—was added. All other elements of the page remained the same.
The test was set up as a split URL experiment, with 50% of traffic directed to the control version and 50% to the treatment.
This test aimed to answer a key question: How much friction can we introduce in the name of better data collection—without meaningfully hurting conversion rates?
We did expect a potential drop in sign-ups due to the added field but hypothesized that the value of acquiring phone numbers would outweigh the slight loss in volume.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results we saw were surprising. We had anticipated a slight drop in email conversions, but didn’t expect it to be as significant as it was. Requiring a phone number led to a 26.4% decrease in email sign-ups, with a 99.9% level of confidence—clear evidence that this added field created too much friction for users coming from Facebook ads.
We also saw an almost statistically significant 40% drop in transactions on the post-sign-up donation page (92.2% LOC), suggesting the impact extended beyond the initial form. While average gift amount and recurring donor conversion didn’t change, the decrease in overall donor volume is still meaningful.
For this acquisition audience, the added requirement of a phone number appeared to outweigh the perceived value of the free video series. This test shows that asking for too much, too soon—especially from new visitors—can hurt both lead generation and potential revenue.
Going forward, it may be more effective to collect phone numbers later in the journey such as on the donation page, once trust has been built or value has been delivered. Or test adding the Phone Number field on the sign-up page but do not make it required to try and reduce friction.

experiments.title: Does Direct Messaging Increase Donor Conversion During High-Urgency Campaigns?
experiments.researchQuestion: The direct messaging treatment will drive higher conversion rates by reducing ambiguity and increasing perceived urgency; individuals are more likely to take action when the problem and required response are stated explicitly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 05/01/2025
This experiment was conducted by an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina, during their Spring Fund Drive. The campaign took place during a period of elevated urgency, as potential federal funding cuts were in the news cycle and top of mind for many supporters.
The goal of the experiment was to test the impact of message explicitness on donor engagement. We compared two versions of campaign messaging: the control featured a more indirect, suggestive tone, implying the risk to public media funding without naming specific actors. In contrast, the treatment used more direct, unambiguous language, explicitly referencing Congress and the political threats to public media funding.
While the tone and framing of the email copy differed, both versions directed users to the same donation landing page. This allowed us to isolate the effect of message directness on donor behavior, particularly under conditions of heightened urgency and media salience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This messaging experiment, run during a period of heightened urgency around public media funding, tested two approaches:
A Funding Version with direct, issue-specific language referencing Congressional threats, and
 	A General Version with a softer, more implied message.
Key Learnings:
The Funding Version drove nearly double the total attributed purchases (125 vs. 67), including post-view conversions, indicating a stronger overall persuasive effect.
 	Both versions saw similar direct donor counts (11 vs. 10), but the Funding Version's stronger post-view performance suggests it had a more lasting cognitive impact—viewers retained the message and returned later to donate, even without clicking.
 	Despite lower click volume, the Funding Version likely attracted more qualified or high-intent users—those who clicked were more likely to convert, contributing to a lower cost per donor ($54.22 vs. $59.62).
 	The alignment between the Funding Version’s direct messaging and the donation page content may have created a more seamless and cognitively consistent user experience, reducing friction and increasing likelihood to give.
 	Although the General Version received more clicks overall, these users were less likely to donate, highlighting the importance of click quality over quantity.
Takeaway:
Lean into Direct, Issue-Oriented Messaging
Test Message-to-Landing Page Alignment
Explore New Creative to Expand Reach
new creative formats
Differentiate Messaging by Audience Segment
Analyze Post-View Conversion Behavior

experiments.title: How Meta creative with a landscape or a soldier impacted email acquisition and donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that If we use a more emotionally engaging image that features a soldier making eye contact and smiling (Version B), for the Meta audience will achieve an increase in email acquisition and donations because the human-centered imagery creates a stronger emotional connection, conveys resilience and personal sacrifice, and draws the viewer into the narrative more effectively than a distant, abstract group scene..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 04/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated two different types of meta ad creative for one of their content offers, Victory and the Savior Generals. We wanted to test:
Image A: A group of soldiers hiking in silhouette, viewed from behind, ascending a hill.
VS.
Image B: A smiling, bandaged soldier holding a helmet with a bullet hole — direct eye contact with the viewer.
We wanted to test to see what the impact would be on email acquisition, donations, and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results Overview
Version A (Landscape Image)
Reach: 51,400Email Acquisitions: 516Cost per Email: $8.61Donations: 27Cost per Donation: $164.61Female Donors: $96.39 cost per donationMale Donors: $196.29 cost per donation
Version B (Soldier Image)
Reach: 51,900Email Acquisitions: 441Cost per Email: $10.08Donations: 28Cost per Donation: $158.79Female Donors: $162.07 cost per donationMale Donors: $155.99 cost per donation
Key Learnings:
Version A generated more email signups (516 vs. 441) and did so at a lower cost per acquisition. The clearer "FREE EBOOK" call to action and more spacious design likely made the value proposition more immediately apparent.Version B slightly outperformed in donation count (28 vs. 27) and had a slightly lower cost per donor. The emotional appeal and personal connection from the soldier’s face may have played a role in nudging more donors across the finish line.Creative tone influences outcome based on objective. Version A was more efficient for lead generation. Version B may have had marginally stronger performance for donor conversion, supporting the idea that emotional resonance matters more in the lower funnel.Creative resonance may vary by gender. Version A was significantly more effective at converting female donors, with a 40% lower cost per donation compared to Version B. Version B performed notably better with male donors, with a ~20% lower cost per donation than Version A...This suggests that creative resonance may vary by gender, with the soldier image potentially appealing more to men and the landscape image connecting more with women in this context.
Interpretation of Results:
Clarity beats emotion at the top of the funnel. The straightforward visual layout and “FREE EBOOK” message in Version A likely made it easier for users to understand what they were getting, which helped with email conversion.Emotion might matter more post-conversion. The slight lift in donations from Version B suggests the image's human connection and storytelling potential could increase the perceived significance of the offer, nudging users toward giving.Cost difference is minimal at the donation level. Though not statistically significant, the lower cost per donor in Version B is worth noting as a potential trend to explore further.
Next Testing Steps:
Combine the strengths of both creatives. Test a version that uses the emotionally compelling soldier image but adds back the “FREE EBOOK” call to action in bold, high-contrast text.Test additional human-centered imagery. Try other close-up image that evokes emotional engagement (e.g., generals, veterans, sacrifice) to validate the emotional-resonance hypothesis.Test gender-targeted creative variations. Run separate ad sets targeting men vs. women, using the image that performed better for each: Use Version A (landscape) for female audiences. Use Version B (soldier) for male audiences. Measure whether tailoring creative by gender improves overall cost efficiency.

experiments.title: Will longer copy help donors convert on a lower third slide up?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more copy value proposition for the lower third slide up will achieve more transactions because the additional value prop will provide social proof and clarity into what their gift is doing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/13/2025
Focus on the Family is a global ministry that is a well recognized brand. They drive tons of traffic to their site daily.
Right now, they are in the middle of a sustainer drive. To support this campaign we added what we call a ‘lower third’ slideup element to their content pages. We wanted to see if long or short copy worked best on the these pages to convert donors. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Long copy, which includes a value proposition for the lower third slideup, was more effective in converting donors compared to short copy.

2. The additional value proposition provided social proof and clarity, which likely contributed to the higher donation rate.

3. The treatment resulted in a significant increase in donations, with a 98.3% level of confidence that the results were not due to random chance.

In future experimentation, we should:

1. Continue testing different copy lengths and value propositions to further optimize donation conversions.

2. Consider incorporating social proof and clear explanations of how donations will be used in our messaging.

3. Regularly analyze and track the performance of different elements to identify what resonates best with our audience and drives the most meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How retargeting direct mail recipients with Facebook ads during year-end impacts total revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing Facebook ads to retarget direct mail recipients will achieve an increase in donations because it helps reinforce that the mail piece is incoming – keeping the ask and year-end giving to Time of Grace top of mind.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2025
During Time of Grace’s calendar year-end campaign, we hypothesized that retargeting ads in Facebook that referenced the incoming direct mail piece would increase donor conversion rate. It is important to note that these ads did not contain an ask or a donation page to give. Instead, direct mail donors were retargeted with ads that just reinforced the timing of year-end and that their direct mail piece was in the mail. Our hypothesis is that this second-channel approach would keep the direct mail piece (and Time of Grace’s ministry) top of mind during this critical fundraising period – resulting in more donors looking out for the piece and donating to it. The second related hypothesis was that if efforts did not increase donations directly to the mail piece, treatment donors would still give more overall to the organization (in either offline or online).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

First, it was worth noting that the treatment did not increase overall donations and revenue directly to the offline mail piece. However, when expanding the definition to include giving in the control and treatment (both online and offline) the increase in revenue is statistically significant. This means that this multi-channel targeting, if not directly impactful upon offline giving, still increases overall giving to the ministry across channels.

Here is an additional breakdown of key facts: 

1. Utilizing Facebook ads to retarget direct mail recipients resulted in a significant increase in revenue for Time of Grace Ministry.

2. The treatment group, which received the retargeting ads referencing the incoming direct mail piece, showed a 16.9% increase in revenue compared to the control group.

3. The increase in revenue was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the results were not due to chance.

4. The hypothesis that using Facebook ads to remind donors of the incoming direct mail piece would increase donations and keep the organization top of mind during the critical year-end fundraising period was validated.

experiments.title: How testing an organic single image ad creative against a carousel ad impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the utilization of carousel ads for prospective donors will achieve an increase in click-though rates because the multi-imagery and story-telling aspects of the carousel ads will will provide a more comprehensive and compelling story, allowing donors to see the tangible impact of their support.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit serving children and families for over one hundred years. Their top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign.
For this experiment, we tested an organic-style direct appeal ad against a carousel ad telling a personal story of a child receiving shoes, from the child’s perspective. The experiment aims to determine which ad format is more effective in driving donor action. The carousel ad utilized the image of a child on the first card, and used organic-style ads for the remaining cards, telling the child’s personal story.
Our hypothesis is that the carousel direct appeal ad featuring a narration of a child’s personal story receiving their shoes will outperform the organic-style direct appeal ad in engagement because showcasing multiple images will provide a more comprehensive and compelling story, allowing prospective engagers to see the tangible impact of their support.
In addition, by presenting a child benefiting from donations with the utilization of their personal story, the carousel ad may create a stronger emotional connection and drive more conversions by demonstrating the broader impact of giving.
We set this test up on Facebook as an A/B test so that 50% of our audience would see the control ads and the other 50% would see the treatment ads. The control campaign had the organic style single-image ad, and the treatment campaign had the carousel multi-image ads. Both campaigns had the same budget and audience targeting, and the test ran for 15 days on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the organic-style direct appeal ad outperformed the carousel ad featuring a child's personal story in driving donor action, as evidenced by a 31.1% decrease in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the single-image ad was more effective in engaging the audience and motivating them to take action compared to the multi-image carousel ad.

In addition, it is important to note that the organic-style direct appeal ad significantly outperformed the carousel ad in both donor response and cost efficiency. The organic ad generated 18 gifts with an ROI of 0.61 and a $63 gross cost per donor, whereas the carousel ad resulted in only 3 gifts, an ROI of 0.16, and a $341 cost per donor. These results suggest that a singular, emotionally compelling message with a focused visual may more effectively drive conversions than a multi-image format that disperses attention.

While the data does suggest that prioritizing organic-style direct appeals will maximize donor engagement and cost efficiency, in future campaigns, it would be beneficial to continue testing different ad formats and messaging to identify the most effective strategies for driving donor engagement and action.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 05/31/2023
PTV is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
In an effort to find new way to acquire cheaper names and donors, we decided to test Meta Lead Ads for PTV’s top performing content offer.
In the normal funnel set up, a user clicks the Facebook ad, is directed to a landing page on the orgs website where they are asked to complete a form of First Name, Last Name and Email. Once the form is completed the visitor will receive the offer in their inbox and they are redirected to a donation page where they can make a gift to ensure more resources are produced and made available.
In the Meta Lead Ad version, users never leave the Facebook app. The form is completed in the app and the funnel ends with an option to click a button to learn more about the org and therefore make a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment showed a 56% increase in the number of names acquired for this offer at a 100% level of confidence.

While this method would show great potential as a method for new name acquisition, our ultimate goal is to acquire donors. We conducted this experiment across multiple and for all offers we saw a valid decrease in donations anywhere from 50-75%.

Due to this, we decided to stick with the traditional funnel and optimize it to get the best results we can.

experiments.title: How using direct, biblically-aligned language in a Facebook ebook ad impacted sign-ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads will increase email sign-ups for the ebook because it makes the offer feel more interesting, accessible, and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/16/2024 - 10/28/2024
Luther Seminary was running Facebook ads to promote their free ebook about the book of Numbers. We wanted to test whether a shorter, clearer opening paragraph in the ad would lead to more engagement and email signups. Our theory was that the Control ad might scare users away because it used phrases like “daunting hike” and “endless desert”.
For the treatment language, we focused on getting the reader’s attention by referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using more biblically-aligned language to describe the journey of the people of Israel (“guided through the wilderness” vs the control that said “daunting hike…endless desert”).
Our hypothesis was that using direct, biblically-aligned language in our Facebook ads would increase email sign-ups for the ebook because:
It creates a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, 
Evokes a sense of divine guidance by mentioning God’s guidance, and 
Reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using direct, biblically-aligned language in the opening of our Facebook ads can increase email sign-ups for an biblical study guide ebook. The treatment ad, which focused on referencing the content of the book of Numbers and using clearer, more biblically-aligned language, led to a valid 22.1% in email sign-ups with a 99.2% level of confidence (LOC).
We also saw that the treatment ads drove a 225.5% increase in instant donations with a 97.4% level of confidence!
When thinking about the donor psychology behind these results, these are some possible reasons for why the treatment ads performed better:
Authentic Biblical Imagery: The use of traditional biblical language like "wilderness" and the reference to "walking alongside the people of Israel" resonated strongly with the target audience who are familiar with biblical narratives, appealing to those seeking a genuine, faith-based study experience.Divine Guidance and Protection: Mentioning "God guided them through the wilderness" taps into the core desire for divine guidance and protection, which adds to the spiritual significance and relevance.Accessibility and Curiosity: The simpler, more straightforward language reduces anxiety by making the offer feel more accessible. The phrase "walking alongside" suggests a less intimidating, observational role compared to the language in the control ad.
In future experimentation, Luther Seminary should continue to use direct, biblically-aligned language in their marketing materials to create a vivid, authentic connection to scripture, evoke a sense of divine guidance, and make the offer feel more accessible and relevant to potential readers seeking spiritual growth. Additionally, since instant donations also saw a significant increase with the treatment language, it may be worthwhile to explore how this language can be incorporated into donation campaigns as well.

experiments.title: How Facebook copy focused on the felt need met by an offer impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the felt need of Pastors Pen for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher engagement because there is a personal connection of what I could get out of Pastors Pen.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/28/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Via direct mail, Pathway to Victory offers a regular mailing of “Pastor’s Pen” a monthly mailout of encouragement from Dr. Robert Jeffress. This piece via direct mail has performed well in being a favorite piece to the audience but also as a channel for revenue.
In an effort to increase channel crossover between digital and direct mail, we decided to try out offering Pastors Pen through Facebook ads where the viewer can sign up to receive Pastors Pen in the mail.
For this offer, we decided to test copy. The control version focuses on the letter being a personal letter from Dr. Jeffress. Whereas the treatment presents Pastors Pen as an encouraging offer when life is difficult.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the perceived benefit or felt need of a product or service can significantly impact engagement and conversion rates. In this case, highlighting how Pastors Pen can be an encouraging resource during difficult times led to a 16.8% increase in clicks through Facebook ads.

While the treatment saw more clicks we actually did not experience an increase in form fills with a 2% increase with 12% loc.

However with donations we saw a 60% decrease with 89% loc.

So while this treatment got more clicks on the ad, the downstream metrics would tell us to keep the control.

experiments.title: How using more vigorous copy that focuses on providing value to the reader in Facebook ads affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing value through more vigorous copy for Facebook ad recipients will achieve more email acquisition because it will motivate them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary used Facebook ads to promote their free online course How to Read the Bible Like a Seminary Professor. The objective of the ads was to acquire new email addresses. The original ad copy focused on the organization first. Would more vigorous ad copy that provided value to the reader result in an increase in motivation and email acquisition?
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing value through more vigorous ad copy can significantly increase email acquisition rates. The treatment, which included more engaging and valuable ad copy, resulted in a 148.4% increase in emails acquired compared to the control group. This success was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.1%.

In future experimentation, it is essential to prioritize providing value to the audience in ad copy. By focusing on how the offering can benefit the reader and addressing their needs and interests, it is more likely to motivate them to take action, such as providing their email address. This approach can be applied to other marketing campaigns to improve results and increase the effectiveness of acquiring new leads or customers.

experiments.title: How localizing Facebook creative impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that localizing the Facebook ad creative to directly address the target audience and incorporating a clear call to action will increase the rate of completed registrations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a radio station based in North Carolina, involved a targeted Facebook media campaign designed to evaluate a novel approach. Our objective was to determine whether localizing the ad creative and directly addressing the target audience, coupled with a clear call to action, would enhance the rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Localizing the Facebook ad creative to directly address the target audience and incorporating a clear call to action significantly increased the rate of completed registrations by 51.5% for all traffic with a 97.4% level of confidence. This indicates that personalized and targeted messaging can have a significant impact on the desired outcome.

2. The Cost per Click increased by 157%, indicating that while the targeted approach may have increased engagement, it also came at a higher cost. However, the overall decrease in Cost per Registration by 352% suggests that the investment in targeted advertising paid off in terms of efficiency and effectiveness.

3. Female visitors showed a 518% increase in completed registrations with a 97% level of confidence, indicating that the personalized messaging resonated particularly well with this demographic group. This highlights the importance of understanding and tailoring marketing efforts to specific audience segments.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue leveraging personalized and targeted messaging to engage with the audience effectively. By understanding the preferences and behaviors of different demographic groups, such as females in this case, marketing strategies can be optimized to drive better results. Additionally, monitoring and analyzing key metrics such as Cost per Click and Cost per Registration can provide insights into the efficiency and effectiveness of advertising campaigns, allowing for continuous improvement and optimization.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by presenting the offer as a solution to 7 questions for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher engagement because the book becomes a solution and not just another end times book.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore this strategy of framing the book as a solution rather than just another end times book. This could involve incorporating the 7 questions into other marketing materials such as emails, website banners, and social media posts to see if it consistently boosts engagement and drives conversions.

Additionally, we should strive to reach the minimum sample size of 99,999,999 in future experiments to ensure that the results are statistically significant. This will provide more confidence in the findings and allow us to make informed decisions based on the data collected.

Overall, the success of this experiment underscores the importance of testing different messaging strategies and continuously optimizing our approach to maximize engagement and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impact clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2024 - 07/12/2024
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit committed to serving children and families, with their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign being a significant donor acquisition effort.
In this experiment, we compared the effectiveness of an iPhone notes app style ad to an organic-style text ad. The iPhone notes app style ad mimics the look of a personal notes application, featuring a familiar and engaging format. The organic-style text ad has a straightforward design with just text in a plain background. Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We set up two Advantage+ campaigns on Facebook with equal budgets for each ad style, and the test ran for 18 days. Both ad creatives have the same call-to-action: donate $15 to provide shoes but with slight differences in language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, we anticipated that the iPhone notes app style ad would outperform the organic-style text ad, leveraging its familiar and visually engaging format. However, the results showed the opposite, with the Control ads (organic-style text) achieving better performance.
The Treatment ads led to a -17.9% decrease in clicks compared to the Control ads, with a 96.5% level of confidence (LOC). The Control ads had a higher click rate (0.50%) compared to the Treatment ads (0.41%).
This test did reach statistical validity, indicating that the iPhone notes app style ad was less effective at driving clicks. Despite our hypothesis, the Treatment ads may not have resonated as strongly with the audience.
We theorized it could be due to visual engagement. The Treatment ads, designed to mimic the iPhone notes app, may have lacked the necessary visual contrast to stand out in users’ Facebook feeds. The minimalistic design, while intended to appear authentic, could have been perceived as bland or too subtle, leading users to overlook the ad. The Control, while also very minimal in design was mostly in a deep blue color that can stand out more clearly.

experiments.title: How targeting a specific audience effected click-through rates on a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe targeting a specific audience on Facebook ads leads to higher click-through rates due to increased ad relevance, reduced ad fatigue, and enhanced personalization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 08/30/2024
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, utilized a paid Facebook media campaign to test a new approach. Our goal was to determine whether tailoring our creative and ad copy to target Facebook users with an interest in health and wellness would lead to higher click-through rates and an increased number of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. **Targeting a specific audience leads to higher click-through rates**: The experiment showed that personalizing the Facebook ad creative and copy to target an audience interested in health resulted in a 153.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This demonstrates that targeting a specific audience can significantly improve the effectiveness of ad campaigns.
2. **Significant improvements in key metrics**: The treatment group not only saw a substantial increase in Clicks for All Traffic but also experienced a 453.9% increase in Complete Registrations. This indicates that by targeting a specific audience, not only can you drive more traffic but also increase the likelihood of conversions.
3. **Segment-specific improvements**: The experiment also revealed segment-specific improvements, such as a 342.4% increase in clicks for the 55-64 age group, a 71.4% increase for visitors aged 65 and above, a 194.6% increase for Mobile visitors, and a 137.4% increase for Female visitors. This underscores the importance of audience segmentation in ad targeting.

experiments.title: How a Facebook call to action impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the CTA to Download for Facebook traffic will achieve higher conversions because the action step is clear.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
Hoover runs many offers through Facebook ads, therefore in an effort to increase donor conversions on content offers, we decided to test the standard Learn More CTA vs Download CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the Call to Action (CTA) from "Learn More" to "Download" for Facebook traffic led to a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group, which had the "Download" CTA, saw a 56.1% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This result suggests that the action step of downloading may not have been clear or compelling enough for the Facebook audience, leading to a decrease in conversions. It is important to note that this experiment achieved a high statistical level of confidence, indicating that the results are reliable and likely to represent the true outcome.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the specific characteristics and preferences of the target audience when choosing and testing different CTAs. It may be helpful to gather feedback from the audience or conduct user research to better understand their motivations and expectations.

Additionally, it is essential to track and analyze the impact of any changes in CTAs on different metrics, such as conversions, click-through rates, and engagement. A/B testing allows for data-driven decision-making and helps identify what works best for maximizing the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a Facebook ad that looks like an organic Facebook post for potential subscribers will achieve a higher CTR and lower CPC because it feels more human and not like an advertisement..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
This ministry client has campaigns that helps give relief to people around the world. We wanted to test into their Facebook ad click-through rate and see if we could get a higher down-stream conversion rate. IMB’s Facebook ads normally have designed graphics with a clear message, as you can see in Sample A below. The organic ad we created for this test has simple white font text on a navy background, it included no graphics unlike the control. We’ve tested running “organic posts” for other organizations and have seen a tremendous increase in click-through rate and a decrease in cost per click. We believe that the organic Facebook ad could create a higher click-through rate since it is simple and clear and looks like an organic Facebook post which humanizes the ad. This could also lead to a decrease in cost per click since the organic ad appearance does not seem as “markety” as the control graphic.
We tested this as an A/B test with a 50/50 split in targeting.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a Facebook ad that resembles an organic post can significantly increase click-through rates and potentially decrease cost per click. The treatment ad, which had simple white font on a navy background and no graphics, outperformed the control ad with designed graphics and clear messaging.

The results of this experiment show a 51.3% increase in clicks for all traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence at 100.0%. This confirms the hypothesis that the organic-looking ad feels more human and less like an advertisement, leading to higher engagement from users.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing organic-looking ads against more traditional ad formats. This can help optimize ad performance and potentially reduce advertising costs. Additionally, considering the success of this experiment, it may be worth exploring the length of copy or color of organic ads.

experiments.title: How providing additional value prop on a content offer impacts email sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including more value prop in the ad copy for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is additional appeal and clarity on the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV utilizes Facebook as a platform to acquire new names and donors for their organization.
One offer that has been a consistently well-performing content offer is their Jesus Map digital download.
The current version of the ad has been running for multiple years and had not been optimized.
We decided to test out expanding the copy so that there is more information regarding the offer and the reasons to request it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. **Including more value proposition in the ad copy is effective**: The treatment with expanded ad copy led to a significant increase in Complete Registrations, Clicks, and improvements in Cost per Click and Cost per Registration. This indicates that providing more information and clarity on the offer can attract more viewers and increase conversions.

2. **Audience segmentation is crucial**: The experiment revealed significant increases in Complete Registrations across different visitor segments, such as 65+ visitors, Female visitors, Mobile visitors, and Tablet visitors. This highlights the importance of tailoring messaging and targeting specific audience segments to maximize engagement and conversions.

3. **Statistical significance and confidence levels**: The high confidence levels, especially at 100% for Clicks, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration, indicate that the results are reliable and not due to chance. This reinforces the validity and impact of the experiment's findings.

To apply these learnings in future experimentation, we should:

1. **Continue to test and optimize ad copy**: Based on the success of the expanded ad copy in this experiment, we should continue exploring ways to enhance value proposition and clarity in our messaging. This could involve testing different variations of ad copy to identify the most effective messaging strategies.

2. **Segment and target specific audience groups**: Building on the positive outcomes seen across different visitor segments, we should further analyze audience data to understand preferences and behaviors. By tailoring content and targeting specific groups, we can drive higher engagement and conversions.

3. **Maintain statistical rigor in experimentation**: To ensure the reliability of results, we should continue to prioritize statistical significance and confidence levels in future experiments. This will help us make data-driven decisions and confidently implement successful strategies.

Overall, by leveraging the learnings from this experiment, we can optimize our digital marketing efforts, enhance audience engagement, and ultimately drive greater success in acquiring new names and donors for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to Facebook ad copy effects registration rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating urgency into the ad copy will lead to higher completion rates for registrations. This approach aims to instill a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of acquiring this information in anticipation of the election year.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/24/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, using a paid media channel on Facebook. The ad copy promotes an email series content offer providing prospects with information on elections and politics. Our objective was to determine whether adding urgency to the copy would increase the rate of complete registrations. Both the control and treatment groups utilized the same creative elements and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Incorporating urgency into ad copy can significantly increase completion rates for registrations.
2. The treatment group, which included urgency in the ad copy, saw a 46.8% increase in emails acquired for all traffic compared to the control group.
3. The results were statistically significant with a 96.3% confidence level.
4. This approach successfully instilled a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of the information provided in the email series in anticipation of the election year.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of urgency in ad copy across different campaigns and audiences. Additionally, we can explore other ways to create a sense of urgency, such as limited-time offers or exclusive content. It is important to analyze the impact of urgency on different metrics besides email acquisition, such as engagement and conversion rates, to further optimize our marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How using simplified, invitational ad creative for a video series ad increased clicks to the landing page

rows.name: NA | Kingdom Mindset, normal ads - PO (March 2024)
NA | Kingdom Mindset, simple ads - PO (March 2024)

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a simplified style of ad creative for video series ads on Facebook will achieve an increase in clicks to the landing page because the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more. because  the ads are intriguing and make the reader want to visit the landing page to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2024 - 04/27/2024
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promote these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if using a simplified, “invitation” style of ad creative would lead to more clicks to the video series landing page.
For this experiment, we tested their standard style of ad creative against a version that had fewer design elements and was written to sound more like an invitation from the speaker in the video series, rather than an informal ad from an organization.
Our hypothesis is that the simple invitation ad design will increase clicks because it is more personal and engaging.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. Both ad variants used the same text and CTA button, the only variable was the ad creative, and we ran this test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for one month, we found that the simple, invitational ad creative increased in clicks by 46.2%, with a level of confidence (LOC) of 100%. This indicates that this result is valid and that we'd get a similar result if we tested this again.
Aside from clicks, the treatment ads also resulted in a 26% increase in email sign-ups for the video series, with a 99.9% LOC. This shows us that the simple, invitation style of ad creative didn't just motivate people to click on the ads. It also made them more motivated to sign up for the series!
Our key learnings from this experiment are:
The simplified style of ad creative, designed to sound like a personal invitation, led to a significant increase in clicks and email sign-ups. This indicates that the treatment ad was more effective in engaging and attracting viewers to take action.Testing this kind of simple invitation ad with Focus on the Family's other video series offers may lead to similar results, but each series offer is different and more testing is needed to have a more definitive answer.

experiments.title: How congruency in CTA impacted donor conversion on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the CTA consistent across an ad for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because this is congruency in the ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a different book as an incentive for donors to donate. A main channel for PTV in donor acquisition is Facebook. For July 2024, PTV’s featured item is Choose Your Attitude, Change Your Life.
In an effort to increase donor conversion we decided to test using the same CTA in the body, image and headline of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that maintaining consistency in the call-to-action (CTA) across an ad for Facebook viewers can significantly increase conversion rates. In this case, using the same CTA in the body, image, and headline of the ad resulted in a 74.8% increase in donations for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 97.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember the impact of congruency in the ask. By consistently delivering the same CTA message throughout an ad, viewers are more likely to respond positively and take the desired action. This can lead to higher conversion rates and ultimately, more successful donation campaigns.

Moving forward, we should continue to test and optimize our ad strategies, keeping in mind the importance of message consistency and congruency in driving viewer engagement and conversions. By leveraging this learning in future experiments, we can further refine our approach and continue to improve our donor acquisition efforts for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of the primary ad text on a content offer Facebook ad impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the primary ad text for a free content offer Facebook ad will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream donations because it will reduce friction for the user.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. These ads rely on compelling ad imagery and lengthy ad copy promoting the series and explaining its value.
We aimed to determine if using a compelling image with shorter ad copy would result in greater engagement, more clicks, and increased donations. The hypothesis was that shorter copy would reduce friction, allowing the video series landing page to effectively communicate the offer’s value and convert traffic.
We developed a new campaign for their “Cultural Deceptions” video series, using the same imagery as our control campaign but with abbreviated primary ad text. The revised ad included just a single sentence: “Check out best-selling author and apologist Alisa Childer’s new free video series, “God’s Truth Amidst Cultural Deceptions,” to uncover the lies behind some of today’s most popular social media slogans.”
We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature and conducted the test over 12 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our key learning from this experiment was that shortening the length of the primary ad text on our free video series offer Facebook ads led to a significant decrease in donations for the "Cultural Deceptions" video series campaign.
The treatment ads with the shorter primary ad text saw a 53.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).
In future experimentation, it's essential to consider the impact of ad text on donation behavior. Our hypothesis assumed that shortening the primary ad text would reduce friction for the user and thus increase engagement. However, our results in this test showed the opposite effect. Instead of adding friction, the longer ad text in the Control ads provided important context for social audiences. Not only did we validate a decrease in donations, but also a 52.7% decrease in clicks with a 100% LOC.
However, there are a few ________
The cost per click was $0.12 cheaper on the short ads vs the long ads. 61% diff in CPM $4.73 instead of $12.06The GCPD was $3 less for the short
The results of this experiment show that longer, more detailed ad copy may be necessary to effectively communicate the value of the video series offer and encourage donations. While reducing friction for the user is crucial, it should not come at the expense of providing important information that can drive conversions. In future ads and testing, we will look at how to balance brevity and informativeness to optimize campaign performance.

experiments.title: How using a ‘pattern interrupt’ in a paid ad affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts will produce higher acquisition rates and lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA—despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 3 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
The simple design did not distract from the value of the offer itself
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to snatch the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test we pitted our usual control —3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field—against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.
The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 1786.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 5556.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.1946.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90
This suggests that by using a creative approach to funnel design, one that subtly blends the ordinary with the unexpected, we can entice prospects to engage by creating an experience beyond just "showing them an ad."
The key to the success of this concept, we believe, is presenting everyday design elements and  language outside of their expected context, using day-to-day, non-promotional urgency, to stoke curiosity and build momentum through to the landing page
What's interesting about this test is that if you stop to think about the treatment logically, it doesn't make any sense. But that does not mean that its "illogical" construction was unintentional. The incongruity of the presentation, we hypothesize, plays a large part in piquing the prospect's interest and pulling them into the funnel.
In future experimentation, we should continue exploring the surprising juxtapositions of elements. It would be interesting to test this concept throughout the entire funnel.
And while we are intrigued and inspired to keep testing this concept, we are also aware of the potential for "fatigue" with the specific application we used in this test. Therefore, while we encourage the "note app" treatment for low-investment to test the concept, and will try it with a few different offers, we believe we will eventually need to test this concept using different tactics and applications.

experiments.title: How using an iPhone Notes App style of Facebook ad creative impacted clicks to a video series landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative image to mimic a Notes App style for Facebook traffic will achieve an increase in clicks and email signups because the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching. because  the ad creative looks more authentic and eye-catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2024 - 06/24/2024
Focus on the Family is a large Christian ministry organization that creates and shares free resources like video series to encourage families. They promoted these free video series on Facebook and wanted to see if we could try some radically different ad creatives to improve clicks and email signups from their ads.
For this experiment, we tested using an organic, non-branded ad that resembles a screenshot of someone’s iPhone Notes App. This ad creative featured an impact-focused headline and three bullet point statements below.
The control ads for this test used the same headline and bullet points, but they would look like the typical branded ads we usually run on Facebook.
Our hypothesis is that the Notes App ads, with their authentic and eye-catching design, will significantly boost clicks and email signups. This is due to their unique appearance, which we believe will resonate more with our target audience on Facebook.
We ran this as an A/B campaigns test in Facebook with the ad budget split evenly. The Control campaign had the standard ads for two video series, and the Treatment campaign had the Notes App version of the ad creative for those same two video series. All other variables remained the same, and the test ran for a total of 2.5 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We expected the Notes App style of ad creative to perform better in this test, and we were surprised to see that our Control ads actually had the best results.
These are the results we saw:
The Treatment ads led to a -6.1% decrease in clicks to the video series landing page, with a 99.9% level of confidence (LOC).The Treatment ads also validated a -7.9% decrease in the number of emails acquired, with a 99.6% LOC.And the Treatment ads had a -24.0% decrease in donations, with a 98.8% LOC.The Control ads had a campaign ROI of 1.39, which was higher than the Treatment campaign's ROI of 0.94.
Our key learnings from this experiment are that the Notes App style ad creative did not perform as well as the standard branded ads in terms of clicks, email signups, and donations. Despite the hypothesis that the more authentic and eye-catching design would lead to an increase in engagement, the results showed a decrease in clicks to the landing page, emails acquired, and donations.
We have a couple of theories on why the Treatment ads performed worse in this test, and these are ideas that we can test into with future ads:
The Treatment ads lacked the Focus on the Family branding and logo, which could have decreased their credibilityThe Treatment ads did not incorporate any imagery to support the value or impact of the video series, but the Control ads used imagery to support their claims.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.The Control ads had more color to help them stand out on Facebook, whereas the white background of the Treatment ads caused them to almost "blend in" with the rest of the white space on the page.Ex: For the "Reconnected" marriage video series ad, the image in the Control is a couple holding hands. This supports the message that the video series will help the reader reconnect with their spouse.

experiments.title: How video ad creative impacts engagement n compared to static ad creative
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that video ad creative for a direct appeal campaign will be more engaging than an organic image and lead to higher engagement by increasing the visual appeal and clarity of the donation CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/10/2024
Bible League Canada ran a direct appeal campaign on Facebook, inviting users to donate to support children’s Biblical education worldwide. In a prior test, we validated that using “organic” looking ad creative, we could increase engagement and donations for direct appeal campaigns. However, for this campaign, we could use an inspirational short video that demonstrated the need for Christian education worldwide. We hypothesized that this moving video could attract more users on Facebook and lead to better results.
We built two campaigns with matching ad copy; the only change was the ad creative. Our control campaign featured the “organic” looking static image we’d tested in the past against the video ad creative. We ran the test for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Video outperformed static image: The experiment demonstrated that using a video ad creative for direct appeal campaigns led to a significant increase in engagement, as indicated by the 84.1% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This result supports the hypothesis that video ads are more engaging than static images for this type of campaign. This in turn led to a 300% increase in donations at a 92% Level of confidence.

2. Visual appeal and clarity: The success of the video ad creative in this experiment may be attributed to its ability to convey the need for Christian education worldwide in a more compelling and emotionally impactful way compared to the static image. The moving visuals likely captured users' attention and prompted them to take action.

It is worth noting that our CPM more than doubled for the video campaign and our cost per click went up 8%. However, the people we were paying to place the ad in front of were far more interested in the content, and thus, our click-through rate and instant donation page conversion rate both increased significantly, leading to the 300% increase in donations. (Our instant conversion rate increased 117%).

We will continue testing the impact of inspirational videos like this in other parts of the funnel and on other campaigns to see their impact.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing AVID Pathways to Facebook as an audience resulted in a significantly higher match rate compared to uploading donor information via CSV. The AVID Pathways connection saw a 45.6% increase in match rate for all segments, with a 100.0% statistical confidence level, indicating a clear win.
For this experiment, we utilized 4 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
multichannel donorsoffline only donorsoffline with email donorsonline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For multichannel donors, donors who have donated through online and offline channels, we saw a 5.5% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline-only donors, we saw an increase of 144.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For offline with email donors, we saw an increase of 34.8% in match rate at a 100% level of confidence. For online donors, we saw an 8.3% decrease in match rate at a 100% level of confidence.
While a couple of cohorts did see decreases in match rates, we believe that the AVID Pathways continuous connection will more than compensate for the fewer matches. In addition, we anticipate that AVID Pathways data will continue to grow in knowledge and data points as more systems are connected and data points are expanded.
For this organization, the most surprising cohort result is for offline-only donors. These donors do not have email addresses on file with the organization and, therefore, have fewer data points than the other cohorts. However, even with that missing point, we saw a significant increase in the match rate, which means we will be able to retarget these donors with Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How removing an eBook from Facebook creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that substituting the 3D eBook with a scientific image will lead to increased registration rates, as it is expected to captivate the attention of the targeted demographic more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/28/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of a non-profit organization specializing in scientific research, education, and charitable initiatives. The test was carried out as a paid social media advertising campaign on Facebook, comparing the effectiveness of two different creative images. Our objective was to ascertain whether the target audience of this non-profit organization would respond more favorably to a 3D image of an eBook or a photograph. The purpose of this experiment was to determine whether adjusting the creative approach could enhance audience engagement, ultimately leading to a higher rate of completed registrations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that the treatment, which involved using a scientific image instead of a 3D eBook image, significantly increased registration rates across various segments of the target audience. The treatment resulted in a 247.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 100.0% level of confidence.

In addition, the experiment also showed significant decreases in Cost per Click (78%) and Cost per Registration (243%) with high levels of confidence, indicating that the treatment not only attracted more registrations but did so more cost-effectively.

Moving forward, the learnings from this experiment suggest that utilizing scientific images may be more effective in capturing the attention and interest of the target audience for the SETI Institute. Future experimentation should focus on leveraging visually engaging scientific images to drive higher engagement and conversion rates. It is important to continue testing different visual creatives to identify the most effective approach for engaging the audience and achieving the organization's objectives.
