experiments.title: How showing a well known speaker on a Facebook ad impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a photo of a well-known speaker for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve a higher CTR  because the familarity principle suggests that people will develop a preference to what they are familiar with.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2024 - 04/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. A Hoover content offer, Dueling Populisms, showed a low CTR rate. Therefore, we wanted to test at the beginning of the funnel on the Facebook ad. Looking at previous content offers’ ad creatives; we saw ads with a photo of Victor David Hanson performed well because the audience was familiar with him. Since Dueling Populisms is written by Victor, we decided to apply that to this content offer as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:
Using a photo of a well-known speaker (Victor David Hanson) for Hoover's Facebook audience resulted in a 49.7% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This indicates that leveraging familiarity with a respected figure can significantly improve audience engagement. Cost per Click decreased by 51.2% and Cost per Registration decreased by 40.5% with a 100% level of confidence, showing that the treatment was not only more effective in driving clicks but also more cost-efficient in converting these clicks into registrations. Cost per Registration decreased by 40.5% with a 100% level of confidence.While this did not validate, it had a CPP decrease of 81.9%
In future experimentation, it is crucial to:
Continue leveraging the familiarity principle by featuring well-known speakers or personalities in ad creatives to drive higher engagement rates.Monitor cost metrics closely to ensure that any changes in ad creatives or audience targeting not only drive engagement but also remain cost-effective.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of understanding the preferences and behaviors of the target audience, and using this knowledge to optimize future advertising strategies for the Hoover Institution.

experiments.title: How removing most of the primary ad text from a content offer Facebook ad impacts downstream donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing most of the the primary ad text from a free content offer Facebook Ad will remove friction and increase clicks and downstream donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/04/2024
Focus on the Family uses dozens of free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. These ads rely on compelling ad imagery and lengthy ad copy promoting the series and explaining its value. We hypothesized that removing this and instead using a compelling ad image with a short overlaid Call to Action would lead to greater engagement on the ads and, thus, more clicks and donations because it was removing friction from engaging with the ad and letting the video series landing page do the “heavy lifting” of explaining the offer value and converting traffic.
We created a new campaign for their Proof You Need video series that used the same ad imagery as our control campaign but without the extra primary ad text, leaving just a single ad sentence: ” Experience a bold confidence in the reliability of Scripture with these free videos!” We split the campaign traffic using Facebook’s AB testing feature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing the primary ad text from a free content offer Facebook Ad led to a significant decrease in donations for the "Proof You Need" video series campaign. The treatment group, which had the primary ad text removed, saw a 55.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the control performed better than the treatment.
In future experimentation, it's essential to consider the impact of ad text on donation behavior. While the hypothesis assumed that removing ad text would reduce friction and increase engagement, the results showed the opposite effect. Instead of adding friction, this ad text provided important context for social audiences. Not only did we validate a decrease in donations, but also a 95% decrease in clicks.

experiments.title: How does testing donor lookalike audience from HubSpot versus AVID Pathways compare
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using AVID Pathways connection to create donor lookalike audiences for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate in donations because AVID has more data points on donor and therefore can create better lookalike audiences.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/23/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Historically, PTV uses HubSpot’s integration with Facebook to push HS lists into FB and create audiences. While HubSpot is a fantastic platform and has greatly increased the overall performance of PTV’s program, HS only has contacts with email addresses. This means we are unable to target contacts with no email addresses on file.
Recently, PTV signed up for AVID Pathways. AVID has the ability to pull in data from many, many different platforms and marry it all together. This means AVID has access to all donor data regardless of if they have an email or not on file. AVID Pathways is the method in which AVID is able to take the mass amount of data and create segments that can then be pushed into multiple systems, such as Facebook.
We decided to test the HubSpot Facebook integration versus the AVID Pathways Facebook integration to see which platform would convert the most donors. And to make things more interesting, we decided to do this through having Facebook create lookalike audience from HS’s connection and AVID’s connection.
PTV has a popular campaign promoting its book “Perfect Ending” therefore we took this offer and created 2 campaigns. For the control, we took HubSpot’s list of donors, pushed it into Facebook and created a 1% lookalike audience in Facebook. For the treatment, we took the AVID Pathway’s list of donors, pushed it into Facebook and created a 1% lookalike audience in Facebook.
Each variant used the same ad copy and creative, the only difference was the targeting efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that AVID Pathways saw a 143% increase in donations with a 99.8% level of confidence.
In additional we found that donors acquired from HubSpot cost $59.83 whereas AVID Pathway donors cost $27.48, this is a 54% decrease in cost with a 99% level of confidence.
With this information, PTV has updated all Facebook campaigns to utilize the AVID Pathways connection. We anticipate results to vary across segments and offers but we expect them to be positive changes.
In addition, just in this one test, a $500/mo. (max) AVID Pathway service saved PTV over $1K on acquisition.

experiments.title: How co-targeting with Facebook ads affects direct mail acquisition response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing Facebook ads for direct mail acquisition prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because we will increase brand awareness and familiarity during the “ask period”. because we will increase brand awareness and familiarity during the “ask period”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/25/2023
Human Coalition was running direct mail acquisition and wanted to test the impact of adding brand ads alongside these pieces so that people would see ads 7 days before and 7 days after the direct mail piece arrived in their mailbox.
There was a signer test in the direct mail piece itself, between the Human Coalition President Jeff Bradford and the former NFL Player Benjamin Watson. Ads were reflective of the signer that the recipient would receive a letter from.
They split the acquisition lists in two and launched an ad campaign, spending $5000 for the total audience to maximize reach across the target audiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding brand ads alongside direct mail acquisition pieces significantly increased the conversion rate and resulted in a 35.8% increase in donations. This indicates that the strategy of increasing brand awareness and familiarity during the "ask period" positively influenced donors to take action.

It is important to note that these results only came with the Benjamin Watson treatment — there was no significant difference on the Jeff Bradford-signed email, which had a lower response rate and lost the signer test.

Based on these findings, it is recommended to continue using brand ads alongside direct mail acquisition campaigns in future experimentation. This strategy can be an effective way to maximize reach across target audiences and increase conversion rates. However, it is important to carefully monitor and analyze the performance of these ads to ensure they are effectively driving donations.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to further explore the impact of different ad messaging and visuals to optimize the effectiveness of the brand ads. By continuously testing and refining the ad content, it is possible to identify the most compelling and persuasive elements that resonate with the target audience and drive even higher conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of integrating digital advertising with traditional direct mail campaigns and the potential for significant increases in donations and conversion rates when done effectively.

experiments.title: How using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad impacted clicks compared to a designed image using an overlaid CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad will increase clicks by making the ad appear more authentic because it looks like a post from another Facebook user
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/20/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For one of their best-performing campaigns, they hypothesized that they could increase engagement by making the ad look like a post from another user. Users who share content don’t create “ads” with a custom photo with overlaid call to action text. Instead, they often post a plain image with some thoughts about it.
We created an ad treatment with no text or CTA overlaying the image. We used the ad copy itself to promote the free video series to users and invite them to sign up. We ran this against our control ad images that featured overlaid CTAs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an organic-looking Facebook ad design, which resembles a post from another user, can increase clicks significantly compared to traditional ad designs with overlaid call-to-action text. This approach creates a more authentic and engaging experience for the audience, leading to higher engagement with the ad content.
Moving forward, we should leverage this learning in future experimentation by incorporating more organic-looking ad designs in our campaigns. By focusing on creating content that mimics user-generated posts rather than traditional ad formats, we can build a stronger connection with our audience and drive higher click-through rates. Additionally, we should continue to test and optimize different creative approaches to further enhance our advertising strategies and maximize results.
For a future offer, we will test no ad copy and instead use an image with a clear, short call to action on a plain-colored background.
Impact on other metrics:
The treatment decreased the gross cost per click by $0.48 ($1.42 vs $190)There was no statistically significant difference in emails acquired during the experiment.

experiments.title: How co-targeting with Facebook ads affects direct mail acquisition response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing Facebook ads for direct mail acquisition prospects  will achieve an increase in conversion rate because we will increase brand awareness and familiarity during the “ask period”. because  we will increase brand awareness and familiarity during the “ask period”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/29/2024
The Navigators were running direct mail acquisition and wanted to test the impact of adding brand ads alongside these pieces so that people would see ads 10 days before and 10 days after the direct mail piece arrived in their mailbox.
In the ads, they addressed that the user would be receiving Christmas cards and a calendar from The Navigators in the mailbox soon. The ads had no other call to action or button to click through to another page.
They launched an ad campaign targeting 50% of the list (25,000 people) and spent $2500 to maximize reach across the target audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding Facebook ads alongside direct mail acquisition efforts significantly increases conversion rates. In this case, The Navigators saw an 11.9% increase in donations when they ran brand ads 10 days before and 10 days after sending out direct mail pieces.
This demonstrates the importance of building brand awareness and familiarity with potential donors before making the direct ask for donations. By priming prospects with information about the upcoming direct mail pieces, the organization was able to improve the effectiveness of their fundraising efforts.
Moving forward, The Navigators should continue to integrate digital advertising with their direct mail campaigns to maximize their impact. They can use similar strategies in future experimentation by testing different messaging, creative, and timing to further optimize their results. Additionally, they should consider expanding this approach to other marketing channels to create a cohesive and multi-touchpoint donor acquisition strategy.

experiments.title: How an ultra-personal, interruptive ad format affects email acquisition and CPA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ultra-personal format in an unfamiliar context will produce higher acquisition rates and a lower CPA by using incongruence to “jar the prospect to attention” and entice them to engage with the offer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/03/2023
For our content offers aimed at name acquisition, we’ve traditionally had a difficult time finding a treatment that outperformed a simple 3D render of the resource, especially in terms of CPA — despite the widely parroted best practice of putting value prop copy on the image.
Our hypothesis has always been that the simple presentation of a 3D render on a plain white background worked for 2 primary reasons:
The simple design was more authentic than a heavily-designed ad
The simple design, in its simplicity, stood out from the crowd of busier, more colorful ads and posts
But still, to be convinced of the value of the offer, the prospect had either to read the ad’s “primary text” or click through to the landing page.
And we continued to suspect that if there was a way to prominently feature the value of an offer on the ad image itself, where a user’s attention is naturally drawn, without sacrificing the authentic, human appeal of the 3D render, it may be able to grab the prospect’s attention, in a unique way, and more effectively.
For this test, we pitted our usual control — a 3D render of the offer as the ad image with value proposition copy in the “primary text” field — against a simple screen grab of a note we created using the Apple Note app on our phone, which communicated the value of the offer, along with common, everyday language used for the primary text.
Because we were testing a new concept, we changed both the ad image and primary text but made sure both fit into our overall hypothesis. We split traffic between the control and the treatment and let the test run until it reached statistical validity and acquired more than 50 conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that presenting everyday elements in uncommon contexts can significantly improve acquisition rates and lower CPA.

The treatment used unexpected juxtaposition to catch the prospect's attention, resulting in a 106.6% increase in email acquisition for all traffic compared to the control. But that's not all. Here are the highlights:
107% increase in email acquisition -- 39 v. 17
 	86.49% increase in CTR -- 64.9% v. 34.8%
 	87.3% increase in outbound clicks -- 103 v. 55
 	56.4% decrease in CPA -- $9.68 v. $22.19
 	46.5% decrease in cost-per-click -- $3.69 v. $6.90

experiments.title: How an organic style ad impacted conversions on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a Facebook ad that looks like an organic Facebook post for potential pledge signers will result in an increase in email conversion because the ad comes across more like a message from a human and less like marketing.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/19/2024
When promoting a pledge asking people to add their signature of support, we wondered if we could increase click through rate by developing a treatment ad that looked more like a personal request and less like an organization-centric marketing ad. Historically, we’ve seen a higher response rate this style of ad improve CPC and CTR in previous tests.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a Facebook ad that resembles an organic Facebook post, rather than a traditional marketing ad, can significantly increase email conversions. The treatment ad, which looked more like a personal message, resulted in a 43.6% increase in emails acquired compared to the control ad. This improvement was statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on this finding, it is recommended to continue using the treatment ad format in future experiments and campaigns. By creating ads that mimic the style of a personal message, the organization can enhance its engagement with potential pledge signers and increase the likelihood of conversion. It is important to prioritize a human-centered approach in ad design and make the content feel more relatable and less promotional.

With almost identical spend on each version, clickthrough rate on the treatment version improved by 43%. Cost per click decreased 28%. Sign up rate improved by 45%. Instant donor conversion rate improved by 14%. Gross Cost per Name decreased by 50%, Gross Cost Per Donor decreased by 56% and Net Cost Per Donor decreased by 76% - leading to an improved ROI by 231% on the treatment. We believe one reason we saw these results is due to the clarity of the call to action by putting the desired action front and center in easy to read large text directly on the image so it is more clear to the ad viewer what the next step to take is. in future tests, we can test new messaging on the same style of graphic to see if we can continue to improve response by finding the right CTA.

experiments.title: How using a pledge vs a direct account registration ask through Facebook Lead Ads affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe we’ll receive cheaper names through a pledge lead ad than through a direct account creation ask because signing a pledge is less of a commitment.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/05/2023
This organization is trying to acquire more new accounts for its school curriculum program. We wanted to see how lead ads would affect sign up rate and cost per new account, but we decided to test two different types of lead ads to see which “ask” performed better.
The first type was a teacher pledge. This Facebook ad led to a form in Facebook where they submit their name and email address to sign the pledge. Then their account is created using a zapier connection. Their log in information is emailed to them.
The second type was a direct ask to make an account. This ad lef to a form in Facebook where they submitted their name and email address to create their account. It was created and their log in information was emailed to them.
Although the first type is an indirect ask to create an account, we anticipated we’d receive cheaper names since the ask is more straight-forward, and has less commitment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the teacher pledge ask performed better than the direct account creation ask in terms of acquiring emails. The treatment saw a 25.7% decrease in emails acquired compared to the control. This finding is statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is recommended to continue using the teacher pledge ask in future experimentation for acquiring new accounts. This type of ask leads to more email acquisitions and is more effective in generating new accounts.

experiments.title: How Facebook Audience+ impacted conversions

rows.name: PTV DA Christmas CD 2023 Advantage audience

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by using Facebook audience+ targeting for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because it is a Facebook feature and Facebook tends to favor itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/08/2023 - 11/15/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Facebook is continually releasing new features to improve ad delivery and conversions.
For this test, we decided to test the Facebook Audience+ versus the traditional audience setup (open, housefile, web visitors, page likers, and LAL of all).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the traditional audience setup (open, housefile, web visitors, page likers, and LAL of all) performed better than the Facebook Audience+ targeting in terms of clicks, complete registrations, purchases, and cost per click.
The treatment using Facebook Audience+ targeting resulted in a 14.9% decrease in clicks for all traffic and a 497% decrease in purchases. The cost per click also increased by 111% and the cost per purchase increased by 879%.
Furthermore, certain segments of the audience also saw significant decreases in clicks, including the 45-54 age group (526% decrease), the 65+ age group (146% decrease), tablet visitors (351% decrease), and female visitors (157% decrease).
These results indicate that the Facebook Audience+ targeting did not perform as well as the traditional audience setup in terms of driving engagement and conversions.
In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue using the traditional audience setup for Facebook ads. It is important to remember that while Facebook may release new features and tools, it does not necessarily mean that they will always outperform the existing methods. Testing different targeting options and strategies is crucial to determine what works best for the specific audience and objectives.
Future test iterations, we will be testing the Facebook Advantage+ against open ad sets only while holding other ad sets the same.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because they understand what we are asking them to do..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2023
Ben Carson’s American Cornerstone Institute was running ads promoting their Little Patriots Curriculum. On review, the ads that had performed best seemed to have a mismatch of creative and text. “Liberty the Eagle” was featured in the image, while the text asked the reader to pledge to teach kids founding principles.
Liberty the Eagle is a character in the Little Patriots curriculum, but that’s only encountered after they sign up — which means at the point of seeing the ad, the reader has no idea who Liberty is. They decided to replace this with a picture of Dr. Carson, a well-known figure. Once they did that, they realized they could change the “sign the pledge” ask to a “statement of belief” ask that is followed by the “sign the pledge” ask.
They launched two copy variants of the new ads to their audiences to see what worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The importance of aligning the creative and text in an ad: The mismatch between the image of "Liberty the Eagle" and the text asking for a pledge to teach kids founding principles resulted in lower clickthrough rates. This suggests that the audience may not have understood the ask or the connection between the image and text.
2. Clarity in the call-to-action: Increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors led to a significant increase in clicks. This emphasizes the importance of clearly communicating the desired action or response from the audience.
3. The significance of statistical confidence: The treatment group showed a 218.6% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This high level of statistical confidence suggests that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
To utilize these learnings in future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the creative and text in ads are aligned and coherent. Additionally, clear and explicit calls-to-action should be employed to improve engagement and conversions. It is also important to continue using statistical analysis to determine the significance of the results and validate the effectiveness of the changes made.

experiments.title: How a more direct value proposition affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that direct ask ads for Facebook users will achieve an increase in conversion rate because they already understand the story of the situation and the needs.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/20/2023
IMB was raising money for missionaries in the Holy Land. It was a high-urgency event, and they didn’t know which way of presenting the value proposition would resonate best. They created four versions of the ads:
A story-based approach
A more aggressive version of #1
A needs-based approach
A direct ask
Though they hypothesized that the direct ask would work best, they launched these ads across all audiences to determine which approach was most impactful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads was the most impactful in terms of generating clicks and achieving a high conversion rate. Despite the hypothesis that the direct ask approach would work best, it was important to run the experiment and gather data to confirm this assumption.
Based on the results, it is clear that Facebook users already understand the story and needs, and they respond well to a direct ask for support. This suggests that in future experimentation, the nonprofit should continue using the direct ask approach in their ads, as it has shown to be effective in increasing conversion rates.
Additionally, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different approaches and not relying solely on assumptions or hypotheses. By running experiments and analyzing the results, the nonprofit can make data-informed decisions and optimize their ads for maximum impact.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the direct ask approach for Facebook ads resonates well with the audience and should be used in future experimentation and campaigns.

experiments.title: How yes/no ads on Facebook for a course impacted conversions

The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.

2. However, the treatment version did show significant improvements in other metrics: Clicks increased by 355%, Complete Registrations decreased by 289%, and Cost per Click decreased by 814%. These findings suggest that the treatment had a strong impact on engagement and cost-effectiveness.
Based on these learnings, in future experimentation, it is important to consider the specific audience and context in which "yes/no" buttons are being used. While they may be effective for some clients, it appears that they did not resonate with the target audience of the Hoover Institution.
When designing future experiments, it is essential to carefully evaluate the potential impact of the changes being made and consider the potential risks and benefits. It is also crucial to closely monitor and analyze various metrics to fully understand the impact on different aspects of the campaign.

experiments.title: How an “Organic Status” Facebook Ad Affected Conversion Rate

rows.name: NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Control
NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an “Organic Status” ad for Facebook users will achieve an increased conversion rate because  the ad will stand out amongst other sponsored posts..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/10/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. We decided to test if an organic looking facebook post used as an ad increased clicks and conversion rates compared to their traditional looking ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning in this experiment is that Facebook users are more likely to click on an ad if it seems like it is an organic post. Because the ad looks like an organic status post, it stands out to users, so they don't immediately assume it's an ad and scroll past it. Making the ad stand out more to users increased the number of clicks by 49.5% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence & decreased the Cost per Click by 19.6% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence as well. I believe this positive effect was also impacted by the fact that the treatment seemed like a personal recommendation versus just a promotion of a product. It is important to note that these ads ran exclusively in-feed on Facebook. All other ad placements were turned off.
It is also interesting to note that the older the audience, the higher the conversion rate. This could be due to the fact that an older user may not be able to tell as easily a a younger user that it is an ad and not an organic post making them more likely to click on it.
Moving forward, we should continue to test this type of "Organic Status" ad on various types on content offers that appeal to varying types and ages of audiences. We should also try applying the strategy of making an ad look more organic in other ways, such as a normal status post, and maybe try an organic looking Instagram & Facebook story post as well.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update(Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
The first time we ran this experiment CaringBridge was undergoing a change in donation platform. This additional variable introduced during the experiment did not give us the clean data needed. We rested this concept to gather better data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN emails led to a significant decrease in donations for all traffic. This experiment resulted in a 34% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 99% - making this a valid result. This confirms that the initial experiment is also a valid result in the decrease in donor conversion rate.
In future experimentation, it is recommended to explore other messaging strategies that may resonate better with the audience and use other psychological principles to move prospective donors to action.

experiments.title: How different types of graphics for Facebook ads affect click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a more inviting image with better value proposition for Facebook users will achieve a higher CTR because the treatment image is telling the user what value they will receive through these daily devotions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/10/2023
This ministry client wanted to work on increasing the click-through rate on their Facebook campaign for daily devotionals program.
We decided to do a radical redesign of the image and created a new image to test against the original to see if we could increase CTR and decrease CPC. The treatment image used a background image that was more inviting than the solid red background on the control.
The control image described WHAT the user can do (Connect with God every day), and the treatment image described HOW the user’s day can start by connecting with God (Start your day with purpose).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The control version performed better in terms of click-through rate compared to the treatment version. The treatment version had a 26.2% decrease in CTR with a 99.9% level of confidence. Despite using a more inviting image and emphasizing the value proposition, the treatment version failed to attract as many clicks as the control version.

When looking at the downstream metrics, the treatment also had:
A 4.7% decrease in email acquisition with a 31.7% LoC
 	A 0% conversion rate compared to the control with a 1.53% conversion rate (This is based on two gifts)

experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA impacted FB clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, which had the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 48.8% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version with 97.7% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid. We also saw a 30% decrease in cost per click on the "Learn More" version.
This suggests that the direct ask of "Donate Now" may have created friction and anxiety for the viewers, causing them to hesitate or be reluctant to click on the ad. By offering a lower level of commitment with the "Learn More" CTA, viewers may have felt more comfortable and willing to engage with the ad.
When reviewing results, we also noted that one additional reason the treatment could have outperformed the control is that Facebook, by default, includes a subheader message when the "Donate Now" button is selected that says "Not affiliated with Meta" which could also be introducing more friction for visitors and introducing anxiety if the message appears suspicious or untrustworthy. This default subheader also replaces the available room to display the full headline so the treatment version has the benefit of showing the full headline of "Help equip future conservative leaders" which offers more clarity than the cut off version "Help equip future..."

experiments.title: How an organic looking Facebook Ad impacts ad engagement and email acquisition in a content offer funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an organic looking ad design will increase the number of emails acquired by increasing engagement with the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2023 - 05/30/2023
KCBI is a Christian broadcast ministry in Dallas Texas. For their latest devotional eBook content offer, they wanted to test a more “authentic” and “organic” looking design to their ads. They hypothesized that a plain image with text against a solid colored background, mimicking Facebook’s status update template, would increase engagement on the ad because it wouldn’t look like an “ad” compared to their standard design that featured the eBook cover in the image and ad copy describing the resource. The only copy in the treatment would be the copy in the organic looking image.
They set it up as a AB test in Facebook and ran it for 10 days with equal budget behind each variation
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic design had had a significantly lower CPM of just $7.55 vs $20.75 on the control. However, the Link Click Through Rate on the treatment was significantly lower than the CTR on the control. Just 45% on the treatment vs 78% on the control. This meant that the treatment had a smaller lead in cost per link click.  $0.96 on the treatment vs $1.38 on the control.
Ultimately, we were trying to get more subscribers, not just clicks. The treatment had a 90% lower subscriber rate which led to a $10.23 gross cost per name for the organic design vs a $2.37 gross cost per name for the control.
What this shows is that we don't just need more clicks, we need the right clicks on our ads and the lack of additional value propositional copy in the ad led to a significantly higher proportion of unmotivated traffic to the landing page. Additional testing is needed to see if an "organic" ad design with ad copy would perform better than our standard ad creative.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a petition page affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an element of social proof for potential petition signers will achieve a higher email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/19/2022 - 11/12/2022
MSLF is running a petition as an acquisition offer. We wanted to test how adding social proof to the petition page of how many people have already signed the petition would affect email conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The social proof element caused a 6.8% increase in email acquisition with a 99.8% level of confidence. We believe this means that users are more likely to sign a petition when they see the evidence of others signing and believing in the value proposition as well.

Although we saw a valid increase in email acquisition, we saw a directional decrease of 28.6% for revenue received on the following instant donation page. We would like to test this concept further on other acquisition offers to see if we have the same results for revenue.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 28.6% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increased appeal through value proposition and social proof impacted course sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increased appeal through value proposition and social proof for potential coursetakers will achieve an increase in course sign ups because potential coursetakers will be inspired by what the course offers them and by the overall popularity of the course.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/08/2023
In an effort to increase course sign ups through their Revelations course registration page, DTS has developed a treatment to communicate enhanced appeal for the offer and leverage social proof.
For the treatment, we’re refocusing the value proposition on the potential course taker rather than the organization and the course professor. We want to test the angle of “what can this course do for me” or “what can I get out of this course”. And we also want to leverage social proof to see if joining “one of our most popular courses” or “30,000 people who have already enrolled” will motivate people to take the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our combination of increased appeal and social proof produced a valid 62.7% decrease in course sign ups during a time span of only 13 days.
It seems this approach was not well-received by our audience, as we saw a decrease in all segments and down-funnel at the donations and revenue levels as well.
Perhaps our audience does appreciate the background of the professor of the course and learning about the organization's purpose for their courses as they get ready to sign up – more so than learning what they may get out of this specific course. Additionally, social proof may not move the needle if someone is interested in the course from a personal education standpoint rather than a "popularity" stand point.
Ultimately, we may want to test different value proposition that better compliments the course instructor and organization's mission instead of taking an entirely different angle like we did in this experiment. We might also test different types of social proof such as testimonials instead of popularity call outs.
We can isolate these elements to get better direction for future tests.

experiments.title: How social proof and communal language impacted donor conversion rate in an email update
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using social proof and communal language for those receiving JEN emails will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because   people will identify with being a compassionate person and will want to join others who have donated.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/31/2023
CaringBridge sends out what are called JEN asks. These are auto-generated emails that are sent out to those connected to sites. They provide journal updates and always include an ask in the email. We wanted to test using social proof/communal language to let donors know that they’re joining a larger group of compassionate people when they give. The last test we ran for this experiment generated a decrease in gifts. We had tested using fear of loss language so our hypothesis is that CaringBridge’s audience may respond more positively to joining in on something rather than missing out on something.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Before going through results, it is worth noting that CaringBridge underwent a change in donation platform on the same day that this experiment was launched. Therefore, the results of this experiment were compromised and it should be launched again to confirm results.
The key learning from this experiment is that using social proof and communal language in JEN asks did not result in an increase in donor conversion rate for CaringBridge. In fact, the treatment resulted in a significant decrease in donations compared to the control. We saw a valid 58% decrease in donations resulting in a level of confidence of 99%.
This suggests that CaringBridge's audience may not respond well to communal language and instead, may require a different approach to encourage donations. However, given the change in an additional variable (the donation platform), it's recommended we retest this approach.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2023 - 05/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment for First Baptist Dallas is that creating ad copy specific to their existing contacts on Facebook can drive higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 20.9% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence of 97.4%. Additionally, Cost per Click experienced a 13.5% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and there was an increase in clicks from both mobile and tablet visitors.
Based on these findings, First Baptist Dallas should continue to personalize their ad copy for their existing contacts on Facebook and consider testing different variations of personalized copy in future experiments. They should also explore other ways to personalize their messaging to their audience, such as tailoring images or targeting specific demographics. By continuing to experiment and optimize their Facebook ad campaigns, First Baptist Dallas can maximize their conversion and engagement rates.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/02/2023
Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. We will compare the performance of these two ads to our original top-performing ad, which is a standard marketing ad with ad copy and an image.
The ads are sharing the same budget and are targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 56.6% increase in click-through rate with a 100% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 10.8% increase also with a 62.8% level of confidence. While click through rate is improved, other initial metrics including CPM and CPC worsened, and end-of-funnel metrics (donations and GCPD) also worsened compared to the control.
Spend - Control ($1,445), Treatment 1 ($297), Treatment 2 (70)
CPM - Control ($3.60), Treatment 1 ($9.20), Treatment 2 ($5.45)
CPC - Control ($0.59), Treatment 1 ($1.01), Treatment 2 ($0.80)
% Sign Up - Control (34.2%), Treatment 1 (61.5%) - 35.6% increase in email conversion with a 100 LoC, Treatment 2 (53.5%)
ICR - Control (6.51%), Treatment 1 (3.16%) - 51.4% decrease with a 95.4% LoC, Treatment 2 (0.00%)
GCPN - Control ($2.09, Treatment 1 ($1.88), Treatment 2 (1.83)
GCPD - Control ($-2), Treatment ($32), Treatment 2 (null)
ROI - Control (1.06), Treatment 1 (0.45), Treatment 2 (null)
Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. In this instance, the treatments resulted in higher CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs for the control were also better than the two treatments.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC and higher CPMs - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.
Image and likeness of ACI's Founder Dr. Ben Carson historically leads to high performance. The control ad for this experiment includes an image of Dr. Carson, which may contribute significant engagement at the beginning and throughout the funnel.

experiments.title: How an “Organic Status” Facebook Ad Affected Conversion Rate

rows.name: NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Control
NA | The People's Pharmacy Cookbook Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an “Organic Status” ad for Facebook users will achieve an increased conversion rate because the ad will stand out amongst other sponsored posts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/10/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. We decided to test if an organic looking facebook post used as an ad increased clicks and conversion rates compared to their traditional looking ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning in this experiment is that Facebook users are more likely to click on an ad if it seems like it is an organic post. Because the ad looks like an organic status post, it stands out to users, so they don't immediately assume it's an ad and scroll past it. Making the ad stand out more to users increased the number of clicks by 49.5% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence & decreased the Cost per Click by 19.6% with a 99.9% Level of Confidence as well. I believe this positive effect was also impacted by the fact that the treatment seemed like a personal recommendation versus just a promotion of a product. It is important to note that these ads ran exclusively in-feed on Facebook. All other ad placements were turned off.

It is also interesting to note that the older the audience, the higher the conversion rate. This could be due to the fact that an older user may not be able to tell as easily a a younger user that it is an ad and not an organic post making them more likely to click on it.

Moving forward, we should continue to test this type of "Organic Status" ad on various types on content offers that appeal to varying types and ages of audiences. We should also try applying the strategy of making an ad look more organic in other ways, such as a normal status post, and maybe try an organic looking Instagram & Facebook story post as well.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising. because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2023 - 04/28/2023
Another organization tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and saw a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate.
MSLF decided to test this concept on their Idaho Stockwater petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, they had one campaign with a typical-looking Facebook ad and a second campaign with just an image that looked like an organic post.
Each campaign had the same budget and was targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The organic post caused a decrease of 32.1% in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence. Although our click-through rate decreased, the organic post positively affected most of the downstream metrics:

Spend - Control ($413, Treatment ($409)

CPM - Control ($40.20), Treatment ($11.82)

CPC - Control ($1.15), Treatment ($0.50)

% Sign Up - Control (69.7%), Treatment (76.7%)

Emails - Control (251), Treatment (628) - 10% increase in email conversion with a 98.6% LoC

ICR - Control (2.39%), Treatment (1.27%) - 41.4% decrease in donor conversion with a 63.8% LoC

Donors - Control (6), Treatment (8)

GCPN - Control ($1.65), Treatment ($0.65)

GCPD - Control ($69), Treatment ($51)

ROI - Control (0.33), Treatment (0.38)

Because traffic was so much cheaper to get at the Facebook ad level (CPM and CPC), we saw our downstream metrics perform better due to the volume of users going through the funnel. Although our ICR decreased by 41.4%, it did not negatively affect our GCPD which was $18 cheaper for the organic ads.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/02/2023
Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. We will compare the performance of these two ads to our original top-performing ad, which is a standard marketing ad with ad copy and an image.
The ads are sharing the same budget and are targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 23.3% decrease in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 44.1% decrease also with a 99.9% level of confidence. Initial metrics and downstream metrics proved discouraging with the exception of petition sign up rate:
Spend - Control ($413, Treatment ($409)
CPM - Control ($10.88), Treatment 1 ($9.47), Treatment 2 ($5.65)
CPC - Control ($0.88), Treatment 1 ($1.16), Treatment 2 ($1.04)
% Sign Up - Control (45.9%), Treatment 1 (62.3%) - 35.6% increase in email conversion with a 100 LoC, Treatment 2 (59.4%)
ICR - Control (4.81%), Treatment 1 (3.18%), Treatment 2 (0.00%)
GCPN - Control ($1.93, Treatment 1 ($1.86), Treatment 2 (null)
GCPD - Control ($-2), Treatment ($31), Treatment 2 (null)
ROI - Control (1.04), Treatment 1 (0.46), Treatment 2 (null)
Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. However, in this instance, the treatments resulted in lower CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs did decrease with the treatments, though only marginally for treatment 1.
Notably, the sample size was out of whack. Next time we run this type of experiment, we should split the treatment(s) into a separate campaign and split the overall campaign budget between the control and treatment to ensure relatively equal spend. Still, the sample sizes were significant enough here to exceed the required sample size and reach a significant level of confidence.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC than the control and lower CTR - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.
Image and likeness of ACI's Founder Dr. Ben Carson historically leads to high performance. The control ad for this experiment includes a video message from Dr. Carson, which may contribute significant engagement at the beginning and throughout the funnel. Video historically produces higher CPM and lower CPC, but in this case, CPC remained lower than the treatments and donor conversion metrics for the control vastly outperformed the treatments

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad radical redesign impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by offering to pray for Facebook viewers will achieve higher click through rate because it is a tangible thing that people may be looking for .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas involved testing two different types of Facebook ads: one that offered to pray for viewers and another that advertised the message and sermon series. The hypothesis was that the ad offering prayer would achieve a higher click-through rate. However, the experiment's results showed a 15.1% decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group compared to the control group.
The key learning from this experiment is that offering to pray for viewers may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates for Facebook ads. However, we saw increased prayer requests received and interaction with the iCampus site. So while the Prayer ad had a lower click conversion rate, we did see greater engagement through it.

experiments.title: How introducing a felt need affects clickthrough rate on site start remarketing ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing the “why” for potential site creators will achieve an increase in click thru rate  because we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution. . because  we are addressing their personal needs and providing our services as the solution.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/14/2023
CaringBridge was running remarketing ads in Facebook in order to recapture traffic that may have been interested to start a new site with CaringBridge. In an effort to increase click thru rate we proposed 3 different ad variations with 3 different motivations. We wanted to see which of these motivations would resonate more with their audiences.
The first treatment highlighted the privacy that a CaringBridge site offers. The second treatment addresses the felt need for the individual and how a CaringBridge site keeps you connected to others. The third treatment emphasizes how you can journal your progress on the site to continue getting support with others.
All three ads ran congruently on Facebook with the original remarketing ad which focused more on efficiency.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The winning variant for this experiment was treatment #2 with a valid 31.8% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 95.1% level of confidence. The other two ads had almost no difference in click thru rates. This informs us that treatment #2 more effectively connected with the reader's felt need with the first sentence of the ad being: "The moments you need help most are precisely when its hardest to ask for it." This is a sentence that introduces empathy and gets to the heart of why someone needs support in their health journey. In fact the first treatment may have introduced a level of anxiety because of the mention of publicly sharing their personal life.
It's recommended that we test similar language on the landing page itself in order to increase new site creations after people click through so that the messaging of their journey is consistent.

experiments.title: How your Facebook (Meta) Advertising performance goal impacts video campaign reach
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Ad recall lift” goal in Facebook (Meta) Advertising for our awareness campaign will achieve a more cost-effective reach  because the algorithm is showing it to whoever it believes will likely “remember” seeing the ad.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 03/15/2023
As a part of NextAfter’s “Fundraising Genius” branding ad campaign, our team wanted to experiment with the video awareness campaign goals to monitor what performance goal would achieve the greatest “reach” (which is defined as: how many people in the target audience received at least one ad impression) with the campaign.
So, our team decided to try the “Maximize number of impressions” goal (as the control) vs. a new awareness building campaign goal called “Maximize ad recall lift” (as the treatment).
Otherwise, the two campaigns were identical, targeting the same audience.
Our research questions really were:
Which campaign setting would generate the greatest reach, and to what amount are we trading off “frequency” of ad placements (eg how many times anyone in the audience sees an ad per day)? And,
Which of the two campaign types is more cost effective for the investment?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Maximize ad recall lift" goal in Facebook advertising for awareness campaigns is more effective than using the "Maximize number of impressions" goal because it reached nearly 2x the number of people on just 12.8% of the spending of the control audience.
This was confirmed by a 91.3% increase in "Reach" (how many unique people in our target audience received at least one impression) with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.
To put that another way — the cost to reach 1,000 people in the control ad setting was $34.84, while the cost to reach 1,000 people with the treatment ad setting was just $2.68 (which is is a 92.3% decrease in cost to reach 1,000 people).
Having said all of that — it's worth noting that our frequency has plummeted, dropping from 12.4 in the control campaign to just 1.3 in the treatment.
Conclusion:
If you're looking to reach far more people in your target audience with at least one ad impression, prioritized to whomever the platform believes is most likely to consume the ad and remember it, you should use the "Awareness > Maximize ad recall lift" campaign setting.If you just want to overpower your competitors and ensure high ad frequency for a smaller number of your target audience, then you should use the "Awareness > Maximize the number of impressions" campaign setting.

First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each Sunday morning First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share live now ads to invite Facebook ad viewers to join the church on their online campus named iCampus.
Over the last few weeks, Facebook’s ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to do a radical experiment by testing the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series vs asking if they have a prayer need.

The A/B experiment conducted by First Baptist Dallas showed that the traditional ad with copy about the message and sermon series outperformed the ad that asked for prayer requests in terms of clicks for all traffic. However, the results did not reach statistical significance due to a lack of minimum sample size.
The key learning from this experiment is that addressing the felt need of the viewer may not necessarily result in higher ad clicks. It is important to continue testing and experimenting with different ad copy and messaging to determine what resonates best with the target audience. Future experimentation should also focus on achieving a larger sample size to increase statistical significance in the results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as timing and targeting in the ads to further improve performance.

rows.name: PTV Kids Book shop without prices

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including the price of a book for Facebook users will achieve a higher click conversion because the user knows the cost upfront.

Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
PTV decided to try out a new revenue stream by promoting items from their store for a set price rather than a generous gift. With this new revenue stream, we decided to test on the Facebook ad if showing the price of the item would increase click conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to determine if showing the price on a Facebook ad for a book would increase click conversion rate. However, results showed the control version had a 40.8% higher click conversion rate than the treatment. Complete Registrations, Cost per Click, and Cost per Registration also experienced decreases, while visitor groups, including 65+, mobile, tablet, desktop, female, and unknown, had significant decreases in clicks.
The key learning from this experiment is that including price on a Facebook ad did not increase click conversion rate but instead decreased it. In future experimentation, it may be worth testing other variables on Facebook ads, such as language, design, or audience targeting to increase click conversion rates. It is also essential to determine the target audience and tailor the ad accordingly. Overall, experimentation is a valuable tool for discovering what works best for promoting revenue streams in Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How images and video of a personality impacted clicks on an eBook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images and videos of a well-known figure for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because this introduces more credibility and authority than using an eBook image alone.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
When running ads for an eBook offer, we wondered if the cover of the eBook was appealing enough to drive interest from Facebook ad viewers. In previous testing on other offers, we had found success in using the organization’s founder’s name and photo. We wondered if we could achieve similar success by featuring images and videos in the ads that were relevant to the eBook’s contents. The first treatment had a background of an American flag to serve as a “trigger image” that can help with familiarity as users scroll. The second treatment was a 30-second video quoting Martin Luther King, Jr. and the third treatment was a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies. We launched all versions of the ad and let them run for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we saw valid decreases in clickthrough rate for the first and second treatments, the third treatment that included a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies achieved a 44.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. We believe this video saw a lift in engagement and effectiveness due to the relevancy of the messaging that gave a sneak peek into how the topic of the eBook was discussed. The video that produced the lift also had an enhanced sense of urgency compared to the other treatments that could have positively impacted performance. Future experimentation should focus on incorporating relevant and credible images and videos in the ad campaigns, accompanied by concise and compelling messages that resonate with the target audience.

experiments.summary:

In a previous test on this offer, we tested short copy vs. longer copy and found that the longer copy version motivated more visitors to click the ad. In this next iteration, we wanted to test back into using shorter copy but installing the learning from the previous experiment which was that visitors needed clarity about the pledge they would be signing before they would commit to taking the next step. So this time, we kept the outline of the four pillars they would be agreeing to in the pledge in the copy, but removed all the extra lead-in copy. The treatment also introduced checkbox emojis to indicate each statement of the pledge they would be agreeing with if they signed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that concise communication of the call to action in Facebook ads significantly increases clickthrough rates. By removing unnecessary lead-in copy and emphasizing the four pillars of the pledge with checkbox emojis, the treatment achieved a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This suggests that visitors respond well to clear, concise messaging that ensures congruence with the landing page. In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of concise messaging and strong calls to action that align with the landing page content.

experiments.title: How urgency language impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which element of urgency will drive response limited time or few left in stock?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Each year PTV releases a new daily devotional book with 260 days of encouragement and teachings.
To boost response in the final days of the devotional being offered we decided to test whether an urgency of time or an urgency of exclusivity would drive results on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment provided key learnings around the use of urgency to drive response on a Facebook campaign. The results showed that creating a sense of exclusivity, or creating an urgency of few left in stock, yielded 30.2 % more clicks compared to the control which only used an urgency of time. This illustrates the value of using scarcity when crafting a message on a digital campaign. Going forward, creating a sense of exclusivity, combined with a sense of limited time can be used across all types of marketing campaigns to further drive response. If a campaign is not time-sensitive, encouraging users to act quickly due to limited availability can be an effective way to increase response rates.
In addition to driving a click response, we saw a significant impact on donations. The few left in stock generated a 49.6% increase in donor conversions with an 87.1% level of confidence (13 to 75 donations)!
For revenue, we saw a 22.6% increase with a 70.9% level of confidence.
While donations and revenue did not yield confidence levels over the required 95%, we did see directional lifts in a positive direction.

experiments.title: How adding clarity in a Facebook ad impacted email conversions on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in the ad copy for Facebook audiences will achieve an increase in pledge signatures.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/19/2022
During calendar year-end, we sent an offer to the housefile asking them to commit to ACI’s Four Pillars Pledge. We saw strong results from the email subscribers so we took the offer to Facebook for additional conversion opportunities for existing leads and prospecting to acquire new names. The email version included a preview of all four belief statements they would be asked to sign on the landing page, so we wanted to test including this in the Facebook ad as well. We ran this against the control, which kept some mystery to intrigue the ad viewer to sign the pledge.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the short ad and the long ad side by side for two weeks was the long treatment version increased email conversions by 15.2% with 99.% level of confidence. We believe this increase is due to giving the reader the option to review the values they would be affirming by signing the Four Pillars Pledge, so they were already primed to say "Yes" and agree to these same items when they were presented on the landing page. Not only did this improve our email conversion rate, but we also saw a 14% lift on donations downstream with 53% level of confidence. Additional benefits were that the treatment ad reduced cost per name by 21.5% and reduced net cost per donor by 44.8%. Going forward, we will run the long version of the ad as the new control and develop a new hypothesis to run against it to see if we can continue to improve performance.

experiments.title: How more focused offers increase your lead quality in acquisition campaigns

rows.name: Facebook Lead Ads w/Facebook Ad Template Offer
Facebook Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers
LinkedIn Lead Ads w/better qualifying offers

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using offers that do a better job of prequalifying my audience for acquisition campaigns will achieve a higher number of “qualified leads” (and a lower cost per qualified lead) because we believe the offer itself plays an important role in pre-qualifying the audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2023 - 01/31/2023
As a part of recent experimentation with using Facebook Lead Ad forms in our own acquisition efforts, we observed a substantial decrease in the cost per lead, without decreasing the disengagement and unsubscribe rates of the leads generated.
But, with this much higher volume, we received a substantial number of personal email addresses, which made it difficult to understand whether or not we were actually generating leads from our prime audience (fundraising professionals and/or marketers at nonprofit organizations that raise a certain number of dollars per year).
Our hypothesis was that the offer used in December of 2022 and previous experiments could have more “generic appeal” to any marketer (not just those who work at nonprofit organizations).
We also wondered whether Facebook advertising is the best platform for acquiring leads in general, as well as qualified leads tied to qualified nonprofit organizations.
Therefore, we ran a month-long experiment testing two offers that are more specific to nonprofit organizations across both Facebook (using the lead ads conversion type), while also trying those same (more specific) offers on LinkedIn advertising (using their lead ad equivalent).
Our goal was to compare the results acquired during this experiment time period against the results collected during the previous month’s experiment with Facebook Lead Ads with an offer that would (theoretically) have more generic appeal.
We had four metrics in mind to determine success of this treatment type, which are:
Cost per lead (of any type)
% of leads generated that have a “generic domain” (something like: “@gmail.com” as opposed to a work email address)
% of leads generated that are tied to a “qualified nonprofit organization”
Cost per qualified lead generated
experiments.keyLearnings:
As it turns out, the offers used to better qualify the audience indeed increased the number of qualified leads generated through our acquisition efforts during the duration of this experiment.
In fact, both platforms substantially increased the number of qualified leads generated for our advertising dollar spent. But, the question really is: "By how much?"
We observed that the "better quality offer" treatments across both platforms decreased the cost per qualified lead in the following way:
Facebook: -82.9%LinkedIn: -19.8%
Key Takeaway: So, not only did we increased the volume of qualified leads, we did so by dropping the cost per qualified lead by significant amounts.
Cost per lead trends compared in this way:
Facebook: -48.4%Linked: +1,182%
Key Takeaway: While running the "better quality offers" on Facebook, we actually decreased our cost per lead overall, but the cost per lead (of any type) generated on LinkedIn increased substantially. Therefore, at least in the way the LinkedIn ad campaigns were set up during this experiment period of time, we would not suggest shifting advertising budgets toward LinkedIn to replace Facebook lead generation campaigns.
Finally, our last measurement was to review the % of leads generated with a "generic email domain" — here is how the treatments performed against the control campaigns:
Facebook: -23.9%LinkedIn: -12.9%
Key Takeaway: The "better quality offers" indeed decreased the volume of leads generated with a "generic email domain," as well.
Summary of Findings:
There are actually three key takeaways that we have from this experiment, which are:
The relevance of your offer to your ideal customer/audience is a key factor for increasing both your campaign's response rates overall, but also can attract better quality leads, as well. If your campaigns aren't specific enough to your ideal audience, consider developing new, more relevant offers that can prequalify the audience at the ad impression level.Facebook Lead Ads continues to perform well, as we observed improvements in all success metrics using that tactic. LinkedIn Ads cost (at least in the campaign settings we used) are tremendously more expensive when compared to Facebook Lead Ads, and although we observed higher rates in which that platform produced "better qualified leads," the cost to produce any lead at all on that platform leads us to conclude that we should not shift large amounts of our advertising budget to that platform for net-new lead acquisition efforts.

experiments.title: How using an identity resolution service impacts your first-party record match rates within Facebook (Meta)
experiments.researchQuestion: Would we match our first-party contact list at a higher rate when using LiveRamp’s “identity resolution” technology once it was pushed into Facebook (Meta) Audiences?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2023
LiveRamp is a data enablement platform, that claims to take first-party contact data points provided to them and match those contacts within their platform using a process called “identity resolution” to match your contacts within advertising platforms in a more reliable and consistent way, such that the “match rate” increases dramatically.
Since iOS 14.5, advertising platforms like Facebook (Meta) have had an more difficult time matching uploaded contact records, as well as less visibility into online behavioral tracking data, and also online purchase behavior.
With lower match rates and less information flowing back to the algorithm to learn and refine lookalike models, some clients have experienced less return on ad spend (ROAS).
With all of this in mind, we decided to experiment with what the match rates on Facebook (Meta) would be by uploading contacts via the HubSpot CRM integration, when compared to uploading them to Facebook (Meta) via LiveRamp.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" upload process into Facebook (Meta) Ads Manager significantly improved our first-party contact match rate within the advertising platform — matching +94.9% more of the list that we uploaded.
Now that we know that the LiveRamp "identity resolution" service does indeed match your first-party contacts in a much better way, the real question is:
Do the lookalike audiences modeled from this LiveRamp list produce a more reliable lookalike modeled audience to use for acquisition campaigns?
It stands to reason that this could be the case. Why?
Because if our source list for lookalike modeling uses nearly 2x more contacts, then it would be reasonable to assume that the model it produces is far richer and the prospecting audience it produces is using twice as much data to model prospecting audiences to market to with your acquisition campaigns.
Further experimentation is required, specifically as it pertains to nonprofit organizations who are uploading donor data.
In fact, we're in the process of launching a handful of experiments on this very topic, and will share our findings when they are available.
