experiments.title: How segmenting audiences vs donor audience signal affected donor conversion rate on YouTube
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that segmenting audiences for YouTube will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2023
Seeing YouTube as the next opportunity of paid media channel expansion, we wanted to see the difference in performance between giving Google all of the data and allowing the algorithm to optimize the audience versus segmenting our audiences by stages of awareness.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment highlights the importance of segmenting audiences in order to achieve the best possible results from YouTube advertising.
This showed that segmenting audiences based on factors such as YouTube video views, Facebook web visits, and donor status resulted in significantly higher conversion rates compared to the control.
In particular, the BOF - Donors group saw a conversion rate that was more than 30 times higher than the control group, and the MOF - FB Web Visitors group saw a conversion rate that was more than 7 times higher. These results suggest that by targeting specific groups of users, it is possible to achieve much higher conversion rates and to more effectively drive donations.

experiments.title: How Facebook ad imagery impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which type of Facebook ad image will have the highest clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/23/2017 - 12/15/2017
Illinois Policy Institute recently discovered that showing a cartoon image in a Facebook ad decreased clickthrough rate. They decided to create two new treatments based to test against the control. The first treatment included an image of Michael Madigan–the state legislator pushing for an increase in taxes. The second treatment included an image of a house, to align with the subject of property taxes. They wondered: Which Facebook ad approach will have the highest clickthrough rate? They launched a 3-part test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Illinois Policy Institute found that the first treatment using Madigan’s image increased clickthrough rate by 70%, while the second treatment using the house image actually decreased ad clickthrough rate by 23%. This experiment is a good example of how an active A/B testing approach can lead to incremental performance when done consistently. Despite experiencing a drop in the second treatment, the learning in the first treatment outweighed the impact and will now be implemented as the winner.

It’s apparent that the use of Madigan’s image increased audience motivation. Many Illinoisans are familiar with this particular legislator. When connected with the topic of a potential property tax hike and their ability to take action to stop it, response rate went up significantly.

experiments.title: How Facebook Lead Ads impacted email signup rates and lead quality

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type for lead campaigns targeting net-new prospect audiences will achieve an increase lead volume at a lower cost.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/06/2022
Since the iOS 14.5 software update (among other things) we have noticed a continuous increase in the cost to acquire a new subscriber within our acquisition campaigns here at NextAfter from Facebook advertising.
In an effort to continuously test ways to decrease the cost per lead metric for our own advertising and marketing campaigns, we wanted to experiment with trying the Facebook Lead Ad campaign type.
In previous tests (many years ago), we observed that the quality of the leads generated was far inferior to those collected from the more “recommended” experience of promoting ad clicks to a hosted landing page, making the case as to why someone should download the offered resource.
As a part of this experiment, we used the same resource as the offer, locking the creative used within the ad, targeting the same audience, but the treatment used a “Facebook Lead Ad” conversion point, as opposed to taking ad clicks from Facebook itself to our hosted landing page to secure the email address.
We were curious as to whether this treatment could not only lower the cost per new subscriber but also whether or not the “lead quality” would be negatively impacted, as well.
It’s worth noting that (at least for us) a quality lead is someone who we can tell (either through the information provided directly, or through a quick Google search for those leads) whether or not they were confirmed to work for a nonprofit organization, at least in the marketing or fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99% level of confidence, we observed an increase in email signup rates of +131.5% through the Facebook Lead Ad treatment campaign experience.
We also observed:
A valid increase in ad clickthrough rate by +24.3% (LoC: 99.1%), andThat the CPM (cost per one thousand impressions) also decreased by -35.8%, as well.
Overall, this treatment experience decreased our cost per lead by a whopping -72.3%!
But, the real question is: Did we just create a bunch of "junk leads" by using the Facebook Lead Ad campaign?
To determine this, we researched the lead using the data provided on the form submission to see whether or not the person requesting the resource was working at a charitable organization within the marketing or development/fundraising groups, or as a decision-maker within the organization's executive team.
Of the leads generated by experience type, here is what we found:
61.6% of the Control experience leads were determined to be "qualified."46.2% of the Treatment experience leads were determined to be "qualified."
So, the claim that the Facebook Lead Ads would produce a lower quality is true — but, from a certain point of view.
The truth of the matter is that although we generated a lower percentage of "qualified leads" from the overall amount of leads generated, the cost per "qualified lead" was actually decreased by -63% when compared to the control experience.
In fact, the cost per qualified lead from the Treatment experience was even -40% less expensive when compared to the total cost per lead from the Control experience.
There are two more observations that we made, as well...
When someone completes the Facebook Lead Ad form, the form itself offers marketers with the opportunity to offer one more thing to the person who just submitted the form. It's worth noting that when we promoted a site-hosted webinar recording that was relevant to the offer used within the experiment, we observed that 64% of the leads generated in the Treatment experience also chose to click the button (to go to our website/watch the webinar offered). And,Facebook Ads Manager's web interface is notoriously bad at properly tracking/reporting accurately under the Control experience type of campaign, however, we noted that the Treatment version of the campaign had 100% accuracy in reporting leads generated, cost per lead, etc.
When it's all said and done — here is what we found:
Facebook Lead Ads will very likely decrease your cost per lead/subscriber.But, as you scale up the number of leads, you'll lose a bit on the percentage of those leads being of "high quality."Regardless of that fact, it's worth using this approach because you get so many more leads (and an overall higher volume of "qualified leads" as a part of the higher total of leads overall) with this approach.
So, what comes next? We'll plan to circle back to the leads generated in this study and look at two more data points downstream, which are:
The percentage of leads who opt-out by the end of the new subscriber welcome series. And also,The percentage of leads who opt-out, are disengaged, and/or who have never opened a single email 60-days after securing the lead.
More on those studies soon, but for now — it's advised that you consider using Facebook Lead Ads in this post-iOS 14.5 world of continuously decreasing performance in Facebook Advertising to drive net-new subscriber growth for your organization and marketing campaigns.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition in a Facebook ad impacts conversion and cost.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would help increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2018 - 02/02/2018
Care Net offers a curriculum for churches to start a pro-life ministry called Making Life Disciples. To get more people to sign up for the curriculum, they pushed the offer through paid Facebook ads. They developed a control that had a quote on it about God being able to make abortion unthinkable through the church. They hypothesized that they might be able to motivate more people to convert if they gave more context in the ad through more value proposition copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment significantly increased conversion by 859%. This tells us that when we provide more context, communicate the value of the offer, and have a call-to-action in the text copy of our ads, we can increase conversion. The expanded value proposition not only got more people to click through to the landing page, but these people were much more highly motivated to convert. This treatment increased conversion, but even more, it decreased cost per conversion substantially from $32/conversion to $5.30/conversion.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 3)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the housefile audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/04/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Treatment 1 had a lift of 146.9% compared to the control. This confirms our hypothesis that this audience responds stronger to the learning and results rather than the speaker. Although, it is possible that the results could be different if the speaker was someone they were more familiar with.

Comparing this result to the result of our previous test on the housefile lookalike audience reveals a drastic difference in the motivations of two audiences that are supposedly similar. The lookalikes were 110% more likely to click on the empathy treatment, while the housefile was 26% less likely to click on the empathy treatment.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects the click rate on a Facebook Ad

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/10/2016
At the launch of our value proposition research study, Why Should I Give to You?, we ran Facebook across our typical Facebook audiences. We wanted to determine what components of the value proposition resonated most with different audience groups early on.
The control focused on the depth of the study, treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition, and treatment 2 focused on the result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that with our NPO Professionals target audience, the control that focused on the depth of the study resonated the most and drove the most clicks. Future tests will look at different variations of smaller variables within the control ad to perpetually optimize the click rate.

experiments.title: How removing transactional language on a free trial offer impacted ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing transactional language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in website visits.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/05/2021
When reviewing ads for a free trial offer, we wondered if the language “no credit card needed” was a motivating factor for people to sign up or if it was distancing potential visitors by creating a more transactional mindset and reducing the appeal and overall value of the offer. We wanted to test removing the “no credit card needed” language to see how it impacted clicks on the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we thought that removing transactional language would come across as a more humanized approach and personal in nature, what we found was that removing the "no credit card needed" language caused a 25.9% decrease in traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence. What this data shows us is this language actually decreases anxiety and removes friction from the process, making it a lower perceived amount of effort to accept the offer because people are not worried about having to remember to cancel their free trial at a later date to avoid being charged any membership fees. Because we achieved valid results, we were able to take this learning from a Facebook ad and apply it to other areas and entry points where a free trial is offered on the website and add in this language to increase conversion rates.

This is due to the trust and expertise associated with the founders of FamilyLife, as well as an additional tie-in with the copy that people connect with and believe in. The copy is also signed by Dennis, which creates a more personal connection with the reader.

experiments.title: How making an end-user click “See More” in a Facebook ad impacts click-through to the acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad would increase click-through and ultimately name acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2017 - 09/07/2017
Care Net offers a free online course called Pro-Life 101. They use paid Facebook ads to push the offer. Through previous testing, we had learned that longer Facebook ad copy increases name acquisition. To build upon those learnings, we tested ad copy that was even longer resulting in the end-user having to click “See More” to read the entirety of it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version with the extended copy increased click-through by 82%! What this tell us is that people are not only more highly engaged with long ad copy, but even longer ad copy requiring people to click “See More” increases engagement and results in more people clicking through to the landing page and converting. The “See More” version provided additional information that the end-user needed to make the decision to click-through to the landing page and ultimately sign-up for the course.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a download now button for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2022 - 12/31/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
During the holiday season in 2021 we ran a test where we changed the CTA button on a Facebook ad promoting a specific ebook from ‘learn more’ to ‘download now.’ Our hypothesis was since we were asking people to download the ebook on the landing page by providing their email address, using a ‘download now’ CTA button would reduce the friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: By simply changing the CTA button to download now, Hoover had a 48.3% increase in email acquisition rates, with a 99.9% level of confidence. Tying a download of an ebook into the button copy made a significant impact  for Hoover. This is a test we should continue running on multiple offers.

experiments.title: How a survey question in a Facebook Ad impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Our hypothesis is that by using a survey-style ad (with a “Yes/No” question included in the ad image) will increase clicks on the ad so much, that we can generate more emails at a more efficient cost.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2022
As a part of our own marketing and prospecting via Facebook, we decided to try a new survey-style ad image to see if we could generate a much higher volume of ad clicks, and subsequently from that extra traffic, convert more people on the subsequent landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 100%, we observed a decrease in email signups by -57.8%.

Our hypothesis completely failed with our audiences on Facebook Advertising, because this decrease in email signups was actually driven by fewer clicks overall (we saw that this treatment ad decreased ad clicks by -53.6%, also with a confidence level of 100%).

Ultimately, we'd like to re-try this experiment, but next time with a different question in the treatment ad. With evidence that these style of ads work well in other cases, the thought here is that perhaps the question is too vague. Furthermore, could the length of the question also be impacting ad clickthrough rates?

Would asking a more simple question (like: "Do you want to get more from your Facebook advertising?") get straight to the point, and resonate with a larger portion of the audience? It's worth testing, so we will soon.

experiments.title: How more contextualized ad media affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using more contextualized imagery on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by adding clarity and appeal to an offer in order to increase its perceived value.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we wanted to know if more contextualized ad media would lead to higher click-through rates than a simple 3D render of the ebook associated with the offer. To test this concept, we created new ad media which placed the eBook (dealing with critical race theory) over an image of children in a classroom while also adding some value prop copy that aimed to demonstrate WHAT the viewer would learn by signing up for the ebook.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran the test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 27.5% DROP in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence. When analyzing this experiment, we identified a potential reason for these results:

Decreasing the size of the ebook in the treatment image, along with placing it over a busy background may have created friction for the viewer by making it unclear what they should do.

This may also have reduced the visual impact of showing what is being offered (the ebook), prompting viewers who may have otherwise been enticed by this offer to keep scrolling, not realizing a free ebook was being offered

The image may also have failed to make the intended impact because it is dark in tone, with heavy shadows and much of the subject matter obscured by the ebook, potentially making it difficult for the viewer to quickly discern what the image is trying to convey.

Overall, we believe that these factors combine to make the treatment ad rather "invisible" to the viewer, failing to do enough to catch the reader's attention, get them to stop scrolling, and click through to the offer.

experiments.title: How replacing a static image with a video on a Facebook ad affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting an ad in video format will increase click-through rates for Facebook users by better catching their attention and better communicating the value one receives by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/18/2022 - 10/18/2022
Many of Dallas Theological Seminary’s Facebook ads for their free online courses are similar, using a color-rich background including the names of the courses and the teachers who present them. In an effort to combat low click through rates, we hypothesized that although viewers were seeing ads for different courses, because these ads looked so similar, it was causing viewers to assume they were seeing an ad for the same course time and time again, eventually prompting them to tune the ads out.
We also wondered if the static ads were inadequately communicating the value one could receive by enrolling in one of Dallas Theological Seminary’s free online courses. We wanted to know if we could increase click-through rates by presenting a video ad that would 1) Catch viewers attention and 2) Better communicate the value of registration.
For this experiment, we changed only the ad media, splitting traffic between he control and treatment over a span of 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 93% LIFT in click-through rates, with a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that a more interactive media format would result in more viewers clicking the ad. We believe that this treatment was successful for two primary reasons:
First, we believe that the new media format broke up the monotony of similarly formatted ads being shown to the same people. A different ad from a familiar organization, we believe, cued viewers that a new offer was being advertised.
Second, we believe that the video allowed viewers to see for themselves the quality of the coursework and the expertise of the teacher (these courses are adapted from on-campus seminary lectures) which increased the perceived value of the offer by improving its credibility and exclusivity, which likely provided more incentive for the viewer to click.
Moving forward, we will attempt to gather more video samples that we can use to promote more courses in their catalog.

experiments.title: How video ads affect email acquisition rate for a free course
experiments.researchQuestion: Will video ads produce an increase in email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2017 - 03/03/2017
DTS was promoting their new course, called Can You Trust the Bible?. They had a special video from their Academic Dean to promote it and wanted to test using this video to promote the course. Video ads had shown in some experiments to improve conversion for offers, although DTS had previously used static ads.
They split audiences and tested each ad unit to see which resulted in a better cost-per-acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video ads produced a 37% decrease in conversion when tested against the static ads. While a deeper look at the data showed that many more people engaged with the video ads (which means they clicked somewhere on the ad), the video ad was either too distracting or not compelling enough to convince them to take the next step and sign up for the course. This seems to indicate that, in this case, video is more of a distraction than a motivator to take the course.
This doesn’t entirely rule out video ads on Facebook. There have been experiments where video ads produce a lift, and they can be effective at remarketing. DTS will continue to test video as a promotional tool for their free courses.

experiments.title: How advertising imagery affected the clarity of the value proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will produce the highest motivated visitors resulting in the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 08/31/2016
The ads that focused on the images of children had nearly double the email acquisition rate as the ad that just focused on the guide. Our hypothesis is that the images of children served as a reminder of why the prayers were needed whereas the guide was just what would be given. By answering the “why” first, the visitors were more motivated to download the devotional.
The visual aspect of black and white versus color did not seem to make any significant difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As part of their Shoes for Orphans Souls campaign, Buckner International created a 7-day digital devotional. The e-book guided individuals in prayer as a means to lift up orphans and other vulnerable children.

As part of the advertising for this campaign, we created Facebook ads to drive visitors to a conversion-focused email acquisition page. For similar organizations, past experiments had proven that showing a 3D version of the guide would produce the best response but with the topic of orphans and vulnerable children, we had a hypothesis that images of kids would provoke a better response. We created multiple versions of this ad and tested it across multiple audiences

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in a Facebook ad affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the clarity of the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2017 - 01/23/2017
The treatment with the dark text and the book on the left produced a 95.6% increase in clickthrough rate as compared to the control, beating the other three treatments (which also produced lifts).
This experiment shows the importance of clarity in the ad, even at the initial point of contact. The high contrast in the text makes it jump out, especially in Facebook where the majority of the platform is white. Also, leading with the book image draws the reader’s eye into the text, rather than the other way around.
This experiment will help future ad campaigns begin with more optimized design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International was offering a free eBook by their president, Albert Reyes, called “The Jesus Agenda”. They hypothesized that their control ad, with a photo of Jesus, might lack clarity in the prospect-level value proposition, since it didn’t show the viewer what they were getting.

They drafted three new treatments: one with the eBook and dark text, another with the eBook and lighter text, and one with the eBook right-aligned and the text on the left. They launched a four-way test to determine which one produced the highest clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How image clarity and ask intensity affected clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Does an image of the 3D eBook in the ad produce more clicks than an image of children? How do people react to a soft ask vs. a direct ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2017 - 05/03/2017
The Buckner team realized that users were 40% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings: Buckner International was promoting a new free ebook to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through to the landing page from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

experiments.title: How focusing ad copy and creative on relatable messaging impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that more communal, digestable, and relatable content for ad engagers will achieve an increase in click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/10/2022
TGC is offering its free Secular Creed eBook in acquisition. The Facebook ad promoting the offer was receiving a low click-through rate,  so we wanted to test two new ad variants to see if they would see in increase clicks. We wondered if the existing ad is difficult to process, as it uses a lot of big words, the messaging could be confusing, and overall, it does not do a good enough job of considering the audience.
Treatment #1 took a new direction with the ad copy and the ad image. The copy begins with a more engaging, personal question that asks for their personal opinion instead of asking if they want to learn something. Overall, it uses communal language (“we” and “us”) instead of general language to get the message across that we share a particular outlook. The image is just the ebook as opposed to the ebook with a long quote to follow.
Treatment #2 uses the same copy as treatment #1, but implements video creative instead of a static image. The video is a two-minute motivational excerpt from a speech made by the author on topic with the value prop in the ad copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was little difference between the results of the control and treatment 1, with treatment 1 experiencing a 5.9% decrease in clicks with only a 54.6% level of confidence. However, treatment 2 resulted in a 142.5% increase in clicks with a 100% level of confidence.
There was little statistical difference between the control copy and the treatment copy, which means the video creative in treatment 2 made all the difference. Strategically selecting a 2-minute excerpt from the author of the eBook that aligned with the value prop and put the problem and solution into perspective motivated people to click the ad.
Given the success of this test, it would be wise to, when applicable, leverage eBook authors and leverage video of them that can reach people on a deeper level.

experiments.summary:

The Buckner team realized that users were 120% more likely to respond to an image including the 3D eBook than one that does not. This stems from the clarity and directness of the image’s alignment with the ad- people know exactly what they’re getting when they click the ad. Copy 2 was also more effective because they’re not asking them to commit to something at the ad level, as in Copy 1. The direct ask in Copy 1 is better suited for people who have already clicked through to the landing page. The softer ask in Copy 2 (asking them to learn more) paired with a direct image led to a 40% more successful click through rate and a 120% increase in email acquisition.
They also learned that placing a strong statement (“The foster system is broken”) that people will either agree/disagree with at the beginning of the ad leads to more qualified traffic (and a more likely converter). If the user agrees with the initial statement, then sees the opportunity to learn more paired with a very clear image of what they’re going to receive if they click the link, they’re more likely to continue to the landing page. If they disagree, they’re immediately diverted.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International was promoting a new devotional to grow their email file targeted towards people interested in the foster care system. They wanted to figure out what made people click through from the ad and download the guide. They created four different treatments to test this- 2 images paired with 2 different approaches to copy. One of the images is a photo of the 3D eBook, and the other image is a photo of children. The copy differences hinge on a direct vs. soft ask: “commit to prayer” vs. “learn more about the opportunity”.

In their first test on this ad, treatment number 3 (a photo of the eBook with a soft ask) led to a 40% increase in clickthrough. Their next test was to find out if this increase in clickthrough ultimately led to an increase in conversions.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image for ad visitors would increase click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/09/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition was experiencing a low click-through rate, so we tested a few different ad angles to see if they would increase clicks. We wondered if grabbing the reader’s attention right off the bat with a statement that they could “get behind” and clearly understand what was at stake would increase click-through rate. We also wondered if the existing ad image was experiencing user fatigue, and therefore tested two new images to see how they impact click-through rate. We ran an a/b/c test. The copy for the “b” and “c” variants differs from the “a” variant only in the opening sentence of the copy.
The “b” variant has a different image than “a”, using an image with symbols that represent Texas and that almost any Texan would recognize. The “c” variant has a different image as well, showing a person riding a horse in open Texas land – an image that we hypothesized Texans would relate to and distinguish themselves through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatment 1 and 2 led to an increase in ad clicks. Treatment 1 saw a 38.9% lift in clicks with a 96.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 83.4% lift in clicks with 99.9% level of confidence. This tells us that users did appreciate the clarity in the opening line of the ad copy, as they learned immediately what the ad was asking of them (to sign a petition) and what the petition was for (to keep Texas Texan). Also, by pointing at this this petition was "critical", users likely felt more of a sense of urgency to click the ad to reach the critical petition. As these variables were not isolated, we don't know for sure whether the clarity or urgency factored more into their decision to click through to the landing page.

Ultimately, treatment 2 experienced the biggest life of the three variants. This tells us that the ad image in treatment 2 resonated the most with users. They're asked to complete a petition to "keep Texas Texan", and the image of a man riding a horse through an open field on a dirt path rightly represents Texas. Users were more likely to click an ad image that offered authenticity than an image that was more "cartoony". That's because users see Texas and Texans as "authentic".

This ad approach is worth testing with other ads and for other offers. The results and learning would be clearer, however, if the ad variants are isolated. We don't know for sure if users were more motivated to click because of the copy change or the image change.

experiments.title: How previewing a pledge affects click-through rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a pledge on a Facebook ad will increase click-through rates by engaging the viewer and prompting them to click/tap to make their selection.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/18/2022
After turning off some underperforming Facebook ads, we wanted to introduce a new treatment to test against our previous champion. For this treatment, we chose a concept that has shown success in the past: previewing a choice from the offered asset in order to entice the viewer to engage and click through to the asset itself. For this experiment, we changed only the ad creative and ran this test for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 22.7% drop in click-through-rates with a 99.9% level of confidence. When analyzing these results, we identified a couple of factors that may have contributed to this decline.
First, while such a treatment has worked well for survey offers in the past, we feel that this approach may not be as effective for a pledge because we are not "asking" them to complete any action, i.e. when previewing a survey question, the preview "asks" the viewer a question ... but in the context of a pledge, the viewer simply sees a statement with a checked box beside it. This might make it unclear to the viewer that they are being asked to engage, create friction and fail to do enough to get them to stop scrolling.
Similarly, because the treatment presents a statement with a box already checked, it may simply cue viewers to agree and move on. In the control, action is encouraged by agitating urgency around the issue, likely doing more to encourage the viewer to stop, consider the situation, and want to learn more/take some action.
Lastly, we also noticed that in an attempt to strengthen the call to action, we added more copy and reduced the font size—"sign the pledge" appears much larger and eye-catching on the control than it does on the treatment, which could, again, making it clear to the viewer that there is an action to take.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2017 - 07/31/2017
This test teaches us the importance of testing each of the reasons/needs for someone to engage in an offer.
While the control’s benefit is perfectly fine, it is limited in value according to this audience. By connecting the offer to a deeper, more powerful value/desire in the audience (i.e. leaving no stone unturned for your kids versus making prayer more interesting/less boring), more people responded.
As a result, the new ad achieved a 79% increase in emails and reduced cost per email by 43%.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Buckner International had been running a devotional offer for parents as a means of email acquisition for roughly 2 months. We had performed a series of experiments to boost the performance of the campaign but none had given us the kind of lift that we look for on a campaign like this.

We noticed that the cost per email had begun to rise over time. Instead of turning off the campaign, we decided to try out one more experiment. In this instance, we wanted to see if we could reframe the offer to highlight the benefits of prayer itself (not just the benefits of the offer) and incorporating actual scripture into the ad.

And while the audiences for this client may be a bit more philosophical and cerebral than most, the dramatic lift in conversion certainly makes long-form copy in Facebook ads a tactic worth testing with other organizations and audiences as well.

The shorter ad with the direct question produced a 122.1% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that the elements of this ad increased the clarity of the value proposition. Longer copy can be more effective, but only if if clarifies the value proposition. In addition, the direct question to provoke the reader to respond by taking the intended action.
This success prompted further testing to isolate the variables of length and the direct question to understand how each of these played into the success of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was promoting a quiz around the “Gadsden Flag” that led to a membership offer with the flag as a premium. They had created two versions of a Facebook ad, as they had seen some research that longer, more engaging copy could increase clickthrough and conversion rate. The control, in this case, had much longer copy that talked about the history of the flag and made an offer to the reader to test their knowledge. The treatment copy was much shorter, and asked a direct question to the reader: “How much do you know about the Gadsden Flag?”

They split the traffic to determine which ad attracted more clicks.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/09/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below reflect are from the ad set targeting the Network for Good Facebook interest.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this audience, the result was a resounding “No.” The Network for Good audience had a 75% decrease in course registrations when shown the bullet points ad.

Each post had a comparable amount of clicks to the landing page, implying that people who viewed the control were more primed to complete their registration. It’s possible that by adding these bullet points, it made it easier for someone to skim through the post and not experience the full value proposition of the offer.

This learning should not be taken out of context and applied to every audience. Other ad sets targeting different audience segments are approaching validity with the Bullet Point ad as the winning treatment.

experiments.title: How different audience motivations affect click rate in a Facebook ad (Part 1)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad variation will the nonprofit professionals audience respond to more?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/28/2016 - 08/01/2016
While setting up the advertising and promotions for an upcoming live broadcast, we wanted to determine if our various audience groups for our Facebook Ads would respond differently to various ad treatments. We have 3 main audience groups that we ran this test with: our housefile, housefile lookalikes, and nonprofit professionals.
Our original live broadcast Facebook Ad focused on the speaker and the title, but we thought people may be more motivated by different aspects of the focus topic. For the treatments, we developed an ad that focused on “Growing Your Donor File” and one that focused on “Empathy.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Growing Your Donor File” treatment greatly out performed both the control and treatment 2, lifting click rate by 277% with the nonprofit professionals audience. This learning will allow us to increase our total leads for this specific live broadcast. For future live broadcasts, it shows us that it will be more effective to focus our advertising on the learning and results that someone will get out of it, rather than the speaker and their qualifications.

experiments.title: How the call to action of a Facebook ad affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad call to action will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2016 - 07/15/2016
NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Housefile]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/10/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this ad set, we saw a 184% lift in conversion rate from the ads that used bullet points. Additionally, we say a 32% decrease in cost-per-lead.

Interestingly enough, we saw the opposite result with an ad set targeting people unfamiliar with NextAfter. I would hypothesize that since we have already established a sense of trust with our housefile, they do not require as deep of an understanding of the value proposition of our offers.

By bullet pointing the key aspects of the course, someone who already trusts us is able to see how the course is relevant to them and decide to register. The ads without bullet points have the key aspects of the course buried in paragraph form, making it harder for someone to quickly see whether or not this is a resource they’re interested in.

When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our housefile lookalikes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The use of bullet points with the housefile lookalikes audience saw an 81% decrease in course registrations. This result, combined with the result of the Network for Good audience test, continues to confirm our hypothesis that the use of bullet points is less effective with an audience that is not familiar with NextAfter.

They need to see the full value proposition of the offer in order to understand its credibility and depth, whereas our housefile already trusts our work and just needs to quickly see whether the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.title: How framing offer content in a Facebook ad impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that frames the offer content adequately increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2017 - 10/27/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their online course Revelation when they noticed an opportunity to test. In their original Facebook course promotion ad, they called on viewers to ‘study with one of DTS’ renowned professors, for free.’ Through previous message testing, they’ve seen that this is a strong value proposition for a lot of their online resources and material. However, they wondered: Will a stronger focus on the book being studied (i.e. Revelation) and its complexity increase motivation and email acquisition rate?
They launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that their treatment version did increase email acquisition by 143%. It’s clear that offering more context to the book being studied in the course increased motivation of viewers on Facebook and drove far more course signups then the control version. While the original value proposition remains strong, it was much shorter in length.

This has prompted further message testing that focuses on individual course content, as well as the true value of learning from actual seminary professors for free.

experiments.title: How urgency impacts email acquisition rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that stresses the urgency of a course launch increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/16/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was making preparations to launch its new ‘mini’ course on the life and work of Martin Luther. They wondered: If we tell visitors to pre-enroll in this course by stressing the urgency of the upcoming launch date, will email acquisition increase? They launched an A/B test leading up to their launch date to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version that stressed the upcoming launch date did, in fact, increase email acquisition by 88%. By using the actual date of the launch (October 16, 2017) and the word ‘Monday’ in their call-to-action, Dallas Theological Seminary was able to signal to viewers that this was an urgent offer with a specific launch date. It likely made viewers feel that they were going to miss out on a particular event, even though the course was still offered after the launch date.
This strategy has prompted further tests, especially as Dallas Theological Seminary builds plans to launch new courses.

experiments.title: How congruence between ad and landing page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing congruence affect course signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
The new ad that used the same imagery as the landing page increased conversion by 47.6%. This shows that congruence is a powerful, if often subliminal tool. While the ad image might not be something that the visitor actively thinks about, the repetition of the imagery throughout the funnel reminds them subconsciously that the elements are all linked.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary was promoting their second course, Genesis, on Facebook. They wanted to emphasize the academic nature of the course, so they launched ads that showed a person taking the course while studying. But they hypothesized that they might be able to increase conversion by employing the principle known as congruency: aligning each element of a marketing funnel to increase response.
They pulled the image of the Sistine Chapel from the landing page and put it into the ad, and launched an A/B test to see if they were able to affect conversion.

But as they reviewed their ad, they wondered if there was actually too much copy in the ad, and reading it bogged the reader down and kept them from clicking.
They made a treatment that removed the subheadline from their ad image, leaving only a single call-to-action with no supporting copy in order to simplify the value proposition.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ad with reduced copy produced a 19.8% increase in clickthrough rate, which indicated that less copy simplified the value proposition enough to attract more interest from people who saw it.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects click rate in a Facebook Ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will drive the most clicks from a Facebook Ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2016 - 08/12/2016
We released a research study on nonprofit value proposition and advertised it through Facebook Ads to people affiliated with NextAfter including emails on file and website visitors.
There are many different aspects of the value proposition that we could focus on for the promotion of the study, so we wanted to test 3 primary value propositions to see what resonated the most with our audience.
The control focused on the depth of the study. Treatment 1 focused on the 4 key components of a value proposition. And treatment 2 focused on the end result of an effective value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the audience that has already engaged with us in some way, it was 254% more effective to focus on the results of more names, donors, and dollars. This will inform the way the we promote and communicate this study to our website traffic and engaged contacts.

This also points to an underlying motivation of our engaged contacts: these people are looking for results in their fundraising.

experiments.title: How clarity in the design of a Facebook ad can affect conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad design will acquire more email addresses?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2016 - 09/21/2016
When promoting the Why Should I Give to You? research study on nonprofit value proposition, we came to the realization that our Facebook ads were a little bit hard to read. The yellow text against the light gray background seemed to get lost when scrolling through a Facebook feed.
This led us to the hypothesis that a clearer design would yield at least a greater conversion rate. As a result, we developed a treatment version of the ad that presented the same information, but with a clearer design that would stand out more in the Facebook newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment saw a 56.7% decrease in conversion rate. Initially, this was very confusing. How could a design that appeared so much stronger be so ineffective by comparison? We almost didn’t even run a test on this new design because we were so certain it would outperform the control.

Taking a second look, we realized that the slight change of phrasing in order to improve the design aesthetic may have decreased the clarity of the content of the offer.

There are two main learnings we can glean from this test:
There are no marketing experts, design experts, advertising experts, etc. There is only experience. And the only way to know what is more effective is to test
 	Language and copy that makes sense to you doesn’t always make sense to your audience. In this case, the copy in the treatment design made complete sense to us, yet it appears to not have been quite as clear to the Facebook audience.

experiments.title: How not requiring questions on a survey affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that not requiring questions for the national scouting survey will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2022
Boy Scouts of America is running the National Scouting Survey through paid acquisition on Facebook. The survey consists of 12 questions and all had previously been required. There were a variety of multiple choice questions and text questions.
We wanted to test how changing the requirement for answering all questions would affect the email acquisition rate therefore we removed the requirement from all questions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey page with questions not required resulted in a 7.2% increase in email acquisition rate with a 97.2% level of confidence. We believe the lift happened because there was less friction within the survey by not requiring the user to answer every question.
We've concluded that making the questions optional could have caused less engagement with the survey itself, but all in all, it caused the user to be more likely to give us their name and email address.

experiments.title: How a simplified eBook ad image impacted email signup rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the eBook image in our ad for Facebook prospects will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/03/2022
As a part of our experimentation with new subscriber acquisition offers, we theorized that the amount of text and call-outs on the control ad image used to promote our Facebook Ad Template offer created anxiety — which is defined as the “psychological resistance to take action” — by looking so busy.
Therefore, we decided to simplify the eBook image cover and use it in an ad to see if we could generate more clicks and increase the number of people signing up after they clicked the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the treatment produced 53.2% fewer email signups.

Our hypothesis that simplifying the eBook image cover actually worked against us in this case. Now the question becomes: Why?

There are some other data points that are worth considering, as well. They are:
We observed that the treatment ad variant had a 1.35x higher CPM — meaning that Facebook charged us more to reach 1,000 people when compared to the control audience.
 	When the above note is combined with the fact that we saw a 2.5x higher cost per click — the treatment never really had a chance, but it's worth noting also ...
 	Even once we generated a click from the treatment ad variant, we saw 12% fewer people signing up for the offer once they had arrived on the landing page.
less is not more

experiments.title: How adding in more information about an offer to Facebook copy impacted click-through rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing Facebook copy for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in click-throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/01/2022 - 01/31/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. One of their most popular offers is a brochure focusing on the most popular Parables of Jesus.
For this experiment, we tested both a different copy treatment with a different approach to what the brochure offers, as well as images that showed the inside of the brochure.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the copy and images so they gave a Facebook audience more insight into what they were going to be receiving, we saw a 159% increase in click throughs with 100% level of confidence.
Our hypothesis is that the control copy was too generic to interest people and get them to fill out a form on the landing page with all their information (the resource is mailed); once they got an 'inside' view of the content, they were much more likely to click through on the offer.
This is an experiment we ran across multiple offers like this for Pathway to Victory, with similar results. The next iteration of this test would be to focus on isolating which component of the test (copy, image, etc) really led to the experiment results.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2022
As a part of the NIO Summit 2022 promotional paid advertising campaign to sell event tickets, we wanted to experiment with what kind of video conversion event would produce the best balance between impressions, reach, clicks, and full promotional video views.
Our natural thought was to promote the video ads via “reach” campaign types, but our desire as direct response marketers was to test that natural inclination against a more “direct response” like conversion event of “lowest cost per click.”
How would the video ad start and finish rates play out while balanced against clicks and reach?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed that full video views on the "lowest cost per click" campaign surprisingly produced a staggering increase of full video views in the amount of +851.9% over the "reach" optimized campaigns.
It's worth noting that we did reach 85,286 with the "reach" optimized campaign when compared to just 10,287 prospects in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign.
Alternatively, we did see 9.5x more clicks (+7805% increase in ad link clicks, with a 100% level of confidence) in the "lowest cost per click" optimized campaign, though.
So, to summarize — although we reached 8.2x more people with the "reach" campaign, we saw 9.5x more link clicks with the "lowest cpc" campaign (and 1.14x more "full video views" as noted in the data above) over the "reach" optimized campaign.
Ultimately, it comes down to what you value and your ultimate goal ... if you're looking to just get your video ads in front of the largest number of people — you should optimize for the "reach" event type. However, if you're mostly interested in driving site visitors or full video views based upon who you reached, through your video campaigns — it makes sense to optimize for the "lowest cpc" event type.

experiments.title: How using fear of loss language on an instant donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding fear of loss in the language on the donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/20/2022 - 08/19/2022
Boy Scouts of America is running a National Scouting Survey to hear the opinions and thoughts of former Scouts on what Scouting did for them and how it has impacted their life. The survey acquires traffic through paid Facebook advertising.
The original instant donation page copy focused on what Boy Scouts did for young people and what traits it provided them to grow into strong leaders. We wanted to test using fear of loss language in the donation page copy to see if we could increase the conversion rate on the page.
Within our treatment copy, a sense of urgency is brought in by emphasizing what society and young people will lack without the resources that BSA provides to Scouts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The fear of loss language on the treatment donation page caused a 240.2% increase in conversion rate with a 98.7% level of confidence. This informs us that this language resonates better with prospective donors causing them to be more likely to make a donation.
We believe that the fear of loss language is better aligned with the questions on the survey page. By asking users what their thoughts are on the importance of the skills that BSA teaches within the survey, the prospective donors were more inclined to give a donation on the donation page because we emphasized what the younger generation would lose without the support of the BSA.
This emphasis caused users on the page to care more about donating to the organization because they do not want to see a society that lacks loyalty, trustworthiness, etc.
We believe this concept should be tested further on other instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How including a preview of a survey question in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity in a Facebook ad graphic for potential survey takers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/18/2022
We were running a survey offer on Facebook and wanted to develop a treatment to increase clickthrough rate. We wondered if replacing the stock imagery with a text-based ad that gave a preview of the first question on the survey would offer more clarity to ad viewers about what types of questions they would be asked to complete. We also changed the generic headline “Tell us what you think” to a more direct and specific CTA of “Take the survey” to offer additional clarity about the desired action we wanted them to take from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment design led to a 452% increase in clickthrough rate compared to the control with 100% level of confidence. Not only did we see a lift in the first step of the process, but we also saw a 307% increase in email acquisition. We weren't able to just send more people to visit the landing page with the treatment, we were also able to generate more survey responses. In addition to the lifts in both traffic and conversion, we also saw a 99% decrease in our cost per acquisition. We believe the additional clarity in the text-based ad reduced friction and confusion about the desired next step which made it easier for ad viewers to begin taking the survey and created cognitive momentum to complete the full process.

experiments.title: How emphasizing exclusivity in a Facebook ad graphic impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal by highlighting exclusivity  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/19/2022
We were running an eBook cover image for a new offer from The Heritage Foundation and wondered if we could make the offer more appealing by highlighting the exclusivity of the resource. This research report is very unique as it is only available from this organization and no one else, but this was not communicated anywhere in the ad. We created a treatment graphic that placed emphasis on the fact that this offer was exclusive and we reiterated the CTA “Learn More” directly on the graphic to help it stand out in the newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment produced a 21.6% lift in click rate with 98.4% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We believe this experiment saw positive results because the enhanced appeal of making the offer more exclusive made it more desirable because ad viewers were now aware they could not get a resource like this from any other organization or get access to this information anywhere else. We will continue testing this concept on other exclusive offers from The Heritage Foundation and see if we can achieve similar results in other acquisition spaces—such as email prospecting—using this learning.

experiments.title: How optimizing for video reach in a Facebook Ads campaign impacted video consumption
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing for reach for prospective NIO Summit ticket purchasing audiences will achieve increase the number of overall full video views.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/02/2022
As a part of promotional efforts for NextAfter’s 2022 NIO Summit event, we wanted to experiment with which event we should optimize for as it pertains to video consumption and reaching prospective ticket purchasers.
We set up an experiment on Facebook where one campaign would optimize for “ad recall lift,” while the other campaign was optimized for “reach.”
We were not just looking for reach, though — ultimately we wanted to see how many full video views either campaign would produce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we increased the reach of our videos to our prospective NIO Summit customers by +12.3%.
Remember, though, we were looking at which campaign event produced the most full video views — and with regards to that metric, we saw inconclusive data, achieving only a +10.2% increase with the "Reach" campaign, but with only an 18% level of confidence.
Both campaigns generated the same number of website clicks, which left us needing to make a decision on which way to go.
Therefore, we decided that rolling out the "Reach" campaign against a new "Lowest CPC" campaign would be our next step in the optimization of this campaign.

experiments.title: How a video affects click-through rates when compared to a survey preview for a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a video ad will increase click rates on a Facebook ad for a survey offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
Recently we have seen success using video format ads and survey preview ads, so naturally, we wanted to test them against each other to see how they would perform head-to-head.
For this experiment, we pitted a new video treatment against our survey preview control, left all other ad elements unchanged, and ran the two ads for 30 days to compare results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 30 days, the results of our test were conclusive, with the video ad producing a 77.6% decline in click-through rates when compared with the survey preview, with a 100% level of confidence.
This further validates our past learnings that previewing a survey in the ad creative is likely to increase click rates by catching the user's attention and engaging them visually on an issue they care about, leading them forward into the offer where they want to share their thoughts.
We'll now look to globalize this across survey offers for our other public policy clients.

experiments.title: How asking an open-loop question on an ad image affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking an open loop question on the ad image for Facebook users will achieve increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2022 - 06/06/2022
The Heritage Foundation wanted to improve CTR on their Big Tech Survey campaign. The original ad had long, issue-focused copy and a statement on the ad image telling the audience to take their survey to fight back against big tech.
in an attempt to earn more clicks, Heritage tested two ad treatments: one with the same image/statement but shorter body copy and a second with a new image and an open loop question to encourage people to click to find out what they knew.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the test for 30 days, Heritage observed that treatment #2 (with open loop question on ad image) produced a dramatic lift of 304% with a 100% LoC. This indicates that by opening a loop of curiosity on the most prominent part of the ad, Heritage was able to inspire more people to click through, presumably because they wanted to test their knowledge — confirming the hypothesis that creating curiosity would result in a lift in clicks.
This test also supports the general supposition that making changes to the ad's most prominent element - the ad image - may have a greater impact than making similar changes to the body copy, which is less prominent.
Next, Heritage may choose to test a single element of this concept — either the ad image or the value prop language — to see which element has a bigger impact when changed. But for now, we can conclude that:
Making changes to the ad image has a larger impact on performance than changing the body copyOpening a loop of curiosity is likely to encourage more people to click through

experiments.title: How leveraging a well-known eBook author affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leveraging a well-known ebook author will achieve higher click-through rates on a Facebook ad for a content offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 07/12/2022
In an effort to improve click-through rates for a Fund for American Studies content offer, we wanted to test how adding a callout and image of a well-known author (in this case, Rand Paul) would impact click-through rates.
We hypothesized that featuring a U.S. Senator would add authority to the ebook, catch people’s eye, and inspire more people to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After 30 days of running this experiment, we were able to confirm our hypothesis by producing a 58.9% lift in click-through rates with a 99.6% level of confidence.
This experiment shows that "name recognition" can be a powerful motivator for content offers. This may be especially true in this instance because the well-known name in this offer is associated as an ally and "force for good" by the target audience.
When prospects recognize the name and face of someone they respect, they may be more likely to accept a content offer as credible + valuable.
We can now test different treatments against this concept to continue to improve performance.

experiments.title: How removing a progress thermometer to a donation page affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a progress thermometer on a campaign donation page will result in lower conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 08/08/2022
In previous high-urgency campaigns, we’ve had success increasing conversion rates by showing a progress thermometer on the top of the donation page once we were nearing our goal. Knowing this, we wanted to see if we could replicate these results during a recent high-urgency campaign for a public policy client. We hoped to increase conversion rates by inspiring prospects to be one of the donors that helped “fill the bar” and help us meet our goal.
This concept attempts to employ the principles of urgency (we only have X to go), social proof (many others found this cause worthy to support), and unity (belonging to a group) in an effort to inspire donors to act.
We initially planned to show the progress thermometer to 100% of traffic, but then we decided to test this concept again and instead split traffic 50/50 between the control (including the progress thermometer) and the treatment (without the progress thermometer), hypothesizing that removing the thermometer would reduce conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite previous success using the progress thermometer concept during high urgency campaigns, in this instance removing the thermometer produced a valid lift of 17.1% at a 99% level of confidence.
Interestingly, while removing the progress thermometer increased donations, it also resulted in an 8.7% decrease in revenue. And while we were unable to validate this decrease (75.9% level of confidence), when we combine this finding with the fact that the average gift given by donors increased by 24.8% with the thermometer, it appears that while removing the thermometer increased the amount of gifts received, including the thermometer improved the quality of gifts and ultimately raised more money—presumably by motivating donors to give more so that the goal could be reached and because the amount of money already raised indicated that this was a worthy cause to support.
Because of the mixed results produced by this experiment, it may be worth seeking additional opportunities to test this hypothesis again, focusing on revenue, in an effort to validate the portion of our findings that indicated that including the progress thermometer increased overall revenue during the time this experiment was run.

experiments.title: Does a personal iPhone video on a Facebook Ad increase resource downloads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an iPhone Facebook ad video for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in resource downloads as compared to our typical model.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2022 - 05/19/2022
For a couple months we were seeing our typical Facebook ad model with our tools and templates underperforming. It was time to shake things up and we wanted to take a more “human” approach to the creative rather than our typical image of the resource.
After creating a brand new New Donor Welcome Series Timeline tool using content from a previous NIO Summit session, we wondered if using a personal iPhone video created by the presenter would increase resource downloads as opposed to our typical Facebook ad model.
You’ll see in the screenshots that we tailored the copy in the treatment ad version to align more with the video itself.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Surprisingly, there was a 57% decrease in resource downloads from the video ad. However, it is important to note here that the control ad version had just 492 link clicks, where the treatment video ad version had 695 link clicks. These results show that the treatment version of the ad was much more successful at getting traffic to the landing page, but the landing page did not convert visitors.
Based on this observation, I can infer that the offer may not be the right match for this kind of ad. Moving forward we should test this same hypothesis on a different kind of offer.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using video to highlight a free online course for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2022
We were looking to increase click through rates on a free online course offer on Facebook and wanted to see if using a video clip of the course content in the ad creative would increase clarity around the offer as well as enhance the appeal of the offer by giving visitors a glimpse of the content to spike their curiosity.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the video ad was the treatment produced a 111% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence. We believe the increased appeal and clarity introduced in the video led to more interest in learning more about the offer and led additional visitors to click through to the landing page. The course is also taught by the Seminary's President so highlighting this in the video could have also increased credibility and legitimacy of this specific course. Based on these results, we will identify more video clips that can be used to promote free online courses to see if we are able to achieve similar lifts.

experiments.title: How introducing a micro-yes in a Facebook ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a text-based ad with a micro-yes for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2022
We were seeing a steady decline in CTR on Facebook ads due to creative fatigue. We had been using the same graphics to feature online courses for quite a while and wanted to see if we could impact clickthrough rate by making some changes to the creative. We added a text-based image featuring the question “Do you want to learn how to persevere and receive trials with pure joy?” and a banner to click “Yes, access my free course” and ran it alongside the existing control.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, the treatment saw a 28.6% decrease in clickthrough rate with 99.9% level of confidence and did not achieve the results we hoped. We believe this is because the image is still too similar to the existing control and is not different enough or distinguishable enough from the previous image. We also think we may have disqualified some traffic by introducing the question - if their answer to the posed question is "no" they will just keep scrolling and not click to learn more. We will test again using a less definite question and instead choose one that invokes curiosity and we will test using imagery that is more distinctly varied from the control to draw attention and combat creative fatigue.
