experiments.title: How Facebook ad image text affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more specific language in the image of a Facebook ad increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2016 - 06/07/2016
Hillsdale College launched a free digital offer for their popular eBook Liberty and Learning, with a donation ask after the download was processed. As they created the ads for this offer, they wanted to test copy in the Facebook ad to determine how “ready to download” their traffic was.
The first ad assumed that people who saw the ads weren’t very convinced and needed to “learn more” first. This language was highlighted in the image, which is the most visually engaging part of a Facebook ad.
The second ad gave more context to the offer, explaining that it was a “free eBook”. While this gave more clarity to the offer, it also made an assumption that someone was “ready to download” when they clicked.
They launched an A/B test of ads to validate a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 20.6% increase in clickthrough rate, which showed that people who saw the ad were more likely to take initial action when presented with more specific language in the ad. This is aided be by Hillsdale’s precise targeting, but it also tells us that clarity around the offer entices more people to take the first step.
However, clickthrough rate might be deceiving, so Hillsdale measured conversion rate as well to make sure that the lift held all the way through the marketing funnel.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
20.6% increase in traffic
				× 0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How increasing the one-time gift array on a Meta donation form impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing gift arrays for Meta donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/13/2025 - 12/10/2025
This test evaluated the effect of increasing the one-time ask array on Meta forms. Our hypothesis was that keeping the end anchor amount of $150 but increasing the amounts between would increase average gift without negatively impacting conversion. We had previously tested the one-time pre-selected gift array for Meta; the test results can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to determine if increasing the middle amounts within a one-time ask array on Meta forms, while keeping the $150 end anchor, would increase the average gift value without decreasing conversion rates.
What Drove the Lift
Most donors default to the pre-selected amount, so the gains were concentrated among active choosers — donors who navigated away from the default. Two dynamics appear to have influenced their behavior:
The compromise effect: Donors tend to avoid extremes and gravitate toward a middle option. Raising the middle amounts nudged that "safe" choice upward.
 	Reduced sticker shock: Tighter gaps between amounts made the transition from lower to higher options feel more natural.
Next Steps
We'll be rolling out the variant and continuing to test — both around the specific amounts and which value is pre-selected as the default.

experiments.title: Content Offer 2: Can adjusting gift arrays and removing preselection improve donor acquisition for paid social content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom and depends on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that balance donor acquisition with gift value, particularly for newer content offers distributed through paid media.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection affect conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($25/50/100 and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This specific read focuses on the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” content offer, which was newly launched and distributed primarily through Meta during the test period.
Note: this is a drilldown of a larger experiment (link).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
For the “Nine Myths about Capitalism” offer, results were mixed and not statistically valid, but directional patterns were consistent across variants.
📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 1,445Total conversions: 52Overall conversion rate: 3.6%Overall average gift: $38.64Control ($50/100/250, no select):Conversion rate: 3.4%Average gift: $52.50$25/50/100, no select:Conversion rate: 5.2% (+56.6% vs. control)Average gift: $34.97Preselecting $100 on the higher array:Conversion rate: 1.3% (–60.6% vs. control)Average gift: $57.00
✂️ Offer and audience context
This was a new content offerTraffic skewed heavily toward Meta-paid acquisitionPreselecting a gift amount reduced conversion rates on both gift arraysThe $25/50/100 array without preselection leaned toward acquiring more donors, while the higher array without preselection maintained a strong average gift
The data suggests that preselection may introduce friction for this audience and offer type. While the control performed well on average gift, removing preselection—particularly on the lower array—appears more favorable for donor acquisition. Given the newness of the offer and paid social context, these signals likely reflect early-funnel donor sensitivity rather than price resistance alone.
✅ Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significanceConfidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation
Next Steps
Consider breaking out audiences for the higher gift array without preselection to evaluate whether it performs better for higher-intent segments. Alternatively, launch new offers initially with a higher array and no preselect to capture early high-value donors, then shift to a lower array configuration to optimize cost per conversion as the offer matures.

experiments.title: Does adding recency and social proof on a landing page impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the amount of downloads from this month for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it suggests recent ongoing popularity and drives urgency so people will feel like they don’t want to miss out. It also might feel more believable as a smaller number rather than a marketing exaggeration.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/05/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on the landing page for the offer, Dueling Populisms, focusing specifically on its effect on email acquisition. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer overall VS the number of downloads this month to determine which messaging could encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment:

1. Social proof language that highlights recent activity can have a positive impact on email acquisition. The treatment version, which featured the number of downloads for the current month, resulted in a 9.5% increase in email acquisition compared to the control version.

2. The treatment version created a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making the offer more compelling to potential donors.

3. While the results show promise, further testing is recommended to validate the effectiveness of using recent download numbers as social proof.

How to use what was learned in future experimentation:

1. Continue to test different types of social proof language to see what resonates best with potential donors. This could include not just recent activity, but also other forms of social proof such as testimonials or reviews.

2. Consider incorporating elements of urgency and exclusivity in future messaging to increase engagement and drive action.

3. Use this experiment as a foundation for further optimization of landing pages and donation offers, keeping in mind the potential impact of social proof on donor behavior.

experiments.title: How changing the FB creative for an eBook offer impacted click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the creative for avila’s audience will achieve an increase in CTR and engagement because it will increase intrigue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/19/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s newest offers is “What is Purgatory?”. This is an eBook for the viewer to learn more about Purgatory. We wanted to test using creative that was not the ebook. The original ebook had a more traditional, artistic cover, we wanted to see if making the creative a bit more vague would increase intrigue and make people click on the ad. And even further if it would increase engagement and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the creative for Avila's audience led to an increase in clicks for the "What is Purgatory?" eBook offer, indicating that the more vague creative increased intrigue and interest among viewers. While the results were not statistically significant at the 95% level, the 8.8% increase in clicks with an 89.5% statistical confidence suggests that the treatment was effective in capturing the audience's attention.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing different creatives to further validate the initial findings and explore other creative approaches that may resonate with the audience. It is important to analyze the data from each test comprehensively to understand the impact of various creative elements on engagement and ultimately donations. Additionally, considering incorporating qualitative feedback from the audience to gain insights into their preferences and interests when developing future creatives.

experiments.title: How changing ad creative impacted registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the ad creative to look like a book cover for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in registrations/sign ups for the content offer because the ad creative feels more like a book cover rather than a person’s face.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2024 - 09/06/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States.
The Hoover Institution is running one of its long-time content offers, Dueling Populisms. This offer has run for a while, so refreshing creative is a great way for this to seem new to the audience. After seeing a low CTR, we decided we wanted to test the beginning of the funnel with new creative to see if we could increase registrations. For this specific test, we tried testing an image that looks like a book cover and more historical rather than Victor Davis Hanson; however, users have often shown an increase in registrations when we use his face on an ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved changing the ad creative to look like a book cover, resulted in a 25.1% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. This indicates that using an image resembling a book cover instead of featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face did not resonate as well with the audience in terms of driving registrations.

2. Clicks experienced a significant 34.5% decrease, Cost per Click increased by 66.6%, and Cost per Registration increased by 45.7% in the treatment group. These metrics suggest that the new creative may not have been as engaging or appealing to users, leading to a decrease in engagement and an increase in the cost of acquiring registrations.

3. Specific audience segments, such as visitors aged 65+ and Tablet visitors, also showed significant decreases in complete registrations. This indicates that the new creative may have particularly missed the mark with these demographics.

4. While the results show a clear preference for the control ad featuring Victor Davis Hanson's face, the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%. Further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings and ensure their validity.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Audience preference: The audience may have a stronger affinity for ads featuring a recognizable personality like Victor Davis Hanson. It would be beneficial to continue testing different creatives to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

2. Iterative testing: Testing different ad creatives, messaging, and visuals is crucial for optimizing campaign performance. It is essential to analyze the results of each test carefully and iterate based on the learnings to continuously improve outcomes.

3. Audience segmentation: Understanding how different audience segments respond to various creatives can provide valuable insights for targeted campaigns. Segmenting the audience based on demographics, interests, or behaviors can help tailor the creative to specific groups for better engagement.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of testing and iterating on ad creatives to find the most effective approach for driving registrations. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to experiment, the Hoover Institution can optimize its marketing efforts and achieve better results in the future.

experiments.title: How adding a background to an eBook creative impacts ICR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a background to ad creative for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because it will help make a long-time running offer seem “fresher” and therefor want to click the ad and eventually sign up for the offer..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. For one of their long-term eBook offers, Personal Freedom and the Moral Case for Capitalism, we wanted to test whether updating the creative to have an image in the background of the eBook would impact ICR. We hypothesized that updating this creative would increase engagement and the person would be more interested in clicking on the ad and signing up for the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a background to ad creative for Hoover's Facebook audience resulted in a 19.9% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic. While this improvement is significant, the statistical level of confidence was 84.6%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity. Therefore, the results should not be considered conclusive for future decision-making.

2. The treatment also led to a 55.6% increase in Clicks, a 41.9% increase in Cost per Click, and an 84.2% increase in Cost per Registration, all with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment had a strong impact on user engagement and cost-effectiveness.

3. Specifically targeting the 65+ age group resulted in a 45.0% increase in Complete Registrations with a 98% level of confidence. This suggests that tailoring ad content to specific audience segments can yield significant improvements in conversion rates.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

1. Consider running the experiment for a longer duration to gather more data and increase the statistical confidence level of the results.

2. Test different variations of the ad creative, such as using different background images or colors, to understand what resonates best with the target audience.

3. Continue to segment the audience and tailor ad content accordingly to maximize conversion rates among different demographic groups.

4. Use the insights gained from this experiment to inform future ad campaigns and optimizations, focusing on strategies that drive higher engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How a banner style Facebook ad impacted clicks to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that running a yes/no ad during a high urgency campaign for a direct mail audience will achieve increased clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/10/2021
During a high urgency campaign, we wanted to target direct mail recipients with digital ads to prime and reinforce the campaign messaging in order to drive additional highly motivated and qualified traffic to the donation page. We wanted to test our control copy which included the deadline and the Challenge Gift against a treatment that made a specific ask to give a monthly gift and become a new Cornerstone Partner. We also developed a second treatment that asked a question to frame and align the donor’s values and beliefs with the organization and used more sliding door copy that presents what happens if we do not equip Christian leaders with Scripture. We wanted to activate the power of the “micro-yes” through this ad and bring those visitors to the donation page while they are already nodding their heads in agreement with the mission of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: While neither treatment had enough run-time to see valid results since it was a short, time-bound campaign, the ad that had "Learn more about becoming a Cornerstone Partner" as the call to action increased traffic by 65.6% with a 91.8% level of confidence. The second treatment with the banner graphic also drove clicks up 21.8% but only had a 62.7% level of confidence. We will look for additional opportunities to test the banner graphic in acquisition ads to see how the belief alignment impacts traffic and conversion rate for both online courses and instant donation pages.

experiments.title: How Adding “Yes/No” language to Facebook Ads Impacts Performance
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding “yes/no” buttons to creative for questions posed in ad will achieve more clicks because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions. because it taps into the binary nature of close-ended questions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2023 - 10/31/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
Using “Yes/No” language buttons on a static creative piece has improved performance for some other clients, and the NextAfter team believes Hoover’s audience will react positively and improve CTR.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from utilizing "Yes/No" buttons on static ads within Facebook are as follows:

1. Adding "yes/no" buttons to ad creative for questions posed in the ad resulted in an 11.6% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that using close-ended questions with binary options in ad creative can effectively drive engagement.

2. The statistical confidence level is below 95%, but the results approach validity. This suggests that further testing is required to confirm the effectiveness of the "yes/no" buttons.

3. The experiment also revealed some negative impacts. Complete registrations experienced a 442% decrease, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons might have discouraged users from completing the registration process.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration saw significant increases of 115% and 1228%, respectively. This suggests that "yes/no" buttons may have attracted more clicks, but these clicks may not have resulted in a higher number of registrations.

5. Specific audience segments, such as 65+ visitors, tablet visitors, and male visitors, saw significant increases in clicks, indicating that the "yes/no" buttons may appeal more to these demographics.

Based on the insights gained, it is highly recommended to conduct further experiments to validate the effectiveness of employing "yes/no" buttons in ad creative. To understand if there are any usability issues or how they can be mitigated, it is essential to investigate the negative impact on complete registrations. Additionally, analyzing the metrics of cost per click and cost per registration will help determine the overall effectiveness and ROI of implementing "yes/no" buttons in future campaigns. Through careful and controlled experiments, the Hoover Institution can obtain more confidence and better understand the use of "yes/no" buttons to enhance ad creativity and prompt desired user actions.

experiments.title: Does using a PDF icon on a content offer’s Facebook ad impact signups?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a PDF icon on Facebook creative for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher sign up rate because there is clarity in the type of offer they are receiving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
PTV offers multiple digital content offers in an effort to acquire new names, new donors, and to keep known contacts and donors engaged with content.
One of PTV’s most successful offers is about Billy Graham. In an effort to increase the success of this offer, we decided to test adding a PDF icon on the image.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment include:

The treatment, which involved adding a PDF icon to the Facebook creative, resulted in a 10.0% decrease in Complete Registrations for All Traffic with a 83.8% statistical level of confidence. However, since the confidence level is below the 85% threshold, the results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making

experiments.title: How zooming in on ad creative impacted cost per registration

rows.name: Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Control
Hoover Digest Spring 2024 Creative Test Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover  for Hoover’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in the CTR because it engages the viewer with a highly colorful and visual image and provides consistency throughout the offer funnel with the eBook cover.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. Currently, the Hoover Institution is running one of its seasonal offers, the Spring Digest. It showed a low CTR. We wanted to test the beginning of the funnel to see how this would impact clicks and, ultimately, registrations. Since the covers for the Digest are visually appealing, we decided to zoom in on the eBook and delete some of the negative space around the eBook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Adjusting the ad creative to show more of the eBook cover resulted in a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 81.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. Therefore, the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
3. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 18.3% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment had a positive impact on this metric.
4. The Cost per Registration also experienced a significant decrease of 16.2% with a 99.9% level of confidence, revealing that there was an increase in conversions with a lower cost.
In future experimentation, it is important to:
1. Ensure that experiments are run for a longer period to gather more data and increase the statistical validity of the results.
2. Consider other factors that may have influenced the outcome, such as seasonal trends or changes in the target audience.
3. Continue to test different variations of ad creatives to optimize performance, but always prioritize statistical significance in order to make informed decisions.

experiments.title: How humanizing Facebook Creative impacts complete registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/03/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on optimizing click-through rates and conversions for an eBook promotion are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an ad with targeted imagery of a person engaged in outdoor activities, resulted in a 35.0% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control ad with the eBook's cover image.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 92.6%, indicating that the effectiveness of the treatment is approaching validity but further testing would be necessary to confirm its impact.

3. The hypothesis that targeted imagery helps audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook, fostering a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates, was supported by the results of the experiment.

Moving forward, future experimentation should consider the following points:

1. Continue to test and refine the use of targeted imagery in advertisements to enhance engagement and conversions. Further experiments can help strengthen the validity of the findings and optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

2. Explore different types of imagery and messaging to determine which combinations resonate best with the target audience. This can inform future advertising strategies and improve campaign performance.

3. Implement A/B testing on a larger scale or over an extended period to gather more data and increase the confidence level of results. This will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of different ad elements on audience behavior.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that targeted imagery can have a significant effect on engagement and conversions. By leveraging this insight and continuing to test and refine strategies, WUNC can enhance the effectiveness of its promotional campaigns and drive better outcomes for future eBook promotions and other marketing efforts.

experiments.title: How adding a social proof popup to a membership renewal page affected donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a social proof popup for donors on a membership renewal page will achieve an increase in donation rate because people will be motivated by the fear of missing out.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2024 - 06/04/2024
In an effort to increase donation conversion rate on their membership renewal donation page, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding a popup that showed who had recently made a donation would lead to this increase in donations due to a fear of missing out (FOMO) on what actions others are taking.
They launched an A/B test where the control was the donation page without any popup, and the treatment had a small unintrusive popup in the corner of the screen that read:
“(Donor’s Name) from (State) just renewed their Heritage Foundation membership”
The treatment FOMO popup, which showed a new donor every 15 seconds, also had an American flag icon to align with the same flag icon in the monthly tab of the donation form and appeal to the donor’s patriotism.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This A/B test ran for 13 days. The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The addition of a social proof popup on the membership renewal donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, it resulted in a decrease of 43.5% in transactions for all traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence was 84.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making, but are indicative that further testing is needed to solidify the learning.
Reasons for this decrease may include potential donors preferring total privacy, not wanting their personal information (name and state) shared once they made a donation. Visitors to the treatment could have also been too distracted by the popup's animation to read and fully take in the donation page's copy.
In future, we should test this social proof popup on another donation page related to a content offer or a non-membership direct appeal to test whether they lead to the same results.

experiments.title: How using organic direct appeal text ads on Facebook impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that using an organic-style text direct appeal ads for users on Facebook and Instagram will achieve an increased in clicks and donations because   the organic ads will feel like organic content on the platform instead of a paid advertisement.
because    the organic ads will feel like organic content on the platform instead of a paid advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2024 - 04/29/2024
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. Their top donor acquisition campaign has been their Shoes for Orphan Souls (SOS) campaign.
For this experiment, we wanted to test an organic-style text ad against their current dynamic ads with photos of children receiving shoes. Dynamic ads use a catalog of photos in which Facebook’s algorithm will select what image visitors will see.
Our hypothesis is that using an organic-style text direct appeal ads for users on Facebook and Instagram will achieve an increased in clicks and donations because the organic ads will feel like organic content instead of paid advertisement.
In addition, with the organic ads’ streamlined design, it minimizes visual friction and users will be able to clearly see the call-to-action without the potential confusion of plain imagery.
We set this test up in Facebook as an A/B test so that 50% of our audience would see the control ads and the other 50% would see the treatment ads. The control campaign had the image-only ads and the treatment campaign had the organic text ads. Both campaigns had the same budget and audience targeting, and the test ran for 26 days on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for about a month, we found that the organic-style text ads had a 111.5% increase in conversion rates at a 90.1% level of confidence. Since the LoC is below our benchmark for validity of 95%, this experiment is considered invalid. This tells us that organic-style text ads can potentially lead to a significant increase in purchases, however furthering testing is needed to confirm the effectiveness of the ads.
Additionally, the experiment revealed that the Cost per Click experienced a significant 441% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This implies that while the treatment ads may be driving more purchases, they are also more expensive in terms of cost per click.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing organic-style text ads against other ad formats to determine the most effective approach for driving donations. It may also be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with variations in messaging, visuals, and audience targeting to optimize ad performance while keeping the cost per click in check. Lastly, exploring ways to increase the statistical confidence level of the results would provide more reliable insights for decision-making.

experiments.title: Does Humanizing Facebook Creative Improve Click-Through-Rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Utilizing targeted imagery increases engagement and downloads by helping Facebook audiences visualize themselves benefiting from the eBook. It fosters a connection that drives action and boosts conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/30/2024
This study was conducted on Facebook for WUNC, a public radio station in North Carolina, to optimize click-through rates and enhance overall conversions for a promotional campaign. The experiment aimed to refine the effectiveness of an advertisement for an eBook about outdoor activities in North Carolina. The experimental design compared the performance of an ad featuring the eBook’s cover image against an alternative ad featuring a person engaged in outdoor recreation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that utilizing targeted imagery, specifically featuring a person engaged in outdoor activities, can significantly increase engagement and conversions for promotional campaigns such as eBooks. In this case, the treatment ad featuring a person saw a 12.7% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control ad featuring the eBook cover image, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.2%.
These results suggest that incorporating images of people enjoying the outdoor activities showcased in the eBook can help the audience visualize themselves benefiting from the content, thus fostering a stronger connection and driving action. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using targeted imagery that resonates with the target audience to enhance engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it could be beneficial to further test different types of imagery and messaging to continuously optimize the effectiveness of promotional campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a content offer email impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including social proof in a content offer email for the housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversions because it reinforces the perceived value of the offer based on what others are already saying about it..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2024 - 02/14/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s resend of the Genesis 1 e-book offer to their housefile, we hypothesized that since this offer has already run previously including a line of social proof in the email to generate interest would impact sign-up rate and donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The inclusion of social proof in the content offer email for the housefile led to a significant increase in donor conversions. The statistical level of confidence was at 88.2%, indicating that the results are approaching validity but further testing is recommended for certainty.

What's notable is that although this experiment only took place at the email level, however the primary metrics impacted were downstream in the funnel. The line of social proof's impact on click rate was negligible, with a 1.2% lift at a 16.1% LOC. However, those who clicked from the social proof treatment were far more likely to sign up and far more likely to give with a 14.3% increase in sign-ups on the landing page and a 59.6% in donor conversions.

Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential effectiveness of social proof in driving donor conversions. By incorporating this strategy into future email campaigns and conducting further testing, Reasons to Believe can continue to optimize their efforts to attract and convert donors effectively.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Daily Devotional 2024 CYE 2023 - treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Brand awareness can positively impact conversion rates:** The treatment group, which focused on brand ads rather than direct ask ads, saw a 16.1% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. This indicates that building brand awareness through ads can lead to higher conversion rates.

2. **Variations in different metrics:** Clicks experienced a significant decrease, while Complete Registrations saw a substantial increase during the treatment phase. Cost per Click, Cost per Registration, and Cost per Purchase also showed notable changes. Additionally, there were decreases in purchases across different visitor types (65+, Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, Female, and Male visitors).

3. **Statistical confidence levels:** While the changes in metrics were significant, the statistical confidence levels were not high enough (85% or above) to consider the results valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Consider longer test durations:** Running experiments for a longer duration may help in achieving higher statistical confidence levels, providing more reliable results for decision-making.

2. **Diversify test approaches:** While brand awareness seems to have a positive impact on conversion rates, it is essential to continue testing different strategies to find the most effective approach for fundraising campaigns.

3. **Segmentation analysis:** Understanding the impact of different visitor types on conversion rates can help tailor campaigns to specific audiences, potentially increasing overall effectiveness.

Overall, while the experiment showed promising results in terms of the impact of brand ads on conversion rates, it is crucial to conduct further analysis and experiments with higher statistical confidence levels before making significant decisions based on these findings.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
Results are based on December 16-31 as this is the timeframe that would show the fruits of the brand ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that running brand ads on Facebook can potentially lead to a higher conversion rate in terms of donations for a nonprofit like Pathway to Victory. The treatment group, which was exposed to brand ads before the Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads, saw a 16.1% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be relied upon for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we will test running brand ads alongside offer ads rather than only brand at the beginning.

experiments.title: How softening the opening of a Facebook ad impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the opening fear line for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because fear is not always a successful tactic. Does curiosity perform better?.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/17/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV launched a brand-new book “Are We Living in the End Times?” as a response to the war in Israel. In an effort to increase Facebook conversions we decided to test removing the hard opening sentence “With all the chaos in the world recently, you would not be wrong to start worrying that the End Times are right around the corner.”
Our thought was that this opening line was too much of a discouragement to people especially with so many negative things happening in the world. By removing that line, the ad opens with a more curious approach and less fear.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that removing a fear-based opening line in a Facebook ad resulted in a significant increase in donations. This suggests that curiosity may be a more effective tactic for driving conversions compared to fear. However, the results are not considered fully valid as the statistical level of confidence is below 95%.
To further validate these findings and ensure the intended effect, it is recommended to conduct additional testing. This could involve running similar experiments with different variations of ad copy, such as using a different curiosity-based opening line or testing other emotional appeals. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different ad formats, targeting strategies, or visuals on conversion rates.
By continuing to experiment and iterate on ad messaging and strategies, the nonprofit can optimize their Facebook campaigns to achieve higher conversions and ultimately increase fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign for Grace Gone Wild impacted conversion on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Grace Gone Wild traditional
PTV DA Grace Gone Wild radical

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by promoting the book with an exciting creative, that matches the cover for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because there is excitement around the topic and interest.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2023 - 06/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Grace Gone Wild is a book published by PTV. In the past, this book has not be very successful. Therefore in an effort to increase conversions we decided to test a radical approach to the Facebook creative to see if we could increase conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment was conducted to test whether promoting the book "Grace Gone Wild" with an exciting creative that matches the cover on Facebook would increase conversions.
The results showed that the treatment had an 11.7% decrease in clicks for all traffic, indicating that the control version had a higher conversion rate. However, since the statistical level of confidence was just below 95%, further testing would be necessary to validate the results fully.
Another significant observation was that the cost per click increased by 67% with a 100% level of confidence. This information could be beneficial in future experimentation.
The key learning from this experiment is that an exciting creative alone may not necessarily increase conversions, as evidenced by the decrease in clicks for all traffic in the treatment. The language used in the promotional campaign may also be a factor worthy of testing in future experiments.
Based on these results, we should continue to conduct further experimentation to explore the most effective messaging and creative strategies for promoting the book, "Grace Gone Wild."

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a landing page impacts site creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimony for prospective site creators will achieve an increase in site creation conversion rate because social proof reduces anxiety and adds credibility to the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2023 - 04/11/2023
In oder to increase the conversion rate for site creations, we ran a test where we added social proof in the form of a testimonial on a landing page.
This test was ran on an ebook funnel where the confirmation page for their ebook had a call to action to create a site with CaringBridge. The control for this experiment omitted any kind of testimonial whereas the treatment included a real-life quote from someone who has benefited from created a site. Our hypothesis that adding social proof will reduce any kind of anxiety someone may feel when creating a site with CaringBridge. This quote adds credibility to those who may be unfamiliar with CaringBridge.
This was tested on one ebook funnel and traffic was primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a testimonial to a confirmation page may not have the intended effect of reducing anxiety and adding credibility to an organization. The treatment resulted in a 51.0% directional decrease in website creation compared to the control, but the statistical level of confidence was only 86.6%. This means that the results are not considered valid but are approaching validity.
These results are contrary to what was expected. It's probable that the selected testimonial may not be relevant enough to the subject matter of the ebook for this funnel. It's recommended that this is retested in a different ebook funnel to draw further conclusions. Additionally, CaringBridge could anchor the benefits of creating a site by using language like "Join over XXX,XXX families who have benefited from using CaringBridge". This reference point suggests that there are many others who have used CaringBridge successfully, which may add credibility and increase the perceived value of using the platform.

experiments.title: How updating the call-to-action button on a Facebook ad impacts click-throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action button for potential course takes will achieve a change in course sign up rates because the button relates to the action we are asking them to take. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2023 - 03/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For the launch of a new course led by Dan Burke, the founder of Avila, we wanted to test whether a change in the call-to-action on a Facebook ad would make an impact on click-throughs.
Our standard control for most of Avila’s ads has been to use a ‘learn more’ call to action; in this experiment, since we were asking people to sign up to take the course, we tested learn more versus a ‘sign up’ call to action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call-to-action button from 'Learn More' to 'Sign Up' did not lead to an increase in click-throughs for Avila Foundation's new course. The treatment resulted in a 6.7% decrease in clicks, which was statistically significant with a 94.9% level of confidence.
However, when viewing this experiment through the lens of how many people actually signed up for the course, the results are different. While there was a higher number of clicks on the 'learn more' control version, it actually had a 6.3% less sign-up rate once those people got to the landing page. Thus, we can see a correlation between asking people to sign up as the call-to-action, and them actually going through the process. While this only had a 61% level of confidence, it does show a directional lift in response.
Continued testing for this kind of experiment is needed. Future variations could include making the copy of the ad itself correlate more to the call to action, and/or testing within the ad headlines as well so they correlate to the call-to-action button.

experiments.title: How testing YouTube ad text affected donor acquisition costs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing ad messaging for YouTube viewers will achieve a lower cost per donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2023
At Hoover, we’ve been expanding acquisition on YouTube as we’ve seen good results thus far, and a new video became available to launch.
Our main KPI is cost per donor.
So, we wanted to try this experiment in order to identify ad copy that was more effective at driving donations and reducing acquisition costs.
We believed that testing different versions of ad copy could be a major lever in improving the performance of our YouTube ads, and that it could also help us to better understand what types of messaging resonated with our target audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Cost per donor
C: $102.93T1: $63.07 T2: $64.79
Testing your ad messaging:
is easier to executespeed of implementation is usually fastercan be a major lever to reducing your acquisition costs
In this case, treatment 1 saw a 39% reduction in cost per donor for 20 minutes working on ad copy.

experiments.title: How combining audiences in Facebook campaigns affects efficiency in donor acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Combining common ad sets into larger audiences in a Facebook campaign will increase donor conversion and decrease cost per donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/28/2021
The Facebook campaigns for the Missionary Oblates had multiple ad sets for similar audiences. For instance, they Catholic online, Knights of Columbus, Catholic and Proud, were all separate ad sets in Facebook, but are all Catholic audiences. In Facebook it’s more efficient to have ad sets with a larger reach because Facebook is more likely to spend money in a larger ad set. So we hypothesized that combining those smaller ad sets into one big Catholic ad set would increase efficiency and lower cost per donor. We tested a campaign with their broken out ad sets against a campaign with combined ad set of a common audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: As the results show, Facebook had to reach fewer people with combined ad sets. It did increase donor acquisition by 18%, but it did not reach validity, with only 84% level of confidence. However, this did not decrease the cost per donor. In fact, the cost per donor was 3% higher with the test campaign that combined audiences in the ad set. And it decreased the ROI by 9%. We will need to look at new ways to test and optimize Facebook audiences.

experiments.title: Does Adding Social Proof on a Donation Page Increase the Number of Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof to an instant donation page for people who have signed up for a free video course will result in a higher number of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/28/2022 - 11/14/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase the number of donations on an Instant Donation Page by adding a statement of social proof.
We also wanted to see if there was an impact based on where the social proof was located on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test we found that adding social proof above the point of decision (on the donation form) led to a lift 27%, but did not reach validity with only a 52% level of confidence. Even though we were not testing for increases in revenue, we did find that while total donation amounts were similar between the control and variant 2, the average donation amount was around $3 higher for the variant with social proof. Did the social proof increase motivation to give more? Further testing would be needed to say with certainty.

We also discovered that placement did seem to matter...as adding the proof below the form near the submit button resulted in nearly a 50% decrease over the second variant. My hypothesis is that if a donor is to make it that far they do not need the extra motivation to donate, and in fact, could have led to more friction in the giving process.

There will need to be further testing to see if the variables can produce positive results. For our next experiment, we will test the social proof in variant 2 against different copy to see if the way the proof was written will affect the results. And, since we did not have a clear winner, we will start with the same control of no social proof against various statements of social proof above the donation form.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials in a sidebar impacted email acquisitions on a free eBook offer page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding credibility via book reviews for a free eBook offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/27/2022 - 08/05/2022
We wanted to find out if increasing the appeal by adding social proof through credibility and evidentials would improve email acquisition rates on a free eBook offer. We took testimonial quotes from another author and from one of the organization’s trusted pastors and council members and featured them on the landing page to further reinforce to landing page visitors that this was a valuable book offer that was available for free. We split traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment and ran the test for a month.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we observed as a 2.2% decrease in conversion rate with 94% level of confidence. The results were pretty much flat but overall we saw a slight decrease in the percentage of total emails acquired across all traffic for those who experienced the treatment version of the landing page. Our hypothesis is that visitors could have been unfamiliar with those who were quoted in the testimonials. Because the recommendations were coming from another author and from a pastor rather than a layperson, it could have been assumed that this offer was only suitable for those in positions of ministry or Christian writers—rather than a person in the general workforce or average home. This could have led some people to think the offer was not intended for them and so they did not take the next step. Some changes in visitor segments in the experiment that were interesting to note were that while direct visitors had a 16.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence, email visitors had a 14.0% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. It's possible that email visitors could have been more receptive to the treatment since they are likely to be more familiar with the style and nature of TGC book reviews if they have been receiving regular communications via email from the organization.

experiments.title: How previewing the survey affects click-through-rates on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that previewing a survey for FB users will achieve higher click-through rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2020 - 07/09/2022
In an effort to improve click-through-rates for a FB ad pointing people to a survey on the Border Crisis, we wanted to test if a radical redesign centered on previewing the survey, rather than highlighting the problem, would inspire more viewers to click. For this experiment, we mocked up a survey question on the ad image so that the viewer would be prompted to click to select their answer
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed a 25.6% lift in clicks with an 84% level of confidence. These results lead us to believe that by previewing the survey and thus focusing on the solution in the form of an action, rather than the problem, more viewers were inspired to act.

While these results are promising, because this experiment altered several elements of the ad: previewing the survey in the ad image + shortening the body text to focus on the desired action, we can not say with certainty which change had more of an impact on this lift.

Our hunch is that the image, being more visible when people scroll their feeds, played more of a role ... but to further validate these results, we will next want to test a new image using the same body copy as the winning ad above.

experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we needed to turn this experiment off before it validated, the results were directionally accurate when we observed a +36% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 66%) and subsequently a 39.2% increase in revenue (LoC: 90%) when giving the campaign a longer period of time and budget setting.

Our net cost per donor also dropped by 35% for the 6-month campaign and ROI was up +40% when compared to the 1-month campaign type.

Key Takeaway:

Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we're willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you're a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don't have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn't performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the "lifetime budget" setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How the use of the word “you” affects click through rate of an eBook offer on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: The use of the word “you” will increase conversion because it is addressing and engaging the reader specifically.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/23/2021
As we were launching a new eBook offer for Prison Fellowship International, the original language for the funnel copy included a lot of reader centered language focused on the outcome for the reader and the purpose for the reader, which often included the use of the word “you.” As that was not typically the communication style for PFI, they preferred to use more general language about how the eBook benefits people in general. To ensure that this did not affect the outcome, we wanted to test this in the Facebook marketplace.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the click through rate was 8% lower on the reader centered language. And that was a key metric for this funnel, because the cost per click was high, making the funnel more difficult to scale. Also, the ultimate goal was to acquire new donors. And the donor conversion for the more general language brought in two times more donors. For these two key reasons, we discontinued the test before reaching validation to place spend on the more efficient funnel.

experiments.title: How daily budget optimization performs against lifetime budget optimization in Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Does daily budget optimization outperform lifetime budget optimization in getting more instant donors at a lower cost?
experiments.keyLearnings: Although the cost to acquire an instant donor was slightly lower with daily budget optimization, we saw a 23% decrease in conversion rate using this feature. Though the experiment did not validate, we hypothesize that using lifetime budget optimization allows Facebook to spend more at certain times of the day/week that users are most active, rather than forcing it to spend the budget each day, will lead to greater scalability and an increase in donor conversion long-term.

experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you’re a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don’t have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn’t performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the “lifetime budget” setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: The impact of using Facebook’s video creation tool on an ad campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a 15sec video created with the Facebook video creation tool convert more people into donors on the ad than a photo?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2019 - 08/11/2019
We’ve had a successful campaign advertising a pledge that led to a monthly donation page on Facebook. In a previous experiment, we found that using an image of a newborn baby on this offer significantly increased donor conversion (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2321)   We decided to test that image against a new video using the video creation tool in Facebook. They hypothesis was that a 15sec video would get more people to convert than the image.
experiments.keyLearnings: 218% more people who clicked the video ad became monthly donors than who clicked the photo ad. This was despite the fact that the photo ad had  14% more impressions. We will begin using and testing videos in more of our campaigns to see the impact they have on conversion.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Housefile]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad image that is more closely aligned with the title of the course increase clarity and conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
Prior to launching this experiment, we had leaned that it was more effective to use bullet points to clearly convey key objectives of the course to our house file audience.
We hypothesized that we could further increase clarity by visually reinforcing the title of the course “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.” So we took our original ad image and tweaked to show the Facebook logo pointing towards a dollar icon.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this experiment did not reach full validity, it strongly pointed towards the control as being more effective at converting course registrants. The test was turned off due to the significant increase in cost-per-registration that the treatment ad was creating.
It’s possible that the additions to the original image did not communicate the intended message as clearly as we had thought it might. It’s also possible that our original ad already garnered more attention in the newsfeed as a result of likes and shares from the time it had been running before this experiment.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Blackbaud]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting Blackbaud followers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach full validity as it was turned off due to increase cost-per-registration. The result was leaning towards the bulleted copy as the winner, which is the only result in this series of experiments that runs counter to our hypothesis that bullets keep a potential new subscriber from understanding the full weight of the value proposition.
Although, since this experiment did not reach full validity, I’m more inclined to trust the results of the valid tests in this series.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Web Traffic]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting our website traffic, excluding the house file.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not reach validity. It was pointing towards the control as being the winner, further validating the hypothesis that bulleted does not help a potential new subscriber to understand the full value proposition. Whereas, with a contact on our house file, they have a pre-established trust and are simply looking to see if this offer is relevant to them.
This experiment was ended before full validation due to the escalating cost-per-registration.

experiments.title: How a video ad affects the cost of traffic acquisition through Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a video ad drive more traffic to the website?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 03/07/2017
NextAfter runs a series of Facebook ads that promotes their research to a house file look-alike audience. The intent is to get people to self identify that they are interested in nonprofit content, before they would be targeted with an acquisition ad.
We thought that using a video advertisement could capture the attention of more people and generate more clicks. So we developed 3 ads.
The control was an illustration like what we have used in the past. Treatment 1 was used a still image of the experiment break-down video. And Treatment 2 was a 15 second video teasing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The still image of the video started to approach validity with a 36.3% increase in clicks, but the video itself made virtually no difference in click rate what-so-ever.
The most significant metric in this test to us was the Cost-Per-Click (CPC). Regardless of what the click rate is, we want to get the most traffic for the lowest spend possible.
Oddly enough, treatment 1 had a 40% higher CPC than the control even though it had a higher click rate. Treatment 2, the video ad, had a 65% higher CPC than the control even though the click rate was essentially the same.
This test shows us that it is more cost effective for us to use the illustrated ads than it is to use this type of video to drive new traffic to our website.

experiments.title: How the use of video in a Facebook Ad affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook Ad variation will get more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/05/2016 - 08/12/2016
At NextAfter, we’re trying to distribute our research blog to the wider nonprofit audience in an effort to educate the marketplace about the effect that optimization can have on fundraising.
As part of this effort, we were running a series of Facebook Ads to our housefile lookalike audience. The initial click rates were lower than desired, so we wanted to test a new treatment.
Our hypothesis was that using a video advertisement could generate a higher click rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The video Facebook Ad received a 71.5% lift in clicks. The reasons for this increase could be:
The movement and action of the video was more effective at catching the eye of Facebook users scrolling through their newsfeed, causing them to pay more attention to the content of the ad. Or…
The video demonstrated and reinforced the value proposition of the blog in a way that helped increase clarity and appeal.
We will continue to test video in Facebook Ads to try and determine exactly what affect it has on Facebook Ad audiences.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s non-member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for activating non-members for the first time?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind members to activate their membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the non-member segment that received Facebook ads in the 3rd channel of communication saw an online response rate increase of 20.3%.
Other metrics impacted are as follows:
Average gift: +13.1% increaseTotal Revenue: +36.09% increase
During the campaign, the total cost to run these ads to non-members generated a 2.03 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the "with ads" segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an equal amount to the budget spent in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Where possible, adding a 3rd channel of communication to heed the campaign's call to action for non-members should be considered.

experiments.title: How optimizing Facebook for purchases impacts donor acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will Facebook’s standard “Purchase” event yield better donor acquisition for a petition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2018 - 12/14/2018
TPPF had been running their Facebook petition campaign for several weeks with the goal of acquiring additional donors for the organization. After nearly a month of activity, email acquisition had been happening at an extremely low cost but we were seeing exceptionally low instant donor conversion. After a discussion with our Facebook rep, we decided to test out Facebook’s native “Purchase” event to optimize our ads. They recommend that we have at least 50 conversions to optimize off of but we wanted to see if it would still be impactful with our low donor acquisition numbers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 5 days, we saw the Purchase event optimization had yielded three donors compared to the Control’s zero donors. This was the result of a 1.0% instant donor conversion rate for petition signers. Knowing the historical donor conversion rate for this campaign (0.1% instant donor conversion rate) this was a clear winner.
In the ideal world, we would have run this experiment for well over a month in order to see at least one purchase come in for the Control to validate the results. However, when our primary metric is to acquire donors and we are seeing significant improvement with the new method of optimization, it did not make sense to continue wasting our advertising budget just to validate an experiment.
It is worth noting that while the instant donor conversion rate jumped to 1% for petition signers, we also saw the email acquisition rate drop by 62% and our cost per email went up 48%. Both of these were successful tradeoffs given that we were finally able to acquire donors.

experiments.title: How Facebook’s Bid Cap influenced overall results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will setting a bid cap of $50 decrease our costs per donor acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2019 - 01/10/2019
Buckner International had recently experienced two significant lifts to their 7 Scriptures email acquisition offer that had resulted in a boost to instant donor conversion. The resulting boost also reduced the cost per donor. It fluctuated by day but were were seeing some days with a cost per donor acquired of $50 or less. However, it was unpredictable.
In an effort to control the costs, we decided to test out the setting a cap on our bid of $50. This would allow us to potentially limit our costs, assuming the strategy worked.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were unable to validate this experiment because, with the low bid cap of $50, Facebook was not able to show our ads to enough audience to receive a conversion. While we were getting conversions for $50 or less without the cap, limited the spend to this low dollar resulted in Facebook not being able to show the offer enough. Without the audience exposure, we were not able to acquire any donors. While we did save ad spend, we sacrificed results which is not worth the trade off.

experiments.title: How a different Facebook ad image affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing the format of the webinar lead to an increase in webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2018 - 10/30/2018
In this experiment, we wondered if showing the format of the webinar and putting the production quality on display in the imagery would lead to more webinar registrations. The control used a typical webinar ad design – abstract representation of the topic, with å clear focus on the title and content.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this didn’t quite reach 95% validity, this treatment decreased registrations by 45% across all targeted Facebook audiences. It’s possible that what we thought was an added value (seeing the production quality) was actually a distraction from the actual topic, and the image made it more difficult to quickly see what the content was about as someone scrolled through the Facebook newsfeed.

experiments.title: How adding urgency into Facebook ad copy affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding urgency to an ad through body copy increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2018 - 12/03/2018
Buckner International was offering a free Advent devotional to be delivered each week of December. They wanted to test if adding urgency around the start of the Advent season would increase conversion rate. The “December 2” deadline allow for a clear deadline that would necessitate the need for signing up sooner rather than later. We decided to emphasize that date and include some copy early in the ad to introduce that concept prior to Facebook’s “Read More” link that would hide the bottom portion of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the desired 95% level of confidence needed to statistically validate the experiment, we did see a directional decrease of 18%. Since we had reached the Dec 2 deadline mentioned in the ad, we had to stop the ad before reaching confidence. Urgency is a factor that has often proved to increase conversion; in this case, however, it is our hypothesis that the added urgency went against the purpose of Advent. An Advent devotional is intended to be used as a way for an individual to slow down during a hectic season.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affect conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action drive higher course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.”
