experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an 84.9% decrease in conversion for this audience group (Network for Good). This runs counter to the result of the same experiment with the Nextafter.com traffic. Together, these are showing that people who have not been exposed to NextAfter need more exposure to the value propositon in order to trust that our offers are worthwhile. Those who have engaged with us in any capacity already have a high level of trust, and simply need to see whether or not the offer is relevant to them.

experiments.title: Will clarity in a Facebook ad headline increase blog traffic?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a clearer Facebook ad headline increase click to the blog post?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/04/2018
In this experiment, we had set up multiple promoted blog posts for the month of March. When setting up a promoted post for a blog comparing direct mail and email fundraising, we wondered if the blog headline was not clear enough to convey the point of the post. So we created a treatment that used clearer language.
experiments.keyLearnings: By changing the word “cannibalizing” to “stealing,” we were able to add additional clarity that led to a 32.4% increase in clicks to the blog post. This demonstrates the need for continual clarity in the way communicate. It’s easy to use jargon or insider phrases that mean something to us, but may not be as clear to the end user.

experiments.title: How the use of a video in a Facebook ad affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video ad increase conversion rate over an image ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2017 - 08/07/2017
Buckner International was promoting a new guide on how to talk to your kids about poverty. The goal of the campaign was to serve as an email acquisition mechanism to grow their house file. They had created a promotional video to be used in advertising but we wanted to test this new medium against the standard control which was an image of the offer.
They wanted to see whether including the video instead of an image would increase people’s motivation to download the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: The video ad saw an increased conversion rate of nearly 60%. We did not yet reach statistical validity but at a 94% level of confidence, it was quickly approaching this. It is our hypothesis that this is likely because the video lined up more with the ad content and the storyline, creating an emotional connection with the topic that the offer was covering. This increased people’s motivation to download the offer.

experiments.title: How supporting copy length on a Facebook ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing the length of the supporting ad copy increase clickthrough rate on a facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2017 - 12/12/2017
Oral arguments for Jack Phillips–a client of Alliance Defending Freedom–began on December 5th. As the news covered this national story, Alliance Defending Freedom decided to launch a Facebook ad campaign urging people to sign a letter of support for Jack. Since they knew some people were unfamiliar with Jack, they wondered: Will increasing the length of supporting ad copy on a facebook ad increase clickthrough rate? To test this, they created a longer treatment and launched an A/B test to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Alliance Defending Freedom that there was no statistical difference in email acquisition between the control and treatment ad. As we look at this test, it’s important to consider the timing of this campaign. Due to the relevant and captivating nature of this material, this campaign experienced a well above-average clickthrough and email acquisition rate. It’s likely that this played a factor in the A/B test (i.e. people were already pretty motivated to clickthrough, so there was less room for incremental optimization).

experiments.title: How an exit intent with a thermometer and an exit intent with social proof affects donor conversion rate during Calendar Year End
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because for the donor goal thermometer version, the image would highlight what the user could do to fill the gap, and the social proof image would show how the user could make an impact along with the other people that have already made an impact, encouraging the user to help CB meet the match.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
During their 2-day matches, CaringBridge usually has an exit intent that appears on-site profiles, featuring language that reflects the urgency of the match and how the user can help CaringBridge reach its goal.
During this particular match at the end of the calendar year, we wanted to try an A/B/C test with different exit intents that used varying visuals on the exit intent.
The first treatment, which had an exit intent, featured a thermometer image, showing how the user could help fill the gap.
The second treatment, exit intent, featured an image of people, highlighting how they could help reach the goal among the community of people who had already contributed.
This was tested on both mobile and desktop devices, as well as on-site profiles.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an exit intent with a donor goal thermometer image and social proof image for site profile visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rate. The treatment showed an 8.2% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the visuals used in the exit intent may have had a positive impact on user engagement.

There was an increase in revenue for both mobile and desktop, which indicated that the social proof image variant was a clear winner. For desktop, there was a 19.37% increase in revenue. For mobile, there was a 24% increase in revenue.

Control (desktop) - $7,208

Treatment 1(desktop) - $7,515

Treatment 2 (desktop) - $8,770

Control (mobile) - $9,686

Treatment 1 (mobile) - $10,665

Treatment 2 (mobile) - $12,200

So the conclusion for this test is that while both variants produced an almost similar amount of donations, the variant with the social proof image encouraged users to give higher gifts, resulting in an overall increase in revenue.

experiments.title: How Meta Ad Campaign Optimization Goals Affect the Cost of Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing Meta ads for Maximum ROAS for a mixed acquisition and retargeting audience will achieve higher ROI than optimizing for Purchases, because focusing the algorithm on revenue-per-dollar spent may prioritize higher-value donors, even if it reduces overall conversion volume.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2025 - 12/15/2025
To better understand how Meta campaign optimization goals impact both giving behavior and campaign efficiency, Human Coalition tested two different optimization strategies. The control group optimized for Purchases—Meta’s default goal of maximizing the number of tracked donation conversions. The treatment group optimized for maximum Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)—Meta’s strategy that seeks to maximize the total dollar value returned for every ad dollar spent.
The team hypothesized that ROAS (Return on Ad Spend) optimization might reduce the number of conversions but increase the average gift size enough to improve overall ROI. This trade-off between conversion volume and gift value is central to the donor motivation question: Are there optimization strategies that attract higher-value donors, even at lower volume?
Both groups used the same ad creatives, daily budgets, and targeting parameters, isolating only the optimization goal as the variable under test. This test aimed to surface insights on how optimization settings shape both donor behavior and bottom-line campaign performance.
experiments.keyLearnings: Optimizing for Maximum ROAS did not outperform Purchase optimization in this campaign. While the average gift in the ROAS-optimized group was higher ($49.27 vs. $38.24), the overall performance suffered due to a sharp decline in donation volume, with the treatment group generating only 39 gifts compared to 175 in the control. As a result, the gross cost per donor more than doubled in the treatment group ($213.70 vs. $86.17), and overall ROI decreased. The treatment group saw a 2.5% decrease in Donations for All Traffic, which was not significant at a 12.0% level of confidence. Therefore, the hypothesis that focusing on revenue per dollar spent would attract higher-value donors, potentially improving overall ROI, was not supported by the data. This suggests that Meta’s ROAS optimization may struggle to scale donation volume for nonprofit campaigns, especially when the audience includes a large proportion of new or cold prospects. While ROAS optimization likely prioritized users predicted to give larger gifts, the reduced reach or conversion frequency significantly undercut overall efficiency. We learned that gift value alone cannot offset a steep drop in conversions—at least in this audience and campaign context. Optimizing for total donor volume may remain the more effective strategy when the primary goal is acquisition, particularly if follow-up engagement can increase lifetime value over time. These results prompt further questions: Could ROAS optimization be more effective with warmer audiences or higher average gift campaigns? Are there hybrid approaches (e.g., campaign-level ROAS goals with ad set-level Purchase optimization) that could better balance value and volume?

experiments.title: Can gift array preselection increase revenue for paid social content offers with persuasion-heavy messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom, relying on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that can increase revenue per visitor while maintaining healthy conversion rates across acquisition channels.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection influence conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($ 25, $50, and $100, and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This read focuses on the “Trickle Down” content offer, which typically runs on paid social and attracts a more prospect-heavy audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the “Trickle Down” offer, results were directional and not statistically valid, and performance patterns diverged from aggregate findings.

📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 1,085
 	Total conversions: 50
 	Overall conversion rate: 4.6%
 	Overall average gift: $45.30
 	Control ($50/100/250, no select):
 	Conversion rate: 4.3%
 	Average gift: $39.58
 	$25/50/100, no select:
 	Conversion rate: 4.7%
 	Average gift: $52.31
 	Revenue per visitor: $2.44
 	$50/100/250, select 100:
 	Conversion rate: 5.4% (+25.5% vs. control)
 	Average gift: $56.33
 	Revenue per visitor: $3.06 (highest variant)
 	$25/50/100, select 50:
 	Conversion rate: 4.0%
 	Average gift: $26.50
Offer and channel context
Traffic is driven primarily by paid social
 	Sample size is small relative to required validity
 	Contrary to aggregate trends, preselecting the higher gift array performed best on both conversion rate and revenue per visitor
 	This deviation may be influenced by the nature of the offer or donation page copy, which could be priming donors toward higher gift amounts
Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significance
 	Confidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation

experiments.title: Do minimalist ads for newsletter signups improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that simplified, headline-style ad creative with a clear call to action would outperform a more visually complex layout in generating donations. The assumption was that complexity could reduce persuasive impact in mission-driven creative by requiring more cognitive effort to understand.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2025 - 04/25/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether simplified, headline-style ad creative would outperform more complex designs in Meta ads for donor acquisition. The test was inspired by recent behavioral research from the Journal of Consumer Research that found simple, minimalist advertising often outperforms complex designs. While more detailed ads may be perceived as higher effort, simplicity allows messages to be processed more fluently and emotionally, leading to higher engagement.

experiments.title: Does reducing friction on a premium landing page affect conversion rate for Meta Ads?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that removing premium-related fields and focusing the page experience around the donation itself would reduce friction and increase conversion rates and cost efficiency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/08/2025
This experiment was conducted for WUNC, a public radio station serving North Carolina, to evaluate whether reducing friction on a landing page would improve donor conversion. The test was run through Meta ads that featured a premium mug. The control ad linked to a general donation page, while the test ad linked to a simplified landing page that included an image of the mug but removed fields and copy related to premiums.

experiments.title: Does a targeted lookalike audience convert at a higher rate than a broad lookalike audience on Meta?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using a lookalike audience based on past donors from economic content offers would improve cost efficiency in donor acquisition because the audience would be more aligned with historical donor behavior.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2025 - 02/12/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The goal was to evaluate the impact of audience targeting strategies on Meta by testing a broad lookalike audience against a more targeted lookalike audience based on previous donors from economic content offers. The objective was to determine which approach would yield better cost efficiency for donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding a CTA to a Facebook Ad impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a CTA to the ad creative for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in donations and click through rate because the purpose of the ad is clearer and therefore more motivated to download.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s offers is Rule of Life a special guide to use as you chart out your spiritual “growth plan.”
We wanted to test adding the CTA on the ad creative as well as adding a colored background to see if this impacted the donation rate, click through rate, and email acquisition. By encouraging people at the beginning of the funnel to download the ebook – our hypothesis was that more people would click on the ad and therefore donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a call-to-action (CTA) to the ad creative can significantly increase donations and click-through rates for Avila Foundation. The treatment group, which had the CTA added to the ad creative, saw an impressive 81.4% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was at 79.3%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact from adding the CTA, they cannot be considered statistically valid and should not be used for making future decisions.
It also had an impact on how many downloaded the offer:
There was a 13.7% increase in signups on the treatment; with a 76.5% level of confidence.
Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other elements such as different CTA language, colors, or placements to further optimize donation rates and click-through rates.

experiments.title: How using a more provocative title for the subject of a survey impacts Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using an more provocative name for the Administrative State for survey Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks on the ad because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions. because the term “Deep State” invokes stronger emotions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2024 - 01/08/2025
The Heritage Foundation was running a “Stop the Administrative State” survey and promoting through Facebook ads. In an attempt to increase click rates on the ads, they wanted to test whether using the more provocative term “Deep State” in place of the more straightforward “Administrative State” survey title would achieve this effect because people would be more provoked to take action against the subject when referred to as a more negative term.
The control’s Facebook ad image and copy referred to the Administrative State, while the treatment’s ad image and copy referred to the Deep State, and both received 50% of traffic. In addition, the control led to survey landing page with “Administrative State” language, and the treatment led to a survey page with “Deep State” language for continuity.
This test ran for 3 weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using the term "Deep State" in place of "Administrative State" did not lead to an increase in clicks on the Facebook ad. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is only 15.9%, these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In addition to click rate, the treatment saw a valid 12.8% decrease in sign up rate on the landing page at a 97% level of confidence. This suggests that the term "Deep State" may have led to fewer people completing the survey.

Finally, the treatment saw a 8.5% decrease in donor acquisition rate on the post-survey landing page compared to the control, but at a 18.6% level of confidence, this result cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully design the study with a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer period of time to increase the statistical power. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or messaging strategies to determine what resonates best with the audience. Conducting thorough data analysis and ensuring statistical significance will help in making informed decisions based on experimental results.

experiments.title: Does adding social proof on a landing page affect the conversion rate on an instant donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding social proof language (e.g., “___ people downloaded this ebook – Will you?”) to landing pages would increase donor conversion rates on the instant donation page by reinforcing the value and credibility of the content/Hoover.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. The test evaluated the impact of social proof language on landing pages for content offers, focusing specifically on its effect on donor conversion rates. The social proof language highlighted the number of downloads for each offer to determine whether this messaging could encourage more donations.

experiments.title: What happens when you replace an ad with a dear reader
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing an ad style cta with a dear reader for users that are on the article pages will achieve a higher donor converstion rate because a dear reader contains more value proposition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2024 - 04/16/2024
Focus on the Family receives a ton of traffic to their article pages every day. In an effort to further engage this audience there are several ad style CTAs on the page to lead users to different video series or content offers. We wondered if we could test an “ad style” cta with a dear reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that replacing an ad style CTA with a "dear reader" message on article pages resulted in a 32.6% increase in revenue for all traffic. This indicates that although more people don't convert when seeing the dear reader, they do give more generously when they get to the donation page.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of the "dear reader" message against other CTAs or variations. By conducting further experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, more conclusive insights can be gained to inform decisions related to optimizing donor conversion rates on article pages.

experiments.title: How adding a testimonial box with a social proof headline impacts donor conversions during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a testimonial box with the headline “Make an Impact on readers like __” to the right side of FamilyLife’s donation page will increase donor conversions by providing social proof and illustrating the tangible impact of donations, thereby fostering trust and emotional connection with potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2024 - 12/31/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization dedicated to providing biblically-based resources and support to families. This experiment was conducted during their high-urgency calendar year-end campaign, a critical time for driving donor engagement and conversions.
Historically, FamilyLife’s donation pages have focused on clearly presenting the campaign’s messaging and donation options without additional elements like testimonials. For this test, we wanted to explore whether incorporating social proof could enhance donor trust and urgency.
To test this, we added a testimonial box featuring a real quote from a FamilyLife supporter, paired with a social proof headline: “Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!” This headline aimed to create a sense of community and inspire donors to contribute by showcasing the tangible impact of their support.
The Control was the standard donation page without a testimonial, and the Treatment included the testimonial box prominently placed near the donation form. This was a split A/B test built in VWO with 50/50 split traffic, ensuring an equal number of users experienced both the Control and the Treatment.
The goal of this test was to determine whether the addition of social proof could increase donor conversion rates during this critical fundraising period.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Treatment featuring the testimonial box with the headline, "Make an Impact on Readers like Giovanni and Alana!", led to a 6.2% decrease in transactions compared to the Control at a 70% level of confidence (LoC). While the results did not validate due to insufficient data over the testing period, the downward trend suggests that the testimonial box may have negatively impacted conversions.

We hypothesize that the addition of the testimonial box could have distracted users from focusing on the primary goal: completing the donation. While the testimonial and headline aimed to build trust and provide social proof, it may have introduced unnecessary cognitive load or drawn attention away from the donation form itself. Alternatively, the headline or the quote could have lacked relevance or resonance for some users, reducing its effectiveness as a motivator.

Next Steps:

Given the negative trend, we recommend retesting this concept with adjustments:
Rework the Design: Explore a more streamlined and less visually dominant placement for the testimonial box.
 	Refine the Content: Test different quotes or headlines that may better resonate with the audience or align more closely with the campaign’s urgency.
 	Alternative Social Proof Strategies: Consider integrating testimonials in a subtler way, such as below the donation form or as part of an impact statement, to avoid distracting users.

experiments.title: How changing ad copy impacted CTR on Facebook ads
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the copy to be shorter with called out value for Avila’s Facebook audience will achieve an increase in CTR because the engager understand the value better.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/13/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke, Into the Deep. For this Facebook ad test, we wanted to change the copy to be shorter and focused on their relationship with the Lord. Describing more of the positive impact that the engaged would have with the course.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that updating the copy to be shorter with a focus on the value Avila Foundation provides for its Facebook audience resulted in a 7.2% increase in Clicks for All Traffic. This indicates that the audience may have responded positively to the more concise message highlighting the relationship with the Lord that the course offers. However, since the statistical confidence level is below 95%, these results should not be considered valid for future decision making.
The cost per registration/email acquisition had a 8.8% increase with a 46% loc.
Control:
sample size: 7271
emails: 99
Treatment:
sample size: 6951
emails: 103
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of messaging that emphasizes the value of the courses in drawing Christians into deeper union with Christ. It may be beneficial to test variations of the copy further to see if a more significant increase in engagement can be achieved. Additionally, increasing the sample size or running the experiment for a longer period of time may help to gather more reliable data for making informed decisions about messaging strategies.

experiments.title: Do Meta Suggested Image Optimizations really increase engagement?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic will achieve higher engagement because Facebook said it would.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV runs regular content offers and direct asks on Facebook (Meta). Meta has been urging marketers to use its image optimization suggestions, which it promises will significantly increase engagement and, therefore, donations.
As experimenters, we are skeptical.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that implementing Meta image optimizations for Facebook traffic did not lead to higher engagement for Pathway to Victory's content offers and direct asks. In fact, the treatment group saw a 1.5% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 60.8%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 90% for validity.
Purchases saw a 8% increase but only 62% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is important for Pathway to Victory to not solely rely on platform suggestions or promises when conducting experiments. It is essential to rigorously test any changes or optimizations before making decisions based on them. Additionally, future experimentation should aim to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the reliability of the results. It may be beneficial to continue testing different strategies and optimizations to find what works best for engaging with the audience and driving donations.

experiments.title: Does using Yes/NO ads to promote a new premium offer impact donor conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using yes/no ads for a Facebook audience will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2023 - 11/06/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry arm of First Baptist Dallas, one of the largest Baptist churches in the country.
For a new resource that was created to respond to questions PTV had been getting with the chaos in Israel, we wanted to test whether using yes/no creative would make an impact on donor conversions. The yes/no concept is something PTV had used on organic posts; we had not tested this before for Facebook ads.
In this experiment, which we ran as adding an additional creative element to a campaign, not a straight A/B test, we tested the control which was an image of the book cover against the yes/no ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using yes/no ads for a Facebook audience did not result in a significant change in conversion rates. The treatment actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

While CPMs across both creative variations were the same, there was a slight decrease in CTR for the variant (1.09% compared to 0.93%). However the cost to acquire a donor on the yes/no variant was 16% higher.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests using different ad formats, messaging, or targeting strategies to see if there are any other variables that could impact conversion rates. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other channels or platforms to reach the target audience. Experimentation is crucial for understanding what strategies work best for increasing conversions, and it is important to evaluate results with a statistically significant level of confidence before making any conclusive decisions.

experiments.title: How using a static image compares to using a video for a course promotion on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the creative for potential course takes will achieve a change in sign-up rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2024 - 06/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of their most popular courses is a video series from Dan Burke. For this Facebook ad creative test, we wanted to see how the long-running control that featured a trailer from the course would perform against a static image with a ‘free course’ call out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which featured a static image with a 'free course' call out, resulted in a 76.0% increase in Complete Registrations for All Traffic compared to the control.
2. The Cost per Click experienced a 92% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, and the Cost per Registration experienced a 185% decrease with a 100% level of confidence for the treatment.
3. Visitors in the 55-64 age group showed a 13% increase in complete registrations with a 96% level of confidence for the treatment.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for the increase in Complete Registrations is only 63.2%, which is below the threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the treatment with a larger sample size to determine if the increase in Complete Registrations is consistent and statistically significant. Additionally, exploring different creatives and messaging strategies could help identify the most effective approach for driving course sign-ups. As the Cost per Click and Cost per Registration decreased significantly for the treatment, it may be worth investigating why this occurred and if similar cost-saving measures can be implemented in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post as a paid Facebook ad with a brighter color background affects click-through rate and other downstream conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad instead of a darker color for Facebook users will achieve an increase in click through rate and decrease in cost per click because the brighter color background will create a sense of vibrancy and attention-grabbing effect, capturing users’ focus and prompting them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2023
We have recently tested Facebook ads for this ministry client, which was an experiment between organic vs. normal graphic ads. We found that the organic post led to a higher click-through rate and donor conversion. Knowing that, we then wanted to conduct an A/B test between two different organic Facebook ads. The control Facebook organic ad was the original organic ad, with simple white text on a dark-colored navy background. Our treatment for this experiment was the same simple white text but placed on a brighter color teal background. We predict that the brighter color background can create a sense of urgency and stand out to the Facebook user, leading to an increase in click-through rate. The copy on both ads is the same.
This was an A/B test with 50/50 split in traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a brighter color background for the Facebook ad did not perform better in terms of Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Cost Per Click (CPC). The treatment group, which used the brighter color background, actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this decrease in clicks was only 10.1%, which did not validate since it was not above a 95% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test other variables or factors that could impact CTR and CPC, such as ad copy - this can include ad copy length, font, size, and even emojis/symbols.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson for donation page vistors will achieve more transactions because there is social proof that what ACI does is approved by Trump.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2024 - 05/15/2024
ACI’s Dr. Carson has a relationship with former president Trump. Since ACI’s audience is mainly conservatives, we wanted to try using a quote from Trump about Dr. Carson on a few of ACI’s instant donation pages. We created a sticky bar that had the quote and a photo of Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson on the donation page did not result in an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only 38.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide valid and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the audience and their preferences when incorporating endorsements or quotes from public figures. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies or elements to understand what resonates best with ACI's conservative audience. A larger sample size and further testing could provide more conclusive insights for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How does a video as creative in a Facebook ad impact donor conversion

rows.name: PTV DA Beacon of Hope Matching Challenge
PTV DA Beacon of Hope Matching Challenge video

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video with captions for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because more information is provided in the video and a potential donor can have a higher motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each June and December, PTV conducts a matching challenge to encourage donors to give.
For the June 2024 campaign, we decide to run two different ad variants to see which would perform better, a static image vs a video with captions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which involved using a video with captions, resulted in an 11.2% increase in Purchases for All Traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that the treatment had a positive impact on donor conversion rates.

2. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 42.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and it is not advisable to make future decisions based on this experiment alone.

3. Clicks saw a significant increase of 152% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that the video with captions was more engaging and attracted more viewers to click on the ad.

4. The Cost per Click experienced a 65% decrease with a 100% level of confidence, showing that the treatment was more cost-effective in terms of generating clicks.

5. Female visitors showed a remarkable 657% increase in purchases with a 96% level of confidence. This suggests that the video with captions resonated particularly well with female audiences.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. Ensure that the sample size is sufficient to produce statistically significant results with a confidence level of at least 85%. This will help in making more reliable conclusions.

2. Continue to explore the use of videos with captions in advertising campaigns, especially for targeting female audiences, as it showed promising results in this experiment.

3. Take into account all the metrics analyzed in this experiment, such as Clicks and Cost per Click, to gauge the overall effectiveness of different ad formats.

4. Conduct further research and experimentation to validate the initial findings and make informed decisions on the best strategies to improve donor conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to an instant donation page through a FOMO slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2024 - 06/25/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running a free ebook offer through multiple paid advertising channels.
They had previously tested a FOMO slideout on their ‘Renew Membership’ donation page but did not see any valid results in terms of donor conversion. They wanted to test how this concept would perform on an acquisition donation page.
The FOMO slideout pulled the most recent donations from that donation page and slid out every 10 seconds showing a different donor’s first name and state saying they just donated to The Heritage Foundation.
This test was ran for 20 days and traffic was split 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a FOMO slideout for ebook downloaders did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a slight decrease in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a statistical level of confidence of only 2.3%. This means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to detect any significant differences between the control and treatment groups. It is also crucial to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or the targeting of the paid advertising channels. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the FOMO slideout on different types of pages or with different messaging to see if it has a different impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How using a testimonial in a Facebook ad affects CTR
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a testimonial for facebook users will achieve a higher click-through-rate because it adds credibility to the advertisement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2024 - 05/31/2024
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research center and think tank located at Stanford University in California, United States. The Property Rights content offer has a low click-through rate, and the team wants to improve its performance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which included a testimonial for Facebook users, resulted in a 12.7% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic with a 54.0% statistical level of confidence. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision-making.

2. The Cost per Click experienced a significant 470% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that the treatment may not be cost-effective compared to the control.

3. The 25-34 age group saw a substantial 1000% decrease in clicks with a 98% level of confidence when exposed to the treatment. This suggests that the addition of a testimonial may not resonate well with this demographic.

Based on these findings, it is clear that the addition of a testimonial for Facebook users did not yield positive results in terms of Clicks for All Traffic, Cost per Click, and engagement from the 25-34 age group.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider refining the messaging of the testimonial, targeting specific audience segments, or testing different types of social proof to see if it positively impacts the performance of the Property Rights content offer. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that the sample size is sufficient and that the statistical confidence levels are high enough to draw valid conclusions for decision-making.

experiments.title: How does using ad set budget rather than campaign in Facebook impact conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by setting the budget at the ad set level for Facebook will achieve a higher conversion rate because the budget is spend in a more controlled manner between ad sets.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2024 - 02/29/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
PTV uses Facebook as a channel to retain donors, react to lapsed donors, and acquire new donors.
In an effort to increase Facebook performance we decided to test setting the budget at the campaign level vs the ad set level. Historically PTV has set the budget on the campaign level to allow Facebook optimization authority but we were curious if we could see better results with a more controlled approach with the ad set.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that setting the budget at the ad set level for Facebook resulted in a 3.7% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 25.1%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity. This means that while the treatment version appeared to be more effective in terms of purchases, the results cannot be confidently attributed to the budget setting.
Additionally, the Cost per Click experienced a significant 105% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that setting the budget at the ad set level may have resulted in higher costs for each click, which is an important factor to consider when evaluating the overall effectiveness of the strategy.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is sufficiently high to draw meaningful conclusions. It may also be beneficial to further investigate the impact of setting the budget at the ad set level on other key metrics, such as return on ad spend, to get a more comprehensive understanding of the strategy's effectiveness. Further testing with larger sample sizes or different variables may also help to validate the findings and inform future decision-making regarding budget allocation on Facebook.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the AVID Pathways connection for AVID clients showed a slight increase in match rate compared to the traditional CSV upload method. While this result suggests that there may be potential benefits to utilizing AVID Pathways for connecting with donors on Facebook, the statistical level of confidence at 15.9% is not strong enough to draw conclusive findings.
For this experiment, we utilized 2 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
Offline only donorsOnline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For offline donors, we saw a 1.7% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 82%For only donors, we saw a 1.2% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 99%
While these segments did see very slight decreases, we are confident that the continuous flow of information provide via AVID Pathways from multiple data sources will make up the decrease.
As AVID Pathways data continues to improve we expect these match rates to increase over time.
We will check back in on rate in a few months to confirm if match rates have improved.

experiments.title: How call to action on a Facebook ad during a drive impacts donor conversions

rows.name: NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Learn More
NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Donate Now

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by using the Donate Now call to action for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because the call to action is clear.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. During the Spring Drive, we decided to test a Learn More vs Donate Now call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that using a clear call to action like "Donate Now" led to a 16.2% increase in purchases for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.4%, which is below the recommended 85% level, and thus, the results cannot be considered valid. Additionally, the experiment revealed that using "Donate Now" led to a significant increase in Cost per Click, with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while this clear call to action may lead to more conversions, it may also lead to higher costs.

In the future, WUNC should conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and higher levels of statistical significance before making any definitive decisions about changing their call to action. They should also consider the tradeoff between higher conversion rates and increased costs. It may be helpful to explore other variations of the call to action, such as "Donate Today" or "Make a Donation," to see if they lead to similar conversion rates without significantly increasing costs. Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of using data and statistical significance to validate results before making any changes to marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How Facebook creative impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the creative to the authors photo for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because the authors are well known to the Hoover audience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2023 - 08/25/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
Hoover runs many offers through Facebook and therefore we wanted to test a new approach to creative for ebooks. Hoover has many well known personalities that write the offers shared so we decided to test the standard 3d image of the offer vs an image with the authors photo.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The treatment (using the authors' photo as the creative) resulted in a decrease in purchases for all traffic, indicating that it did not perform as well as the control (using a standard 3d image).

2. The level of confidence for the decrease in purchases is only 3.2%, which is below the acceptable threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

3. However, there were significant increases in other metrics such as clicks and complete registrations. Clicks increased by 184% with a 99% level of confidence, and complete registrations increased by 225% with a 95% level of confidence.

4. Cost per click and cost per registration also saw significant increases, with a 424% increase and a 376% increase respectively, both with a level of confidence of 100%.

Based on these findings, future experimentation could focus on leveraging the authors' photos in the creative for specific goals such as increasing clicks and complete registrations. However, it should be noted that the decrease in purchases suggests that using authors' photos may not be effective in driving actual conversions. Therefore, further experiments should be conducted to explore the impact on conversion rates and validity of results. Additionally, it could be valuable to assess the overall impact on campaign performance and return on investment when considering changes to the creative strategy.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a ebook acquisition page affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof for online course enrollees will achieve a higher email sign up rate because the social proof shows the user that there are others who have downloaded the ebook and it offers them a chance to join a group of others with similar interests.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client is offering a free online course on the book of Psalms through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to increase the email sign-up rate on the landing page, so we decided to test adding the concept of social proof. We added social proof by adding a floating white box that says “We’ve already had over 3,000 people sign up for this online course! Will you join the group of people who are expanding their knowledge of the Book of Psalms?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding social proof for the online course resulted in a 10.1% increase in email acquisitions with a 75.1% level of confidence. This indicates that social proof has a positive impact on encouraging users to sign up.

2. When analyzing the results based on gender, it was found that female visitors had a significant 681% increase in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that the addition of social proof had a strong and positive impact specifically on female visitors.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to validate the impact of social proof on overall email sign-up rates.

experiments.title: How social proof vs. credibility impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of credibility for survey takers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it will build trust in the value proposition and give the donor more reasons to believe our claims.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2023 - 06/22/2023
In a previous test, we saw that adding a quote from a current volunteer board member produced a lift in donations. We wondered if adding a statement of credibility using the same format would make any difference in conversion. We pulled a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization and put it in the same placement on the page as an element of social proof right before the donation form and split traffic 50/50, sending half of visitors to the control with Ken’s quote about donating and the other half to the treatment with the Forbes magazine call-out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment aimed to determine if adding a statement of credibility before the donation form would increase donor conversion. The treatment included a quote from Forbes magazine commending the organization, while the control had a quote from a volunteer board member. The experiment ran for 28 days, and the results showed that the treatment had a 53.8% decrease in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.9% and thus cannot be considered valid.

From this experiment, we can learn that adding a statement of credibility may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion. It is essential to conduct further experimentation to confirm the validity of the initial results. What we saw was the audience was more motivated by a donor-centric quote from a supporter regarding their experience with giving to the organization than they were by the organizational-centric quote from a review that gave an exclusive reason to support the organization. One reason for this could be the difference in the positioning of the language in the quotes "Let's go get 'em" which insinuates there is work left to be done versus the treatment that claims the organization has the "largest communication presence" which could lead donors to believe that the organization does not need their help or support since they are already doing the most/best with their existing resources and are already in a position to be successful without the donor making a gift to help.

experiments.title: How language specific to existing contacts impacted Facebook clicks

rows.name: FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member control
FBD 350 - Join Us Reach - May 14 member treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by making ad copy specific to FBD contacts for existing contacts on Facebook will achieve a higher conversion in clicks because they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas. because  they are more personalized to their relationship with First Dallas.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/11/2023 - 05/14/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Leading up to Sunday services, First Dallas utilizes Facebook to share what the sermon will be and invites people to join in person.
This campaign also targets existing First Dallas contacts through the HubSpot/Facebook integration. We decided to test and see if copy that is specific to this audience would increase clicks on the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test if making ad copy specific to First Baptist Dallas's existing contacts on Facebook would result in a higher conversion in clicks. The treatment resulted in a 2.4% increase in clicks for all traffic, but with only a 12.2% statistical level of confidence, the results cannot be considered valid.
During the experiment, the connection between HubSpot and Facebook was broken. This led to the experiment not spending its full budget or reaching its highest potential. We will rerun this experiment in the coming weeks to seek valid results.

experiments.title: How a simplified Facebook image impacts click converstions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by simplifying the image and removing friction  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve higher click conversion because the ad will highlight the important aspects of the sermon.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/20/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks, the Facebook ad performance has been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image that has Dr. Jeffress’s image removed and the sermon title removed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment tested the effectiveness of using an ad with a simplified image and message versus an ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress. The hypothesis was that the simplified ad would achieve higher click conversions by reducing friction. However, the results showed that the control ad with more details and an image featuring Dr. Jeffress performed better, with a 4.3% increase in clicks for all traffic at a 61.1% level of confidence.
Since the level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, the results are not considered valid, and future decisions should not be based on them. There are some potential insights that can be gleaned from the experiment, such as the effectiveness of including details about the sermon and featuring Dr. Jeffress in the ad. Future experiments could build upon these insights to test different messaging and images to see if they can improve ad performance.

experiments.title: How the Learn More vs. Donate Button on Facebook Led to a Higher Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Learn More” vs. the “Donate Now” button on Facebook for potential donors  will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because asking the potential donor to Learn More vs. immediately donate reduces friction..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2023
Prison Fellowship International works to transform the lives of prisons, their families and victims through a global network of community partners. In recent months we have been showcasing the support a child program for the children of prisoners that is more general than 1:1 child sponsorship.  Programmatically it costs less to maintain while allowing more children to participate.  The ads were not converting as many donors as we wanted to see. The current ads running on Facebook were asking the potential donor to select the “Donate Now” button to move to the Donation Page sharing additional information.  We have used the “Learn More” button in Facebook and achieved higher results.  We conducted an A/B test using the Learn More Button vs. Donate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Learn More" button instead of the "Donate Now" button on Facebook for potential donors may lead to a higher donor conversion rate due to reduced friction. The treatment resulted in a 14.5% increase in donations for all traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was 22% and below the 85% necessary to validate the experiment. Because the overall traffic is low, we recommend moving forward with the treatment as it did produce a higher conversion rate and we have used this as a control button on child sponsorship.

experiments.title: How a simplified image impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the content on the image for Facebook viewers will achieve an increase in clicks because it is less friction and simplified information.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2023
First Baptist Dallas is a church located in the heart of downtown Dallas, Texas. Each week First Dallas uses Facebook ads to share the upcoming Sunday’s message and sermon series.
Over the last few weeks the Facebook ad performance as been declining. In an effort to increase performance, we decided to test the traditional ad with an image with sermon details vs an ad with a simplified image of only the sermon series title.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment indicates that simplifying language and images on Facebook ads may not be the best way to achieve increased clicks. Our hypothesis was that by reducing information on the image and content, Facebook viewer engagement would increase. However, as demonstrated in this experiment, clicks were actually 6.1% lower when a simplified image of only the sermon series title was used.
It is important to remember that although the control won in this experiment, the statistical confidence was too low to be considered valid. This means the experiment has not completely invalidated our hypothesis and the test of simpler ad copy and imagery may still be worth experimenting with again in the future.
As a result, the key learning from this experiment is that more experimentation should be done in regards to messaging, images, and ad copy in order to find out what performs best. It is important to conduct experiments often and on a consistent basis in order to stay updated on changes in Facebook ad performance and understand what works best at any given time.

experiments.title: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed through our welcome series

rows.name: Control - Ad Click to a Hosted Landing Page/Form

experiments.researchQuestion: How leads generated through Facebook Lead Ad Forms performed (from an engagement status) once they made it through the welcome series of emails to onboard them as a new subscriber.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/20/2022
Recently, we experimented with using Facebook Lead Ads for our own acquisition campaign efforts, which we discovered that we could increase email signup rates by +131.5% (LoC: 100%), but the real question was “How would these leads perform downstream.”
We marked two check-in points, which were:
To review unsubscribe, bounce, disengagement, and never-opener rates after all leads generated in the experiment have “completed the welcome series.” And,
60-days downstream to see if either lead segment produced a more engaged subscriber well after the welcome series had ended, and they were introduced to 2-months of “live messaging.”
This study is focused on the first of these two check-in points, so we wanted to review how the leads performed now that all leads in the study had enough time to make it through the welcome series of emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that we're through the "welcome series" of messaging, we could not fully validate an increase in email engagement rates, but do see a slight improvement on subscriber engagement from the treatment segment.

This is actually very encouraging news, as it indicates that there is no discernible difference between using the control lead generation experience when compared to using the Facebook Lead Ad Form treatment approach.

To put it another way: The treatment experience has increased email signup rates by +131.5% (with 100% level of confidence), but HAS NOT produced a lower quality of email subscriber (at least through the point where all leads have successfully made it through the "welcome series" for new subscribers).

Perhaps this means that the hypothesis that Facebook Lead Ad Forms generate "bad emails" is debunked — at least in our own case.

We'll continue to monitor performance and look forward to checking back in at the 60-day mark from the point of when these emails were acquired to see if there is any noticeable difference between the quality of the two approaches to generating leads.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an instant donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign on an instant donation page for those who sign up for a course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/07/2022 - 06/03/2022
Reasons to Believe initially ran a radical redesign experiment on their main donation page that provided a significant lift in donor conversion rate. We wanted to see if this same radical redesign applied to an instant donation page to an offer, would result in the same type of lift. The motivation behind someone giving on a main donation page (someone who comes to the page with the intention to give) differs from someone who comes across a donation page after acquiring a resource. We applied this radical redesign to a funnel that was already getting a high instant conversion rate (Listen & Learn, an online course).
Most of the traffic directed to this page was through paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results for this experiment did not validate even after running for a significant amount of time. We saw a 51% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 63.9%. Because this has already validated on the main donation page and because it is a better experience for the end-user, we decided to role out the treatment to 100% of the audience anyway. It could be that this funnel was not getting enough traffic through the sign up page in order for this experiment to validate.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 186.2% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a video in an ad for Facebook users will achieve higher click rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2022 - 02/27/2022
In an effort to increase click through rates on a FB ad promoting a survey, we wanted to know if presenting the issue through video format would provide a lift in click through rates. We hypothesized that by offering a different format that presented the narrative in video form, rather than text, more viewers would stop to interact with the ad and hopefully click through
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we were able to see a 49% lift in click through rates. This indicates that the video format stood out in people's feeds and presented the issue in a way that inspired viewers to click through.

However, due to the low number of impressions received by the two ads, we were unable to validate this experiment. The treatment received only $19.37. We also noted that the video format was not the only ad element that was changed. The body text was also shortened and revised to use more urgent language that compelled viewers to act.

In future experiments, we may want to distribute the spend between control and treatment more evenly and ensure enough spend is designated to provide sufficient traffic. If we can validate the lift provided by the video format, we can then test the copy variations against one another.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and more clarity to a landing page affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity to the headline and adding social proof in the form of a testimonial would result in a higher number of email conversions for people that land on this campaign landing page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 06/28/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof in the form of a testimonial, and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around a 27% for the treatment.

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 28%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof in the form of a testimonial were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How aligning the graphic elements from an ad with the graphic elements on a landing page affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that matching the creative assets from the ad running on social media to the graphics on the landing page will result in higher conversions for visitors to the email landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs).
It is common for this team to update the ad creative in Facebook if we see that the ad conversion rates could use improvement. It is not as common to also have the graphics from the campaign match exactly the images on the landing page. While the change in creative helped the ad conversion rates at the “top of the funnel”, we noticed the signup conversion rates on this campaign (in the “middle of the funnel”) began to decline after making the creative switch. We felt this was an opportunity to test creative congruence between the new ad creative and the existing landing page.
For this particular experiment, in the treatment, we changed out creative assets on the email sign up page to match that of the new ad creative running on Facebook, and we made a slight tweak to the headline copy to add more clarity. The control was left as is.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate. While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw very little difference between the control and the treatment. In fact, the control (with a different graphic treatment between the ad and the landing page) was slightly ahead on email conversions (by just 3).

Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and graphic treatment). While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 10%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the graphic treatment caused the small decrease in conversions. We can say that we gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages. In the end, there were really no positive or adverse effects to making the creative match.

For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and headline clarity affect email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and headline clarity  for readers of a landing page will achieve a higher number of conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/10/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding social proof under a landing page image and more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we did see a small lift of around 2% for the treatment with a 20% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and social proof).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 20%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the social proof were the cause of the lift. We can say that we did see an increase in conversions, and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How a “Learn More” CTA on a Facebook Ad Impacted Donor Conversion When Compared with “Donate Now.”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “Donate Now” CTA on a Facebook Ad will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2022 - 04/29/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if changing the text on the call to action button on an ad would increase donor conversion. Specifically, if saying “donate now” instead of “learn more” would increase donor conversion on the ad. When we tested “Donate Now” with another organization, it increased donor conversion, so KCBI hoped this would work with their audience as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, due to a low donor response, we really didn't have any statistical level of significance for donations or cost per click. We will keep this in mind and see if we can find further opportunities for testing this approach with their followers on Facebook.

experiments.title: How the use of the word “you” affects email acquisition via an eBook offer on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: The use of the word “you” will increase conversion because it is addressing and engaging the reader specifically.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/23/2021
