As we were launching a new eBook offer for Prison Fellowship International, the original language for the funnel copy included a lot of reader centered language focused on the outcome for the reader and the purpose for the reader, which often included the use of the word “you.” As that was not typically the communication style for PFI, they preferred to use more general language about how the eBook benefits people in general. To ensure that this did not affect the outcome, we wanted to test this in the Facebook marketplace.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was that the funnel copy focused more on the outcome for the reader did not affect the email conversion rate dramatically enough to continue using it or continue testing. However, there were two other key metrics that we needed to examine as well. First, the click through rate was 8% lower on the reader centered language. And that was a key metric for this funnel, because the cost per click was high, making the funnel more difficult to scale. Second, the ultimate goal was to acquire new donors. And the donor conversion for the more general language brought in two times more donors. For these two key reasons, we discontinued the test before reaching validation to place spend on the more efficient funnel.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn’t do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a “turn off” for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer’s audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: How a social proof slider showing who just signed a petition impacted petition signature rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing who just signed a petition in a notification window to petition page viewers increase petition signature rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2020 - 09/20/2020
As a part of Canadian Taxpayer’s efforts to increase petition signature rates, we decided to experiment with using social proof to create momentum for those considering signing the petition. We decided to show petition page viewers (who had not yet signed the petition) a treatment that displayed a notification in the bottom/left side of their screen showing the first name & province location of the person that just recently signed the petition.
If others also signed the petition while they stayed on the page, new slide-out notifications with the next signer’s information would display to the person on the page, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for nearly a month and collecting 12,000+ total signatures, we could discern no noticeable difference between the two treatments.

What does this mean? It means that the notification window doesn't do enough to create momentum to improve petition signature rates.

Our overall team did acknowledge that perhaps showing personal details was a "turn off" for some, as much as it might have pushed more to sign, as privacy is really important to some folks in the Canadian Taxpayer's audience.

This theory is testable, so further experimentation with address data security and privacy is a follow-up experiment we are looking to run next.

experiments.title: Will more text on a Facebook video ad lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will more copy lead to more clicks to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/01/2020
KUOW Public Radio is Seattle’s NPR news station. Their independent, nonprofit newsroom produces award-winning stories, podcasts and events. They were running a campaign and wanted to focus on securing more monthly gifts and hoped a Facebook ad could drive more people to click and go on to make a donation. They’ve seen other Facebook advertising experiments where longer copy has led to increased donations and decided to test it for themselves. In each design, there is a video that was clickable but the main call to action and hope for the ad was to get people to click Donate Now and then visit the donation page where they could continue on to complete a recurring donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Neither the control or the treatment led to any donations and while the treatment had a slight increase in clicks, it was not significant. When looking at total unique clicks, the shorter ad (control) showed a statistically significant increase but the bulk of those were video views so, if the goal was to get more people to watch a video, it appears shorter copy is more effective. It should also be noted that the difference between the control and treatment was perhaps not great enough to prove the hypothesis. Also, taking a donor from Facebook ad to a recurring gift can be challenging.
So moving forward some ideas that require more testing and exploration are:
Shorter ads with videos where the goal is video views or just brand awarenessClearly longer ads where the goal is donations or clicks to a donation pageA short vs. long test with just an image and not a video that can be clickedDonation focused ads asking for a smaller one-time donation as opposed to a recurring gift

experiments.title: How video ads on Facebook affect webinar registration
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head video ad lead to greater webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2020 - 03/12/2020
In a previous experiment, we saw indications that a “talking head” style Facebook ad video was performing better when it came to getting people to sign up for an upcoming webinar. But whenever we’re testing something brand new like this, it’s always a good idea to re-test and see if it holds up over time.
This can help fight against the novelty affect where we see an uptick for a time purely because something is new and different.
So for the control, we used the static title image for the webinar. And for the treatment, we used a video of me (Nathan) explaining what folks would learn in the webinar. All the rest of the ad was the same – copy, headline, cta, landing page, etc.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw mixed results from audience segment to audience segment, but no individual audiences saw a significant difference in performance when looking purely at registrations. Overall, an 11.6% increase was noted, but at an extremely low level of confidence.
But looking at cost per registrations paints a slightly different picture. Overall, the static image ad acquired webinar registrants for $7.58. But the video ad acquired registrants for just $4.70. That’s a 37% decrease in cost.
Now, a couple audience segments flipped the other direction in terms of cost and those will be noted for the future. But all in all, this video ad strategy seems to be performing better from a cost perspective, even if the conversion rates are the same as the static image.

experiments.title: How a talking head video in a Facebook ad affects webinar registrations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a talking head style facebook ad lead to more webinar registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2020 - 01/20/2020
I see video-based Facebook ads all the time for different courses and trainings, and they always drive me crazy. But if they’re being used all the time, I wondered if they might just be more effective than static images. In this experiment, we tested using a static webinar title image (which has been our typical ad design for many months) against a “talking head” style video.
The video is of me in our webinar studio giving a high-level description of the webinar and what viewers can expect. It contained subtitles so that it can easily be understood with the sound off.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at results across all audiences, we saw a slight lift in conversion rate. But it did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence. However, we did find that the video ads delivered results that were 34% cheaper.

We will continue to test this in other contexts to see if the results hold up or perform better for specific audiences.

experiments.title: How adding Facebook ads to lift awareness of an upcoming direct mail letter impacted letter response rates
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding Facebook ads to promote direct mail letters impact direct mail responsiveness or giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2019 - 04/06/2019
Heritage Action wanted to experiment with whether or not adding Facebook Ads to half of the direct mail recipients in March would increase their letter response rates. Because of this, we built letter awareness ads that ran from March 1-March 16th, then switched to “urgency” ads which created an “artificial deadline” for letter recipients to “respond before it’s too late”.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no statistically significant impact from the advertising. However, the experiment had to be called prior to the full data set being collected.

experiments.title: How Target ROAS settings in Facebook didn’t impact the email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would “Target ROAS” improve results for the offer in terms of leads generated, donors acquired and/or dollars raised?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 05/10/2019
When The Heritage Foundation launched a new ebook offer, we wanted to test the viability of increased ROI and donor conversion rate efficiency by installing a competitive “Target ROAS” campaign to split target audience traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 19.8% level of confidence, we were only able to notice a 1.6% increase in email registration rate.
Although the campaign served only about 1/3 the number of impressions and percentage-wise was about even with the “Lowest Cost” campaign type, we saw costs continue to scale with donation results flatline. For that reason, we decided to turn off this treatment to the traffic split and focus on creating the volume we needed at this time.
We may reconsider adding a “Target ROAS” element to this campaign in the future, but for now it appears that the best approach is to let the algorithm create results from the “lowest cost” setting.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad image can affect conversion for a free online course offer [Lookalikes]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an image that more clearly conveys the title of the course result in greater registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2017 - 10/23/2017
When running a Facebook ad campaign promoting our online course on Facebook advertising, we wondered if we could increase registrations by adding clarity to the Facebook ad image. We added a couple additional elements to the ad image that clearly showed the idea of “Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors.”
The results below are from the Lookalikes audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was ended early due to a large jump in cost-per-registration from the control. Although it did not reach full validity, the experiment was leaning towards the treatment as the winner. This indicates that the updated image brought additional clarity to the value proposition – something that helped this lookalikes audience understand the its relevancy.

experiments.title: How Bullet Points in a Facebook Ad Affect the Clarity of the Value Proposition [Nonprofit News Sources]
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the use of bullet points in a Facebook ad make the value of the course clearer, and lead to more course registrations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/02/2017 - 10/23/2017
When launching acquisition ads for our new online course on Turning Facebook Likes Into Donors, we wondered if we there was a way to more clearly communicate all the various learnings that someone would get out of the course.
Rather than writing a few paragraphs as we had been, we tested using bullet points within the Facebook ad to clearly point out what we believed to be some of the most valuable aspects of the course.
We launched this test across each target audience. The results below are from the ad set targeting an aggregate audience including follower of the Nonprofit Times and Chronicle of Philanthropy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test did not reach validity based on conversion. The control ad was turned off due to a significantly higher cost-per-registration in the treatment.
Neither the control or treatment was appearing to approach validity, and so it appears that adding bullets into the copy had no significant effect. Although, a low statistical power implies that we may not have had a large enough sample size to detect an effect if there indeed was one to be detected.
In any case, it is possible that the audience by nature is more prone to reading with closer scrutiny given that they are followers of nonprofit industry news sources. If this is the case, they would have reached the same understanding of the value propositon, regardless of whether or not they saw the control or the treatment.

experiments.title: How running Facebook ads to Membership Card campaign targeting impacted donor conversion rates from non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a third channel in Facebook advertising (alongside email and direct mail channels) improve response rates or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 03/31/2019
We wanted to continue experimentation with running Facebook ads alongside focused campaign messaging used this time to promote their Membership Card campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 63.2% level of confidence, we were unable to validate this experiment.
Directionally, we did note a -7.8% decrease in non-member donor conversion rate.
Overall revenue decreased by -11.9% within this treatment, as well.
Our hypothesis is that the non-member segment splits may have more to do with the likelihood that a donor will be reactivated with the third channel of outreach (online advertising).
Note: Offline gifts were not included as a part of this assessment. This included online transactions only, and was run through the first half of the final day of the campaign. Additional updates will be forthcoming.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s lapsed member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for reactivating lapsed members?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind lapsed members to renew their annual membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate for this donor segment, but directionally, we see alignment with the non-member and member segments that both validated towards increasing segment response rates when serving ads.
It’s worth noting that similarly to the non-member experiment we ran, the total cost to run these ads to lapsed members generated a 4.2 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the “with ads” segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an amount equal to slightly more than 3x the spend in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Further experimentation is required with future campaigns for the lapsed segment, but directionally these results align with active and non-member segments seeing an increase in response rates with ROI free and clear of the incremental expenses associated with adding the 3rd messaging channel of Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How budget strategy in Facebook affects instant donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool create more opportunities to get instant donors than setting the budget at the ad set level?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2019 - 02/11/2019
Recent experiments had resulted in a significant improvement in Buckner’s active acquisition campaign known as “7 Scriptures.” The greatest lift was the result of technical optimization of the Facebook ads. With this in mind, we wanted to take this one step further and continue our testing of the various advertising techniques available to us through Facebook.
One of the ways ads are shown is based upon the method of bidding. Historically, we had set daily spending limits for each audience which equally spread the spend across each one. We had the hypothesis that if we allowed Facebook’s algorithm to determine the allocation of spend, we may see better results. To test this, we set the budget at the campaign level, using Facebook’s Budget Optimization tool instead of the ad set level (setting a daily limit per ad set). All ads and targeting remained the same.
At the time of the test, each campaign was spending the same amount per day ($83 and change).
experiments.keyLearnings: This test was not statistically valid, but the campaign that had daily limits set for each ad set did slightly increase instant donor conversion in the time span of the test. After further discussion, we may believe that setting daily limits for the Budget Optimization was not the best route since it still limited Facebook’s algorithm to daily amounts. Instead, we are going to next test setting a campaign spending limit that allows for the media to be spent whenever the algorithm determines it is best. This would potentially allow for increased spending on days that show increased conversion opportunity.

experiments.title: Impact of using a baby image in a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Do images of babies perform better than images of pregnant women.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2018
We have an MLD pledge we use to introduce people to MLD and ask them to become volunteers. We wanted to increase the number of volunteers that sign-up. We knew that baby images were performing well in other tests, so we decided to try it in this Facebook ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: The image of the baby performed significantly better than the image of the pregnant woman. Next, we will test an image of a fetus against this baby image to see if it performs better.

experiments.title: How using a video in a Facebook ad affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a video of Mark promoting the course convert more people than a graphic that looks similar to the course?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/15/2018
Heartlight was promoting their new free online course, Bridging the Gap With Your Teen, on Facebook. They originally launched ads with a graphic that matched the look and feel of the course, but wanted to test a video of Mark promoting the course to see if it increased conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although this test didn’t validate, we saw a 12% directional lift with the video creative. In addition to testing these two creatives, we also tested 3 other stock images and a longer version of the video, which all received a similar or lower conversion rate than these two options. Moving forward, we’ll be testing other elements of the ads to see if we can decrease cost and increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a key insight in a Facebook ad affects eBook downloads
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad focused on a key insight lead to greater eBook downloads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2018 - 08/15/2018
In this experiment, we wondered which ad approach would be most effective for the “Nonprofit News Sources” audience when promoting a recently published study about fundraising teams.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, we saw a contradictory result to the same experiment conducted with the Nonprofit News Sources audience; although, this experiment did not reach validity. I have two hypothesis as to why the results are different. First, it’s possible that the makeup of the lookalike audience is more practitioners than managers and executives, and the specific insight on the ad image was more of a turnoff than it was interesting – where as the nonprofit news sources audience may skew towards people in a leadership role.
The second hypothesis is that the book cover image portrayed more practical ideas of how to “optimize your team,” which may have been more attractive to our typical audience.
Although this experiment didn’t reach validity, there was a substantial difference in cost per acquisition. The control was acquiring emails for $3.17, and the treatment for $5.45.

experiments.title: How a more direct call-to-action affects conversion from a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action increase online course registration conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/28/2018 - 04/20/2018
We ran this experiment across several different audience groups in our Facebook advertising of our new email fundraising course. We wondered if a more direct call-to-action could be effective in driving higher conversions. The control used a call-to-action of “Learn More,” while the treatment used a call-to-action of “Active Your Free Online Course.” This experiment was run on the Nextafter.com traffic Facebook audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The more direct call-to-action saw an increase of 186%, but it did not reach full statistical validity. We chose the treatment as the winner before reaching full validity since it had a 50% lower cost-per-conversion compared to the control.

experiments.title: How a Facebook ad’s inline call-to-action impacted results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action earlier in the ad increase the number of emails acquired?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2018 - 02/27/2018
While running ads for an email acquisition campaign for Alliance Defending Freedom, we wanted to see if we could increase our acquisition by adding a call to action link earlier in the Facebook ad. Normally, a FB ad would only have a button and an image that are linked but it is possible to add another link in the text above if it matches the same location that the other areas go to.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no difference between the two treatments when it comes to the number of emails acquired. We also analyzed the clicks and while the ad with an inline call to action did get a few more visitors, they did not convert on the page. This would indicate that making the call to action available earlier in the process only drove visitors without the proper motivation.

experiments.title: How a Facebook headline affects direct-ask donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the headline ‘Donate now’ or ‘Become a member’ drive more donor conversions on a Facebook ad?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 12/12/2017
The Heritage Foundation noticed an opportunity on Facebook to retarget visitors who viewed their online donation pages. They created to direct ask Facebook ads that invited anyone who visited the page but did not complete the form to return and support the Heritage Foundation. In the first ad, they simply asked all visitors (including members who visited the page) to ‘Donate now.’ In the second ad, the asked all non-member visitors to join the Heritage Foundation by ‘Becoming a member today.’ They launched a test to find out which approach would be more effective at converting donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: They found that that the results were inconclusive. Due to a low overall donor conversion rate, The Heritage Foundation decided to pause this campaign and assess the results. The first ad did receive 1 donation, but this was not enough to validate this approach over the other. Using the urgency of the calendar year, They decided to launch and run a similar test that is directly tied to their CYE theme.

experiments.title: How length of copy affects conversion rate in a Facebook ad
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does longer, more in-depth copy about an offer in a Facebook ad increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2017 - 06/07/2017
The Missionary Oblates were running a campaign promoting their new email acquisition offer, a Daily Prayer via email. They wanted to test whether a more in-depth value proposition in the ad copy converted more people than shorter, more concise ad copy. All other elements of the ad remained the same.
